$$\theta_1 = \theta_0 - v_1 = 4 - 0.8 = 3.2$$

**Result:** $\theta_1 = 3.2$, $v_1 = 0.8$ **(1 Mark)**

**Verification using Vanilla Momentum (for comparison):** With Vanilla Momentum, $g_1^{\text{VM}} = 2 \cdot 4 = 8$ (same here since $v_0 = 0$), giving $v_1^{\text{VM}} = 0.8$, $\theta_1^{\text{VM}} = 3.2$. The difference appears in subsequent iterations due to the look-ahead shift. **(1 Mark)**

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]

**Q: Derive the Nesterov Accelerated Gradient update rule starting from Vanilla Momentum. Discuss the geometric interpretation of the look-ahead mechanism and explain why it leads to a faster convergence rate of $\mathcal{O}(1/k^2)$ for convex $L$-smooth functions.** (14 Marks)

#### Part (a): Derivation from Vanilla Momentum [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

The Vanilla Momentum update is: **(1 Mark)**
$$v_t = \gamma v_{t-1} + \eta \nabla J(\theta_{t-1})$$
$$\theta_t = \theta_{t-1} - v_t$$

Nesterov's modification replaces the gradient evaluation point $\theta_{t-1}$ with the *look-ahead* point $\theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1}$: **(2 Marks)**
$$v_t = \gamma v_{t-1} + \eta \nabla J(\theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1})$$
$$\theta_t = \theta_{t-1} - v_t$$

The effective parameter step is: **(1 Mark)**
$$\theta_t = \theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1} - \eta \nabla J(\theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1})$$

**Geometric Interpretation:** The look-ahead mechanism can be visualized as follows: **(3 Marks)**

- The optimizer first **glides forward** by the momentum term $-\gamma v_{t-1}$ (inertia-driven step).
- At the new *look-ahead* position, it **senses the slope** of the loss landscape.
- It then applies a **corrective gradient step** $-\eta \nabla J(\cdot)$ from the look-ahead position.
- This is equivalent to a two-step "probe and correct" procedure: the optimizer explores ahead, evaluates, and then commits the step.

In contrast, Vanilla Momentum **evaluates first, then moves**, leading to a "reactive" behavior that often overshoots in narrow valleys. Nesterov's "**proactive**" evaluation dampens oscillations and prevents overshooting.

#### Part (b): Convergence Rate Discussion [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

For a convex, $L$-smooth function $J(\theta)$, the suboptimality gap after $k$ iterations is:

- **Standard GD:** $J(\theta_k) - J(\theta^*) \leq \dfrac{L \left\| \theta_0 - \theta^* \right\|^2}{2k} = \mathcal{O}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{k}\right)$ **(2 Marks)**

- **Nesterov GD:** $J(\theta_k) - J(\theta^*) \leq \dfrac{2L \left\| \theta_0 - \theta^* \right\|^2}{(k+1)^2} = \mathcal{O}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{k^2}\right)$ **(2 Marks)**

**Why the $\mathcal{O}(1/k^2)$ rate?** **(3 Marks)**

The Nesterov sequence uses a **time-varying momentum schedule** $\beta_t = \dfrac{t-1}{t+2}$ (increasing with $t$). This causes:
- **Early iterations**: Low momentum → aggressive gradient following.
- **Later iterations**: High momentum → smooth averaging of past gradients.

This adaptive inertia essentially **"remembers" the optimal trajectory shape** that minimizes regret, achieving the optimal first-order convergence bound. By the **Nemirovski-Yudin lower bound (1983)**, no first-order black-box method can beat $\mathcal{O}(1/k^2)$ on convex $L$-smooth problems, so Nesterov's rate is **provably optimal**.

**Total: 7 + 7 = 14 Marks**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ Where Students Typically Lose Marks (Read Carefully!):**

1. **Confusing Vanilla Momentum with Nesterov Momentum:** The single most common error. The **gradient evaluation point is the only mathematical difference**. Vanilla uses $\nabla J(\theta_{t-1})$; Nesterov uses $\nabla J(\theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1})$. Examiners explicitly look for this distinction. *If you write the wrong gradient point, you lose 3-4 marks immediately.*

2. **Missing the Look-Ahead Computation Step:** Many students jump directly to the velocity update without explicitly computing $\theta_{\text{lookahead}}$. The look-ahead is the *defining feature* of Nesterov — always show it.

3. **Wrong Convergence Rate:** Some students write $\mathcal{O}(1/k)$ for Nesterov (which is GD's rate) or $\mathcal{O}(1/k^2)$ for Vanilla Momentum (which is Nesterov's rate). *Nesterov = $\mathcal{O}(1/k^2)$, Vanilla GD/Momentum = $\mathcal{O}(1/k)$.*

4. **Initialization Errors:** Forgetting $v_0 = 0$ as the initial velocity condition. Examiners may deduct a mark if this is not stated.

5. **Numerical Computation Mistakes:** In numerical problems, students often mix up the order of operations (e.g., computing gradient before look-ahead). Always follow the strict 4-step order: **Look-ahead → Gradient → Velocity → Parameter**.

6. **Failing to Explain the "Why":** Merely writing equations without explaining the *intuition* (foresight, braking, oscillation damping) loses you the application-level marks. KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes **understanding over rote memorization**.

7. **No Comparison:** For 14-mark questions, if you do not compare Nesterov with Vanilla Momentum or Standard GD in a table or paragraph, you typically lose 2-3 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **📌 HIGH-DENSITY REVISION SUMMARY — Memorize Before Exam**

- ✅ **Nesterov Accelerated GD** = Momentum + **look-ahead gradient evaluation**.
- ✅ **Core Trick:** Evaluate gradient at $\theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1}$, not at $\theta_{t-1}$.
- ✅ **Four-Step Iteration Order:** (1) Look-ahead position → (2) Gradient at look-ahead → (3) Velocity update → (4) Parameter update.
- ✅ **Update Equations:**
  - $v_t = \gamma v_{t-1} + \eta \nabla J(\theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1})$
  - $\theta_t = \theta_{t-1} - v_t$
- ✅ **Convergence Rate:** $\mathcal{O}(1/k^2)$ for convex, $L$-smooth functions (optimal first-order rate).
- ✅ **Vs. Vanilla Momentum:** Same per-iteration cost; **better convergence** and **less overshooting**.
- ✅ **Vs. Standard GD:** Quadratically faster iteration complexity; same gradient cost.
- ✅ **Inventor:** Yurii Nesterov (1983); part of *Accelerated Gradient Methods*.
- ✅ **Standard Hyperparameters:** $\eta = 0.01$ (or $0.001$), $\gamma = 0.9$.
- ✅ **Initialization:** $\theta_0$ (user-defined), $v_0 = \mathbf{0}$.
- ✅ **Intuition:** "Look-ahead gradient correction" = skier sensing the slope *ahead* before committing to the step.
- ✅ **Real-World Use:** SGD with Nesterov momentum is the **default optimizer in PyTorch** (`torch.optim.SGD(..., nesterov=True)`) and is heavily used in ResNet, ResNeXt, and Mask R-CNN training.
- ✅ **Foundation for:** Nadam (Nesterov-Adam), which combines Nesterov's foresight with Adam's adaptive learning rates.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Module Context:** Part of *Module 2: Optimization Techniques in Deep Learning* (alongside SGD, Momentum, Adam, RMSProp).
- ✅ **Exam Frequency:** High — expect 3-mark short questions and/or 14-mark long questions almost every semester.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Batch
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET753 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Batch

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Deep Learning, the term **Batch** has two dominant, interlinked meanings that are central to Module 2 of the PEEET753 syllabus:

**(A) Batch (in Optimization):** A **batch** is a subset of the entire training dataset that is used to compute the gradient of the loss function during a single forward and backward pass of a neural network. Instead of using the whole dataset (Batch Gradient Descent) or a single sample (Stochastic Gradient Descent), the training set is partitioned into smaller **mini-batches** of size $m$, where $1 < m < N$ (with $N$ being the total number of training samples).

**(B) Batch Normalization (BN):** A regularization and acceleration technique introduced by Ioffe & Szegedy (2015) where, for each **mini-batch**, the activations of a neural network layer are normalized to have zero mean and unit variance, followed by a learnable scale ($\gamma$) and shift ($\beta$) operation. The batch here refers to the mini-batch used to compute the running mean and variance statistics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under the 2024 NEP-aligned scheme, the concept of the **batch** is fundamental because it bridges the gap between classical numerical optimization (full-batch GD) and modern deep learning optimization (mini-batch SGD, Adam, RMSProp). A deep understanding of batch mechanics, batch size selection, and batch normalization is mandatory for the End Semester Evaluation (ESE).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Restaurant Kitchen Analogy:** Imagine you are a head chef training 1000 kitchen assistants to prepare a complex dish (the neural network learning a task).
> - **Full-Batch Gradient Descent:** You wait until ALL 1000 assistants finish cooking a test dish, taste every single one, average the feedback, and only then give one set of corrections. Slow, accurate, but your kitchen is paralyzed.
> - **Stochastic Gradient Descent (Batch size = 1):** You taste and correct each assistant one by one. Fast, but the feedback is extremely noisy.
> - **Mini-Batch Gradient Descent:** You group the assistants into batches of 64. You taste 64 dishes, average the feedback for that batch, and correct them together. This is the **sweet spot** — fast, stable, and computationally efficient on GPUs.
> - **Batch Normalization:** Before tasting, you also standardize the seasoning of every batch to a consistent baseline (zero mean, unit variance) so that your feedback (gradient) is more reliable and your taste buds (the network) don't get overwhelmed by extreme flavors.

**Key Standard Metric:** The **Batch Size** $m$ is always a positive integer, with typical values being powers of 2 (e.g., **32, 64, 128, 256**) due to GPU memory alignment efficiency.

---

## 1.3 Categories of Gradient Descent Based on Batch

| Mode | Batch Size ($m$) | Update Frequency | Noise in Gradient |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Batch GD** | $m = N$ (entire dataset) | Once per epoch | Very Low |
| **Mini-Batch GD** | $1 < m < N$ | Multiple per epoch | Moderate |
| **Stochastic GD** | $m = 1$ | $N$ times per epoch | Very High |

**Where:**
- $N$ = Total number of training samples
- $m$ = Size of one batch
- **One Epoch** = $\lceil N/m \rceil$ iterations
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mini-Batch Gradient Descent — Operational Breakdown

The parameter update rule for a neural network with parameters $\theta$ at iteration $t$ using a mini-batch of size $m$ is:

1. **Sample a mini-batch** of $m$ training examples from the full training set.
2. **Forward Pass:** Compute the predicted output $\hat{y}^{(i)}$ for each example $i$ in the batch.
3. **Compute the Loss:** Calculate the empirical loss $J(\theta)$ averaged over the batch:
$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \mathcal{L}(\hat{y}^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$$
4. **Backward Pass:** Compute the gradient $\nabla_\theta J(\theta)$ via backpropagation on the batch.
5. **Update Parameters:** Apply the optimizer's update rule (e.g., for SGD with learning rate $\eta$):
$$\theta := \theta - \eta \cdot \nabla_\theta J(\theta)$$
6. **Repeat** for all $\lceil N/m \rceil$ mini-batches to complete one epoch.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 'Why':** Mini-batches are used because modern GPUs achieve maximum throughput when processing data in parallel chunks. The gradient computed over a batch is an **unbiased estimator** of the true gradient over the full dataset, but with much lower variance than a single sample.

## 2.2 Batch Normalization (BN) — Deep Theoretical Analysis

Batch Normalization operates on the pre-activation $z$ of a layer (or sometimes the input $a^{[l-1]}$) and forces a stable distribution at every training step.

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic:

Given a mini-batch $\mathcal{B} = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_m\}$ of size $m$ for a particular layer:

1. **Compute Mini-Batch Mean:**
$$\mu_{\mathcal{B}} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} x_i$$
2. **Compute Mini-Batch Variance:**
$$\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2 = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}})^2$$
3. **Normalize (Zero Mean, Unit Variance):**
$$\hat{x}_i = \frac{x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}}}{\sqrt{\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2 + \epsilon}}$$
Where $\epsilon$ is a small constant (typically **$10^{-5}$**) added for numerical stability to prevent division by zero.
4. **Scale and Shift (Learnable Affine Transformation):**
$$y_i = \gamma \hat{x}_i + \beta$$
Where $\gamma$ and $\beta$ are learnable parameters of the network, initialized typically as $\gamma = 1$ and $\beta = 0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 'Why' behind Step 4:** If the network *needs* the original non-normalized distribution to represent a function (e.g., sigmoid saturations on purpose), $\gamma$ and $\beta$ can undo the normalization. This makes BN an **identity function** in the worst case, ensuring it never hurts performance.

### Inference Phase:

During testing/inference, the true dataset mean and variance are unknown per batch. Instead, we use **running averages** collected during training:
$$\mu_{\text{running}} = \alpha \cdot \mu_{\text{running}} + (1 - \alpha) \cdot \mu_{\mathcal{B}}$$
$$\sigma_{\text{running}}^2 = \alpha \cdot \sigma_{\text{running}}^2 + (1 - \alpha) \cdot \sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2$$
Where $\alpha$ is the momentum (typically **0.9** or **0.99**).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Formula / Meaning | Purpose / Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Batch Size | $m \in \{32, 64, 128, 256\}$ | Number of samples per gradient update |
| Iterations per Epoch | $\lceil N / m \rceil$ | Number of updates per full data pass |
| Mini-Batch Mean | $\mu_{\mathcal{B}} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} x_i$ | Average activation over batch |
| Mini-Batch Variance | $\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2 = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}})^2$ | Spread of activations over batch |
| Normalized Value | $\hat{x}_i = \frac{x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}}}{\sqrt{\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2 + \epsilon}}$ | Standardized activation |
| BN Output | $y_i = \gamma \hat{x}_i + \beta$ | Scaled and shifted activation |
| Stability Constant | $\epsilon = 10^{-5}$ | Prevents division by zero |
| Running Mean | $\mu_{\text{run}} \leftarrow \alpha \mu_{\text{run}} + (1-\alpha)\mu_{\mathcal{B}}$ | Used during inference |
| Running Variance | $\sigma_{\text{run}}^2 \leftarrow \alpha \sigma_{\text{run}}^2 + (1-\alpha)\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2$ | Used during inference |
| SGD Update | $\theta := \theta - \eta \nabla_\theta J(\theta)$ | Parameter optimization step |
| Momentum Update | $v := \beta_m v + (1-\beta_m)\nabla_\theta J(\theta); \ \theta := \theta - \eta v$ | Accelerated gradient descent |

**Engineering Utility:** Batch Normalization enables the use of **higher learning rates**, makes the network less sensitive to weight initialization, provides mild regularization (reducing the need for dropout), and is critical for training very deep networks like ResNet-152, Transformers, and GANs. It is a foundational block in virtually every state-of-the-art computer vision and NLP system deployed in industry.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Backward Pass for Batch Normalization

During backpropagation, we need the gradients of the loss $J$ with respect to the BN parameters $\gamma$, $\beta$, and the input $x_i$. Let the loss gradient w.r.t. the BN output $y_i$ be $dy_i = \frac{\partial J}{\partial y_i}$.

### Step A: Gradient with respect to $\gamma$ and $\beta$

These are simple summations because $y_i = \gamma \hat{x}_i + \beta$.

The derivative of $J$ w.r.t. $\gamma$ is the sum of the products of the upstream gradient and $\hat{x}_i$:

$$\begin{aligned}
d\gamma &= \frac{\partial J}{\partial \gamma} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \frac{\partial J}{\partial y_i} \cdot \frac{\partial y_i}{\partial \gamma} \\
d\gamma &= \sum_{i=1}^{m} dy_i \cdot \hat{x}_i
\end{aligned}$$

Similarly, the derivative of $J$ w.r.t. $\beta$ is just the sum of upstream gradients:

$$\begin{aligned}
d\beta &= \frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \frac{\partial J}{\partial y_i} \cdot \frac{\partial y_i}{\partial \beta} \\
d\beta &= \sum_{i=1}^{m} dy_i
\end{aligned}$$

### Step B: Gradient with respect to the normalized $\hat{x}_i$

This is direct from $y_i = \gamma \hat{x}_i + \beta$:

$$\begin{aligned}
d\hat{x}_i &= \frac{\partial J}{\partial \hat{x}_i} = \frac{\partial J}{\partial y_i} \cdot \frac{\partial y_i}{\partial \hat{x}_i} \\
d\hat{x}_i &= dy_i \cdot \gamma
\end{aligned}$$

### Step C: Gradient with respect to variance $\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2$

Recall $\hat{x}_i = \frac{x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}}}{\sqrt{\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2 + \epsilon}}$. Let $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2 + \epsilon}$. Then $\hat{x}_i = (x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}}) / \sigma$.

Using the chain rule with the product rule:

$$\begin{aligned}
d\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2 &= \sum_{i=1}^{m} \frac{\partial J}{\partial \hat{x}_i} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{x}_i}{\partial \sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2} \\
\frac{\partial \hat{x}_i}{\partial \sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2} &= (x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}}) \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{2}\right) (\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2 + \epsilon)^{-3/2} \\
d\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2 &= \sum_{i=1}^{m} d\hat{x}_i \cdot (x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}}) \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{2}\right) (\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2 + \epsilon)^{-3/2} \\
d\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2 &= -\frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{m} d\hat{x}_i \cdot (x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}}) \cdot (\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2 + \epsilon)^{-3/2}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step D: Gradient with respect to mean $\mu_{\mathcal{B}}$

The mean appears in two places: explicitly in the numerator of $\hat{x}_i$ and implicitly in the variance term.

$$\begin{aligned}
d\mu_{\mathcal{B}} &= \sum_{i=1}^{m} \frac{\partial J}{\partial \hat{x}_i} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{x}_i}{\partial \mu_{\mathcal{B}}} + \frac{\partial J}{\partial \sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2} \cdot \frac{\partial \sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2}{\partial \mu_{\mathcal{B}}}
\end{aligned}$$

Computing each piece:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \hat{x}_i}{\partial \mu_{\mathcal{B}}} &= -\frac{1}{\sigma} = -(\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2 + \epsilon)^{-1/2} \\
\frac{\partial \sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2}{\partial \mu_{\mathcal{B}}} &= \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} -2(x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}}) = 0
\end{aligned}$$

The implicit term vanishes because the mean minimizes the variance! Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
d\mu_{\mathcal{B}} &= \sum_{i=1}^{m} d\hat{x}_i \cdot \left( -(\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2 + \epsilon)^{-1/2} \right) + d\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2 \cdot 0 \\
d\mu_{\mathcal{B}} &= -\sum_{i=1}^{m} d\hat{x}_i \cdot (\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2 + \epsilon)^{-1/2}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step E: Gradient with respect to the input $x_i$

The input $x_i$ affects the loss through three paths: directly via $\hat{x}_i$, through the mean $\mu_{\mathcal{B}}$, and through the variance $\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2$.

$$\begin{aligned}
dx_i &= d\hat{x}_i \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{x}_i}{\partial x_i} + d\mu_{\mathcal{B}} \cdot \frac{\partial \mu_{\mathcal{B}}}{\partial x_i} + d\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2 \cdot \frac{\partial \sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2}{\partial x_i}
\end{aligned}$$

Computing each partial derivative:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \hat{x}_i}{\partial x_i} &= \frac{1}{\sigma} = (\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2 + \epsilon)^{-1/2} \\
\frac{\partial \mu_{\mathcal{B}}}{\partial x_i} &= \frac{1}{m} \\
\frac{\partial \sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2}{\partial x_i} &= \frac{2(x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}})}{m}
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting everything:

$$\begin{aligned}
dx_i &= d\hat{x}_i \cdot (\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2 + \epsilon)^{-1/2} + d\mu_{\mathcal{B}} \cdot \frac{1}{m} + d\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2 \cdot \frac{2(x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}})}{m} \\
dx_i &= \frac{d\hat{x}_i}{\sqrt{\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2 + \epsilon}} + \frac{d\mu_{\mathcal{B}}}{m} + \frac{2 d\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2 (x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}})}{m}
\end{aligned}$$

This final $dx_i$ is then passed backward to the previous layer.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Trade-offs of Batch Size

| Batch Size | Training Speed (per epoch) | Memory Usage | Gradient Noise | Generalization Gap | Convergence Stability |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Small (e.g., 16)** | Slow | Low | High | Smaller (better) | Noisy / Oscillating |
| **Medium (e.g., 64)** | Balanced | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate | Stable |
| **Large (e.g., 512)** | Fast (GPU saturated) | High | Low | Larger (worse) | Very Smooth |
| **Full Batch (N)** | Slowest | Very High | None | Largest | Smoothest |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Larger batches tend to converge to **sharp minimizers** of the loss landscape (which generalize worse on test data), while smaller batches converge to **flat minimizers** (which generalize better). This is the famous "large batch training generalization gap" studied by Keskar et al. (2017).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Data Flow: How a Mini-Batch Moves Through a Training Step

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Full Training Set N Samples] --> B[Shuffle and Partition]
    B --> C[Mini-Batch 1 of size m]
    B --> D[Mini-Batch 2 of size m]
    B --> E[Mini-Batch 3 of size m]
    B --> F[Mini-Batch K of size m]
    C --> G[Forward Pass on Batch 1]
    G --> H[Compute Loss J theta]
    H --> I[Backward Pass Backprop]
    I --> J[Update Parameters theta]
    J --> K{All Batches Done}
    K -- No --> C
    K -- Yes --> L[End of 1 Epoch]
    L --> M[Shuffle Again and Repeat]
```

## 4.2 Architecture: Batch Normalization Layer Placement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X1[Input x_i] --> N1[Compute Batch Mean mu_B]
    X1 --> N2[Compute Batch Variance sigma_B squared]
    N1 --> N3[Subtract Mean from x_i]
    N2 --> N4[Add epsilon for stability]
    N3 --> N5[Divide by sqrt of variance]
    N4 --> N5
    N5 --> N6[Normalized x_hat_i]
    N6 --> N7[Scale by gamma]
    N7 --> N8[Shift by beta]
    N8 --> N9[Output y_i to Next Layer]
    G[Learnable gamma] --> N7
    B[Learnable beta] --> N8
```

## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram: Gradient Descent Variants

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph SGD[Batch Size equals 1]
        S1[Sample 1] --> S2[Compute Gradient] --> S3[Update Weights]
    end
    subgraph MBGD[Batch Size equals m Mini-Batch]
        M1[Batch of m Samples] --> M2[Average Loss] --> M3[Compute Gradient] --> M4[Update Weights]
    end
    subgraph BGD[Batch Size equals N Full Batch]
        B1[All N Samples] --> B2[Sum Loss] --> B3[Compute Gradient] --> B4[Update Weights Once per Epoch]
    end
```

## 4.4 Training vs. Inference Pipeline for Batch Normalization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T[Training Phase] --> T1[Compute mu_B and sigma_B squared from current batch]
    T1 --> T2[Normalize using batch statistics]
    T2 --> T3[Apply learnable gamma and beta]
    T3 --> T4[Update running averages for inference]
    I[Inference Phase] --> I1[Use saved running mean and running variance]
    I1 --> I2[Normalize test input]
    I2 --> I3[Apply same gamma and beta]
    I3 --> O[Final Output]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model Question]**
**Q: What is a mini-batch in the context of deep learning? How does mini-batch gradient descent differ from stochastic and batch gradient descent?**

**Model Answer:**
A **mini-batch** is a small subset of the training dataset containing $m$ samples (where $1 < m < N$) that is used to compute one estimate of the gradient of the loss function during a single update step of the model's parameters.

- **Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD):** Uses a batch size of $m = 1$. Updates weights after every single training example, leading to very noisy gradient updates and high variance.
- **Batch Gradient Descent:** Uses a batch size of $m = N$ (the entire dataset). Updates weights only once per epoch, leading to slow iterations and high memory consumption, but a smooth and accurate gradient.
- **Mini-Batch Gradient Descent:** Uses an intermediate batch size $m$ (e.g., 32, 64, 128). Combines the efficiency of batch GD with the speed of SGD, and leverages GPU parallelism. It is the de-facto standard for training deep neural networks.

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**Q: State the forward pass equations for Batch Normalization. Why is the $\epsilon$ constant added during normalization?**

**Model Answer:**
The forward pass of Batch Normalization for a mini-batch $\mathcal{B}$ is given by:

1. $\mu_{\mathcal{B}} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} x_i$
2. $\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2 = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}})^2$
3. $\hat{x}_i = \frac{x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}}}{\sqrt{\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2 + \epsilon}}$
4. $y_i = \gamma \hat{x}_i + \beta$

**Why $\epsilon$ is added:** The small constant $\epsilon$ (typically $10^{-5}$) is added inside the square root purely for **numerical stability**. If the batch variance $\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2$ happens to be exactly zero (all activations are identical), the denominator becomes zero, causing division-by-zero errors. The $\epsilon$ guarantees the denominator is always strictly positive.

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1
**[KTU University Exam - KTU 2024 Scheme Model Paper]**
**Q: (a) [7 Marks]** Explain the complete algorithm of Mini-Batch Gradient Descent with a neat flowchart. Discuss the effect of batch size on training stability, memory, and generalization.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A neural network is trained on $N = 50,000$ images with a batch size of 64. Calculate (i) the number of iterations per epoch, and (ii) the number of weight updates after 5 epochs.

### Model Solution for Q-A:

**Part (a) — Algorithm and Batch Size Effects:**

The Mini-Batch Gradient Descent algorithm proceeds as follows:

1. **Initialize** the network parameters $\theta$ (weights and biases) and the learning rate $\eta$.
2. **Shuffle** the entire training dataset of $N$ samples randomly.
3. **Partition** the shuffled dataset into $K = \lceil N/m \rceil$ mini-batches of size $m$.
4. **For each epoch**, repeat steps 5 to 8 for $k = 1$ to $K$:
    5. **Forward Pass:** Feed the $k$-th mini-batch through the network to get predictions $\hat{y}^{(i)}$ for $i = 1, \ldots, m$.
    6. **Compute Loss:** Evaluate the cost function $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \mathcal{L}(\hat{y}^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$.
    7. **Backward Pass:** Compute the gradient $\nabla_\theta J(\theta)$ via backpropagation.
    8. **Update Parameters:** $\theta := \theta - \eta \nabla_\theta J(\theta)$.
9. **Repeat** steps 2 through 8 for the desired number of epochs.

**Effect of Batch Size:**
- **Training Stability:** Small batches produce noisy gradients, causing the loss curve to oscillate. Large batches produce smooth, stable loss curves.
- **Memory Usage:** Batch size directly determines GPU VRAM consumption. Small batches fit in low-memory GPUs; large batches require high-end hardware.
- **Generalization:** Empirically, small to medium batches (32–128) generalize better because the noise helps escape sharp minimizers. Very large batches converge to sharp minimizers and often show a "generalization gap."

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $N = 50,000$ and $m = 64$.

**(i) Number of iterations per epoch:**

$$\begin{aligned}
K &= \lceil N / m \rceil = \lceil 50000 / 64 \rceil \\
50000 / 64 &= 781.25 \\
K &= 782 \text{ iterations per epoch}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Number of weight updates after 5 epochs:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Updates} &= K \times \text{Epochs} = 782 \times 5 \\
\text{Total Updates} &= 3910 \text{ weight updates}
\end{aligned}$$

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)
**[KTU University Exam - KTU 2024 Scheme Model Paper]**
**Q: (a) [7 Marks]** With neat equations, explain Batch Normalization. Discuss why Batch Normalization works and list any three advantages.

**(b) [7 Marks]** During inference, why can't we use the batch statistics directly? Explain the role of moving averages and momentum in Batch Normalization.

### Model Solution for Q-B:

**Part (a) — Batch Normalization Explanation:**

Batch Normalization (BN) is a technique used to normalize the inputs of each mini-batch to a layer in order to stabilize training. Given a mini-batch $\mathcal{B} = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_m\}$:

$$\mu_{\mathcal{B}} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} x_i$$

$$\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2 = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}})^2$$

$$\hat{x}_i = \frac{x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}}}{\sqrt{\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2 + \epsilon}}$$

$$y_i = \gamma \hat{x}_i + \beta$$

**Why Batch Normalization Works (Three Reasons):**
1. **Reduces Internal Covariate Shift:** It stabilizes the distribution of layer inputs during training, so each layer sees a more consistent input distribution, allowing deeper networks to train successfully.
2. **Allows Higher Learning Rates:** Normalized gradients are smoother, so we can use larger learning rates without divergence, dramatically speeding up training.
3. **Provides Mild Regularization:** The noise introduced by computing mean/variance per batch acts as a regularizer, slightly reducing overfitting.

**Three Advantages:**
1. Faster convergence and shorter training time.
2. Less sensitivity to weight initialization.
3. Acts as a regularizer, sometimes eliminating the need for Dropout.

**Part (b) — Inference Phase Explanation:**

During **inference (testing)**, we cannot use batch statistics because:
- Test data usually comes **one sample at a time** (e.g., a single user query), so we cannot form a meaningful mini-batch.
- We need **deterministic outputs** for the same input, but using per-batch statistics would make the output depend on the other samples in the batch.

**Solution — Moving Averages with Momentum:**

During training, after each mini-batch, the running averages of the mean and variance are updated using an exponential moving average controlled by the **momentum** hyperparameter $\alpha$ (typically 0.9 or 0.99):

$$\mu_{\text{running}} = \alpha \cdot \mu_{\text{running}} + (1 - \alpha) \cdot \mu_{\mathcal{B}}$$

$$\sigma_{\text{running}}^2 = \alpha \cdot \sigma_{\text{running}}^2 + (1 - \alpha) \cdot \sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2$$

At inference time, the saved $\mu_{\text{running}}$ and $\sigma_{\text{running}}^2$ are used in place of $\mu_{\mathcal{B}}$ and $\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2$ to compute:

$$y_{\text{test}} = \gamma \cdot \frac{x_{\text{test}} - \mu_{\text{running}}}{\sqrt{\sigma_{\text{running}}^2 + \epsilon}} + \beta$$

A **higher $\alpha$** (closer to 1) means the running averages change slowly, retaining more of the historical statistics, which is preferred for large datasets.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Batch GD with Batch Normalization:** Many students mix up "batch" as the dataset subset for gradient descent with "Batch" in Batch Normalization. Both are related, but they are distinct concepts. Clearly state the context in your answer.
> 2. **Forgetting $\epsilon$:** Writing $\frac{x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}}}{\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2}$ instead of $\frac{x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}}}{\sqrt{\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2 + \epsilon}}$ is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction. The square root is critical.
> 3. **Ignoring Inference Phase:** Students often describe BN only during training. Examiners specifically test whether you know that **moving averages are used at inference**. This is a 2–3 mark component in Part B.
> 4. **Miscalculating Iterations:** Remember that one epoch = $\lceil N/m \rceil$ iterations, NOT $N/m$ when $N$ is not divisible by $m$. Use the ceiling function correctly.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Batch = Mini-batch:** In modern deep learning, "batch" almost always means a mini-batch of size $m$ where $1 < m < N$.
- **Standard Batch Sizes:** Always powers of 2: **32, 64, 128, 256**. These align with GPU cache and memory hierarchies.
- **Iterations per Epoch:** $\lceil N/m \rceil$. Total weight updates = Iterations $\times$ Epochs.
- **Batch Normalization is a 4-step process:** Mean $\rightarrow$ Variance $\rightarrow$ Normalize $\rightarrow$ Scale & Shift ($\gamma$, $\beta$).
- **The $\epsilon$ term** is non-negotiable for numerical stability; value is $10^{-5}$.
- **$\gamma$ and $\beta$ are learnable**, not hyperparameters. They are updated via backpropagation just like weights.
- **Inference uses moving averages**, NOT the current batch statistics, because test data is processed one-at-a-time and outputs must be deterministic.
- **Momentum ($\alpha$)** for running averages is typically **0.9** (CNN) or **0.99** (Transformers/LSTMs).
- **Batch Size Trade-off:** Small batches = better generalization, more noise, less memory. Large batches = faster epochs, smoother loss, but worse generalization.
- **Vanishing/Exploding Gradients:** Batch Normalization is one of the primary defenses against these problems in deep networks, alongside proper initialization (He/Xavier) and residual connections.
- **Covariate Shift:** BN works primarily by reducing internal covariate shift — the change in the distribution of layer inputs during training.
- **Not a replacement for Dropout:** Although BN regularizes mildly, in many architectures (especially Transformers), Dropout is still used alongside BN.
- **KTU Keyword Mapping:** When you see "batch" in the question paper, always clarify in your answer whether it refers to **(i) mini-batch gradient descent** or **(ii) batch normalization** before proceeding.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mini-batch and Stochastic GD
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET753 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Mini-batch and Stochastic GD

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of training Deep Neural Networks (DNNs), **Gradient Descent (GD)** is a first-order iterative optimization algorithm used to minimize the loss function $J(\theta)$ by updating the model parameters $\theta$ in the direction opposite to the gradient of the loss. Based on the amount of data used to compute the gradient at each iteration, GD is classified into three principal variants:

1. **Batch Gradient Descent (BGD):** Computes the gradient of the loss function with respect to the parameters $\theta$ using the **entire training dataset** $m$ at every iteration.
2. **Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD):** Computes the gradient using **exactly one training example** $(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$ at each iteration.
3. **Mini-Batch Gradient Descent (MBGD):** Computes the gradient using a **small batch of $b$ training examples** (where $1 < b < m$) at each iteration. It is the de-facto standard in modern deep learning.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The term *"SGD"* in deep learning literature is almost always used to loosely refer to **Mini-Batch GD**. When the mini-batch size $b = 1$, it is the *true* Stochastic GD. The KTU examiner expects you to know this contextual nuance.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a mountaineer (the optimizer) trying to descend a foggy mountain (the loss landscape) to reach the lowest valley (the global minimum of $J(\theta)$).

- **Batch GD (BGD):** You sit at a high point, and before taking **a single step**, you send out 60,000 scouts to measure the steepness of the ground in all directions. You then take the single best step. This is extremely accurate but painfully slow per step and computationally expensive.

- **Stochastic GD (SGD):** You stand up and immediately walk downhill based on the **opinion of a single random scout** standing right next to you. You stumble and zigzag a lot, but you take thousands of tiny steps in the time BGD takes to take one. You are noisy, but you reach the valley very fast per step.

- **Mini-Batch GD:** You compromise — before each step, you listen to a **small team of, say, 64 or 256 scouts** (a batch). You get a reasonably accurate sense of the slope and make a confident, efficient step. This is the **"Goldilocks" approach** used in almost all deep learning training loops today.

### 1.3 Key Hyperparameters (Bolded Standard Metrics)

- **Mini-batch size (b):** Typically powers of 2: **32, 64, 128, 256, 512**.
- **Number of iterations:** Number of updates required for one epoch = $m / b$.
- **Number of epochs (E):** One full pass over the training set.
- **Learning rate ($\eta$):** Step size hyperparameter, typically in the range **$10^{-4}$ to $10^{-1}$**.

> [!NOTE]
> The choice of $b$ directly trades off **gradient accuracy** (low noise) with **computational efficiency** (high throughput on GPUs, which prefer parallelizable batches of size $\geq 32$).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Variants — Structured Logic Breakdown

#### (a) Batch Gradient Descent (BGD)
- **How:** For every update, we sum the loss contribution of all $m$ training samples.
- **Why:** Gives the *true* gradient of the empirical risk. The trajectory is smooth and monotonic.
- **Problem:** For modern datasets (e.g., ImageNet with $m = 1.2$ million), one update is extremely expensive. It also cannot fit in GPU memory if $m$ is huge. Finally, it cannot escape shallow local minima effectively.

#### (b) Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)
- **How:** For every update, pick one random sample $i \in \{1, \ldots, m\}$ and take a step based on the gradient of that single sample.
- **Why:** Extremely fast per step. The inherent noise actually *helps* escape shallow local minima and saddle points.
- **Problem:** The path is very noisy and oscillates heavily around the minimum. It never truly settles — it bounces around the valley.

#### (c) Mini-Batch Gradient Descent (MBGD)
- **How:** At each step $t$, sample a mini-batch $\mathcal{B}_t$ of size $b$ and compute the average gradient over this batch.
- **Why:** Provides a **balanced trade-off** between the stability of BGD and the speed of SGD. It leverages the highly parallel architecture of GPUs (matrix multiplications of shape $[b \times d]$).
- **Bonus:** Variance of the gradient estimate decreases as $O(1/b)$, so larger batches give smoother convergence.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Formula / Expression | Meaning / Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| Empirical Risk | $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m} \mathcal{L}\left(\hat{y}^{(i)}, y^{(i)}\right)$ | Total cost over the dataset. Minimized during training. |
| **BGD Update Rule** | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \nabla_\theta J(\theta_t)$ | Uses gradient of full dataset. |
| **SGD Update Rule** | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta_t \cdot \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}\left(\hat{y}^{(i)}, y^{(i)}, \theta_t\right)$ | Uses gradient of a single sample $i$. |
| **MBGD Update Rule** | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta_t \cdot \frac{1}{b}\sum_{i \in \mathcal{B}_t} \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}\left(\hat{y}^{(i)}, y^{(i)}, \theta_t\right)$ | The **core equation** for modern DL. Average gradient over batch $\mathcal{B}_t$. |
| Iterations per Epoch | $N_{\text{iter}} = \lceil m / b \rceil$ | Total parameter updates per full pass over the data. |
| **Gradient Variance (SGD)** | $\mathrm{Var}\left[\nabla \mathcal{L}_i\right] = \sigma^2$ | High noise $\rightarrow$ small effective $b$ required. |
| **Gradient Variance (MBGD)** | $\mathrm{Var}\left[\frac{1}{b}\sum \nabla \mathcal{L}_i\right] \approx \frac{\sigma^2}{b}$ | Variance shrinks as $b$ grows. |
| Learning Rate Decay | $\eta_t = \frac{\eta_0}{1 + \text{decay\_rate} \cdot t}$ | Standard annealing to settle into minima. |
| **Generalization Gap** | $\mathrm{gap} = \mathbb{E}\left[J(\theta) - J_{\text{train}}(\theta)\right]$ | Small batches (and noise) often reduce this gap. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The notation $\nabla_\theta J(\theta_t)$ in BGD requires an $O(md)$ computation (a sum over all samples). For SGD, it is $O(d)$ and for MBGD, it is $O(bd)$. This **computational complexity** is the *primary engineering reason* MBGD dominates in practice.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Computer Vision (CNNs):** MBGD with $b = 32$ or $64$ is the standard for ResNet, VGG, etc.
- **Natural Language Processing (Transformers):** MBGD with $b = 8$ to $256$ depending on sequence length.
- **Recommender Systems:** True SGD is sometimes used with streaming data (online learning).
- **Federated Learning:** Mini-batches correspond to user devices; SGD-like updates are the only feasible approach.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the MBGD Update Rule from First Principles

The general goal of empirical risk minimization is to find parameters $\theta$ that minimize:

$$ J(\theta) = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \mathcal{L}\left(f(x^{(i)}; \theta), y^{(i)}\right) $$

**Step 1:** Take the gradient of the full empirical risk with respect to $\theta$. Because $J(\theta)$ is an average (a sum divided by $m$), the gradient distributes linearly through it:

$$ \nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}\left(f(x^{(i)}; \theta), y^{(i)}\right) $$

**Step 2:** This is the **Batch Gradient Descent** gradient. For large $m$ (e.g., millions of images), computing this sum at every iteration is infeasible. The standard approximation is to replace the expectation over the full dataset with an expectation over a small, randomly drawn mini-batch $\mathcal{B}_t$ of size $b$:

$$ g_t = \frac{1}{b} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{B}_t} \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}\left(f(x^{(i)}; \theta_t), y^{(i)}\right) \approx \nabla_\theta J(\theta_t) $$

**Step 3 (Unbiasedness check):** We can prove $g_t$ is an unbiased estimator of the true gradient by taking its expectation over the random sampling of the batch:

$$ \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{B}_t}\left[g_t\right] = \frac{1}{b} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{B}_t} \mathbb{E}\left[\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}_i\right] = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m} \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}_i = \nabla_\theta J(\theta) $$

**Step 4:** Perform the standard gradient descent parameter update using the noisy estimate $g_t$ instead of the true gradient. The learning rate $\eta_t$ is often decayed over time:

$$ \theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta_t \cdot g_t $$

**Step 5 (Final canonical MBGD update rule):** Substituting the expression for $g_t$ from Step 2 into the update yields the working equation of deep learning:

$$ \theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta_t \cdot \frac{1}{b} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{B}_t} \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}\left(f(x^{(i)}; \theta_t), y^{(i)}\right) $$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: BGD vs. SGD vs. MBGD

| Feature | Batch GD | Stochastic GD | Mini-Batch GD |
|---|---|---|---|
| Samples per update ($k$) | $m$ | $1$ | $b$ (e.g., 64) |
| Update rule | $\theta - \eta \nabla J(\theta)$ | $\theta - \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}_i$ | $\theta - \eta \frac{1}{b}\sum_{i \in \mathcal{B}} \nabla \mathcal{L}_i$ |
| Gradient computation cost | $O(m d)$ per update | $O(d)$ per update | $O(b d)$ per update |
| Variance of update | $0$ (deterministic) | High (proportional to $\sigma^2$) | Reduced by factor $b$ |
| Convergence path | Smooth, deterministic | Very noisy, zigzags | Mild noise, efficient |
| Per-step speed | Very slow | Extremely fast | Fast |
| Can use GPU parallelism? | Poorly | No (single sample) | **Yes (highly parallel)** |
| Helps escape local minima? | No | **Yes (strongest)** | Yes (moderate) |
| Memory requirement | Very high | Negligible | Moderate |
| Used in modern DL? | Rarely (small datasets) | Online/streaming learning | **Standard everywhere** |

### 3.3 Derivation: Learning Rate Decay Schedule

A common schedule used in KTU problems to ensure convergence is the inverse time decay. We start with a base rate $\eta_0$ and reduce it at iteration $t$ as:

$$ \eta_t = \frac{\eta_0}{1 + \beta \cdot t} $$

where $\beta$ is the decay rate. As $t \to \infty$, we have $\eta_t \to 0$, which dampens the noise of the stochastic gradient near the optimum and allows the parameters to settle.

### 3.4 Why Does SGD Generalize Better?

Empirically (Keskar et al., 2016), small mini-batches act as a form of **implicit regularization**. The noise in the gradient updates prevents the model from settling into sharp minima (which generalize poorly to test data) and instead drives it toward **flat minima**, which generalize better.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Training Loop of Mini-Batch GD

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Initialize theta and eta"] --> B["Set epochs E, batch size b"]
    B --> C{"t less than E?"}
    C -- "No" --> D["STOP: Return trained theta"]
    C -- "Yes" --> E["Shuffle training set X, Y"]
    E --> F{"k less than m?"}
    F -- "No" --> C
    F -- "Yes" --> G["Extract mini-batch B of size b"]
    G --> H["Forward pass: compute predictions y_hat"]
    H --> I["Compute loss L over batch B"]
    I --> J["Backward pass: compute gradient g_t"]
    J --> K["Update theta: theta = theta minus eta times g_t"]
    K --> L["Increment iteration k by b"]
    L --> F
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Comparison of Convergence Paths

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SP1 [BATCH GD]
        BG1["theta_0"] --> BG2["theta_1"]
        BG2 --> BG3["theta_2"]
        BG3 --> BG4["theta_star"]
    end

    subgraph SP2 [STOCHASTIC GD]
        SG1["theta_0"] --> SG2["theta_1"]
        SG2 --> SG3["theta_2"]
        SG3 --> SG4["Oscillates around theta_star"]
    end

    subgraph SP3 [MINI BATCH GD]
        MB1["theta_0"] --> MB2["theta_1"]
        MB2 --> MB3["theta_2"]
        MB3 --> MB4["Smooth path to theta_star"]
    end
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Architecture: Functional Block Diagram of an MBGD Update

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Full Training Set of size m] --> B[Data Loader and Shuffler]
    B --> C[Sample Mini Batch B of size b]
    C --> D[Neural Network Model f of x of theta]
    D --> E[Loss Function L]
    E --> F[Compute Gradient g_t via Backprop]
    F --> G[Optimizer: SGD with eta]
    G --> H[Updated Parameters theta]
    H --> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> The closed loop between the Neural Network, the Loss, the Gradient, the Optimizer, and the Parameters represents the **iterative essence of deep learning training** — the same loop is followed whether you call it BGD, SGD, or MBGD. Only the size $b$ of the sampled batch $\mathcal{B}$ changes.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Distinguish between Batch Gradient Descent and Stochastic Gradient Descent. State one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Batch Gradient Descent (BGD):** Computes the gradient of the cost function using the *entire training dataset* of $m$ samples at every iteration. It produces a smooth, deterministic convergence path. **Advantage:** Guaranteed convergence to the global minimum (for convex problems) and stable updates.
- **Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD):** Computes the gradient using only *one randomly selected training sample* per iteration. **Advantage:** Extremely fast updates, very low memory requirement, and the noise helps in escaping shallow local minima in non-convex deep learning loss landscapes.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] What is the role of the learning rate $\eta$ in mini-batch gradient descent? What happens if it is set too high or too low?**

**Model Answer:**
The learning rate $\eta$ controls the **step size** taken in the direction opposite to the gradient at each iteration. It directly governs convergence speed and stability. If $\eta$ is **too high**, the updates overshoot the minimum, causing the loss to diverge or oscillate wildly. If $\eta$ is **too low**, training proceeds very slowly and may get stuck in poor local minima, wasting significant compute. A common best practice is to use learning rate **decay**, starting with a larger $\eta$ and gradually reducing it.

---

### Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Derive the parameter update rule for **Mini-Batch Gradient Descent** starting from the empirical risk minimization objective. Clearly state the meaning of all symbols used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A neural network is trained on a dataset of $m = 50{,}000$ samples using a mini-batch size of $b = 250$. Calculate the number of parameter updates (iterations) in **one epoch**. If the network is trained for $E = 10$ epochs, what is the **total number of updates** and the **total number of gradient evaluations** performed? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Derivation of MBGD Update Rule (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** The empirical risk minimization objective is to find parameters $\theta$ that minimize the average loss over the dataset:

$$ J(\theta) = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \mathcal{L}\left(f(x^{(i)}; \theta), y^{(i)}\right) $$

**Step 2:** The true gradient of the loss with respect to $\theta$ is:

$$ \nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}\left(f(x^{(i)}; \theta), y^{(i)}\right) $$

**Step 3:** Since $m$ is large, we approximate the full gradient using a small random mini-batch $\mathcal{B}_t$ of size $b$ at iteration $t$:

$$ g_t = \frac{1}{b} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{B}_t} \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}\left(f(x^{(i)}; \theta_t), y^{(i)}\right) $$

**Step 4:** The MBGD update rule is then:

$$ \theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta_t \cdot g_t $$

where $\eta_t$ is the learning rate. Substituting Step 3 into Step 4 gives the working equation:

$$ \theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta_t \cdot \frac{1}{b} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{B}_t} \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}\left(f(x^{(i)}; \theta_t), y^{(i)}\right) $$

**Symbols:** $m$ = total samples, $b$ = batch size, $\eta_t$ = learning rate, $\mathcal{B}_t$ = mini-batch at iteration $t$, $\mathcal{L}$ = loss function, $f$ = model.

---

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

Given: $m = 50{,}000$, $b = 250$, $E = 10$.

**Step 1: Iterations per epoch.**

$$ N_{\text{iter}} = \left\lceil \frac{m}{b} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{50{,}000}{250} \right\rceil = 200 \text{ updates per epoch} $$

**Step 2: Total updates over 10 epochs.**

$$ N_{\text{total}} = E \times N_{\text{iter}} = 10 \times 200 = 2000 \text{ parameter updates} $$

**Step 3: Total gradient evaluations.**

Each update processes one mini-batch of $b = 250$ samples, computing one gradient per sample (i.e., one gradient vector per update). Thus the number of gradient evaluations equals the number of updates:

$$ N_{\text{grad}} = 2000 \text{ gradient evaluations} $$

Total samples seen by the optimizer = $2000 \times 250 = 500{,}000$ samples, confirming 10 full passes (10 epochs).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat diagram the **convergence behavior** of Batch GD, Stochastic GD, and Mini-Batch GD on a non-convex loss surface. Mention the relative **gradient variance** of each method. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Suppose the variance of the gradient for a single sample is $\sigma^2 = 4.0$. What is the **expected variance of the mini-batch gradient estimate** if you use $b = 16$? If you instead use $b = 64$, what is the new variance? Comment on the engineering trade-off. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Convergence Behavior (7 marks):**

Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes for the Mermaid schematic. Textually:

- **Batch GD:** Produces a **smooth, deterministic, monotonic** path directly toward the minimum. Variance = **0**. Cannot escape shallow local minima; computationally expensive.

- **Stochastic GD:** Produces a **noisy, zigzagging** path. Variance = **$\sigma^2$** (highest). Fast per step; noise helps escape local minima; oscillates near the optimum.

- **Mini-Batch GD:** Produces a **moderately noisy** path that is far smoother than SGD. Variance = **$\sigma^2 / b$** (intermediate). Combines the speed of SGD with the stability of BGD; leverages GPU parallelism.

**(b) Variance Calculation (7 marks):**

Given: $\sigma^2 = 4.0$.

**Step 1: Variance for $b = 16$.**

$$ \mathrm{Var}\left[g_{16}\right] = \frac{\sigma^2}{b} = \frac{4.0}{16} = 0.25 $$

**Step 2: Variance for $b = 64$.**

$$ \mathrm{Var}\left[g_{64}\right] = \frac{\sigma^2}{b} = \frac{4.0}{64} = 0.0625 $$

**Step 3: Engineering Trade-off Comment.**

Increasing $b$ from 16 to 64 reduces the gradient variance by a factor of 4, giving a much smoother convergence path with less oscillation. However, larger $b$ also means (i) **more memory** is needed on the GPU, and (ii) **fewer parameter updates** per epoch (which can slow down progress in terms of optimizer steps), and (iii) recent research suggests very large batches may converge to **sharp minima**, which generalize worse than the flat minima found by noisier small batches. Hence, batch sizes in the range of **32 to 256** are typically chosen to balance noise, memory, and generalization.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse the symbols:** $m$ is the total dataset size; $b$ is the mini-batch size. Students frequently swap them in derivations, losing 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Always divide by $b$:** The mini-batch gradient is the **average** of the per-sample gradients. Forgetting the $1/b$ factor is a common 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Iterations vs. Epochs:** $N_{\text{iter}} = m/b$ is the *number of updates* per epoch, not the total updates. Multiplication by $E$ is required for total updates.
> 4. **SGD ≠ true SGD:** When answering, clarify that "SGD" in deep learning usually means mini-batch GD unless explicitly stated otherwise.
> 5. **Variance formula:** The $1/b$ scaling of variance applies under the assumption of *independent, identically distributed* samples — state this assumption clearly in exams.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **BGD** uses all $m$ samples per update — smooth but slow.
- **SGD** uses 1 sample per update — fast but noisy, $b = 1$.
- **MBGD** uses $b$ samples per update — the **industry standard**; $1 < b < m$.
- The canonical MBGD update is $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta_t \cdot \frac{1}{b}\sum_{i \in \mathcal{B}_t} \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}_i$.
- **Iterations per epoch** = $\lceil m/b \rceil$; **Total updates** = $E \times \lceil m/b \rceil$.
- Gradient **variance scales as $1/b$** — larger batches are smoother.
- Mini-batch size $b$ is usually a **power of 2** (32, 64, 128, 256) for hardware efficiency.
- **Learning rate $\eta$** is the most critical hyperparameter; use decay schedules (e.g., $\eta_t = \eta_0 / (1 + \beta t)$) for convergence.
- **Modern deep learning = MBGD + backprop + learning rate scheduling.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: AdaGrad
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET753 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** AdaGrad

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**AdaGrad (Adaptive Gradient Algorithm)** is a gradient-based optimization algorithm that adapts the learning rate to the parameters, performing smaller updates (i.e., low learning rates) for parameters associated with frequently occurring features, and larger updates (i.e., high learning rates) for parameters associated with infrequent features. It was introduced by Duchi, Hazan, and Singer in their seminal 2011 JMLR paper *"Adaptive Subgradient Methods for Online Learning and Stochastic Optimization."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** AdaGrad is a parameter-wise adaptive learning rate method that scales the gradient inversely proportional to the square root of the sum of all historical squared values of the gradient. It is particularly effective for **sparse data** and **convex objectives**.

Mathematically, the per-parameter update is:

$$\theta_{t+1, i} = \theta_{t, i} - \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{G_{t, ii} + \epsilon}} \cdot g_{t, i}$$

where $G_t$ is a diagonal matrix where each diagonal element $G_{t,ii}$ is the sum of the squares of the past gradients w.r.t. parameter $\theta_i$ up to time step $t$, and $\epsilon$ is a small **smoothing constant** (typically $10^{-8}$) to prevent division by zero.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Student Studying for Multiple Subjects"

Imagine a student preparing for exams across **10 different subjects** with very different syllabus sizes.

- **Subject 1 (Physics):** Huge syllabus → the student has reviewed it many times → should slow down (smaller steps) to avoid over-fitting one chapter.
- **Subject 2 (Statistics):** Tiny, sparse syllabus → the student rarely sees these problems → should take **bigger, bolder steps** to actually learn it.

AdaGrad behaves exactly like this intelligent student. For every weight (subject), it remembers how much it has been updated before (how much attention it has received) and **automatically tunes the step size**:
- Frequently updated parameters (popular subjects) → **small learning rate** to prevent overshooting.
- Rarely updated parameters (sparse features) → **large learning rate** to make meaningful progress.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Adaptive"?** Standard SGD uses one global learning rate $\eta$ for *all* parameters. AdaGrad replaces this with a *unique, time-varying, per-parameter learning rate* — hence the name **Ada**ptive **Grad**ient.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Used in KTU Boards

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Learning Rate $\eta$** | Initial global step size hyperparameter |
| **Gradient $g_t$** | Derivative of loss w.r.t. parameter at step $t$ |
| **Accumulator $r_t$** | Running sum of squared past gradients |
| **Smoothing $\epsilon$** | Tiny constant to avoid division by zero |
| **Sparse Features** | Input dimensions that are zero most of the time |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithmic Logic — Step by Step

AdaGrad modifies standard Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) by introducing a **per-parameter scaling factor**. The reasoning behind each step is given below:

- **Step 1 — Gradient Computation:** Compute the gradient of the loss function $J(\theta_t)$ with respect to each parameter $\theta_i$ at the current time step $t$.

$$g_{t,i} = \nabla_{\theta_i} J(\theta_t)$$

- **Step 2 — Accumulate Squared Gradients:** Update the running sum of the squares of the gradients for *each parameter independently*. This is a key difference — the accumulator is **per-parameter**, not global.

$$r_{t,i} = r_{t-1,i} + g_{t,i}^2$$

- **Step 3 — Compute Adaptive Learning Rate:** The effective learning rate for parameter $\theta_i$ at step $t$ becomes:

$$\eta_{t,i} = \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{r_{t,i} + \epsilon}} = \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{\sum_{\tau=1}^{t} g_{\tau,i}^2 + \epsilon}}$$

- **Step 4 — Parameter Update:** Apply the scaled update. Notice the **element-wise (Hadamard) product** between the scaled learning rate and the gradient.

$$\theta_{t+1,i} = \theta_{t,i} - \eta_{t,i} \cdot g_{t,i}$$

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind AdaGrad

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why divide by $\sqrt{r_t}$?**
> Because $r_t$ grows monotonically (sum of squares is non-negative), $\eta_{t,i}$ **monotonically decreases** over time. This produces *annealing* of the learning rate without any manual scheduling — a major advantage for **convex problems** where we want aggressive updates early and fine-tuning later.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

| Symbol | Formula / Definition | Purpose / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $g_{t,i}$ | $\nabla_{\theta_i} J(\theta_t)$ | Gradient at step $t$ for parameter $i$ |
| $r_{t,i}$ | $r_{t-1,i} + g_{t,i}^2$ | Accumulated squared gradient (per param) |
| $G_t$ | $\text{diag}(r_{t,1}, r_{t,2}, \ldots, r_{t,n})$ | Diagonal matrix form of accumulator |
| $\eta_{t,i}$ | $\dfrac{\eta}{\sqrt{r_{t,i} + \epsilon}}$ | Adaptive per-parameter learning rate |
| $\theta_{t+1,i}$ | $\theta_{t,i} - \eta_{t,i} \cdot g_{t,i}$ | Final update rule (vector form uses $\odot$) |
| $\epsilon$ | $10^{-8}$ (typical) | Smoothing constant, prevents $\div 0$ |
| $\eta$ | $0.01$ to $0.1$ (typical) | Initial global learning rate hyperparameter |

> [!NOTE]
> **Vectorized Form (commonly asked in KTU):**
> $$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{r_t + \epsilon}} \odot g_t$$
> where $\odot$ is the **Hadamard (element-wise) product**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Natural Language Processing (NLP):** Word embeddings have very sparse gradients (most words don't appear in a given mini-batch). AdaGrad was the *de facto* standard for training word2vec and GloVe.
- **Computer Vision (sparse features):** Image classification with high-dimensional sparse feature vectors (e.g., bag-of-visual-words).
- **Recommendation Systems:** User–item interaction matrices are extremely sparse, making per-parameter adaptation critical.
- **Online Learning:** Convex loss functions in real-time ad-click prediction.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Standard SGD to AdaGrad

We begin with the **Standard SGD update rule**:

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot g_t$$

where $g_t = \nabla_{\theta} J(\theta_t)$ and $\eta$ is a **single global learning rate**.

**Step 1 — Replace the scalar learning rate with a per-parameter vector:**
Recognize that each parameter $\theta_i$ may need its own learning rate $\eta_i$. We therefore propose:

$$\theta_{t+1,i} = \theta_{t,i} - \eta_i \cdot g_{t,i}$$

**Step 2 — Define the per-parameter learning rate inversely proportional to past gradient magnitudes:**
A natural choice is to make $\eta_i$ small when the *historical gradient for $\theta_i$ has been large* (meaning we have updated it a lot), and large when the historical gradient has been small (meaning it is a sparse / rare feature). Define the accumulator:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_{t,i} &= \sum_{\tau=1}^{t} g_{\tau,i}^2 \\
&= r_{t-1,i} + g_{t,i}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the sum of squares — a measure of the **total historical gradient magnitude** for parameter $i$.

**Step 3 — Invert and scale:**
We set the per-parameter learning rate as:

$$\eta_{t,i} = \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{r_{t,i} + \epsilon}}$$

**Reasoning:** The square root dampens the magnitude (since $r_t$ grows quadratically with raw gradient). The $\epsilon$ ensures numerical stability.

**Step 4 — Substitute back into the update:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_{t+1,i} &= \theta_{t,i} - \eta_{t,i} \cdot g_{t,i} \\
&= \theta_{t,i} - \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{r_{t,i} + \epsilon}} \cdot g_{t,i} \\
&= \theta_{t,i} - \frac{\eta \cdot g_{t,i}}{\sqrt{\sum_{\tau=1}^{t} g_{\tau,i}^2 + \epsilon}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Vectorize across all parameters:**
Letting $g_t, r_t \in \mathbb{R}^n$ denote the gradient vector and accumulator vector respectively, and $\odot$ denote element-wise multiplication:

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{r_t + \epsilon}} \odot g_t$$

**Step 6 — Matrix form (KTU exam favorite):**
Define $G_t$ as a diagonal matrix with $G_{t,ii} = \sum_{\tau=1}^{t} g_{\tau,i}^2$. Then:

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot G_t^{-1/2} \cdot g_t$$

where $G_t^{-1/2} = \text{diag}\left( \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{r_{t,1} + \epsilon}}, \ldots, \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{r_{t,n} + \epsilon}} \right)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway of the Derivation:** AdaGrad is *not* a fundamentally new optimizer — it is **SGD with a per-parameter, history-dependent learning rate** built from the diagonal of the outer product of past gradients.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (For KTU Numerical Problems)

> Suppose $\eta = 0.1$, $\epsilon = 10^{-8}$, and for a single parameter $\theta$ we have gradients: $g_1 = 2$, $g_2 = 1$, $g_3 = 0.5$.

**Step (a):** Initialize $r_0 = 0$, $\theta_0 = 5$.

**Step (b):** At $t=1$, $g_1 = 2$:
$$r_1 = r_0 + g_1^2 = 0 + 4 = 4$$
$$\theta_1 = \theta_0 - \frac{0.1}{\sqrt{4 + 10^{-8}}} \cdot 2 = 5 - \frac{0.1 \cdot 2}{2} = 5 - 0.1 = 4.9$$

**Step (c):** At $t=2$, $g_2 = 1$:
$$r_2 = r_1 + g_2^2 = 4 + 1 = 5$$
$$\theta_2 = \theta_1 - \frac{0.1}{\sqrt{5 + 10^{-8}}} \cdot 1 \approx 4.9 - \frac{0.1}{2.236} \approx 4.9 - 0.0447 = 4.8553$$

**Step (d):** At $t=3$, $g_3 = 0.5$:
$$r_3 = r_2 + g_3^2 = 5 + 0.25 = 5.25$$
$$\theta_3 = \theta_2 - \frac{0.1}{\sqrt{5.25 + 10^{-8}}} \cdot 0.5 \approx 4.8553 - \frac{0.05}{2.291} \approx 4.8553 - 0.0218 = 4.8335$$

**Observation:** Notice how the step size **shrinks** from $0.1 \rightarrow 0.0447 \rightarrow 0.0218$ as $r_t$ accumulates — this is **automatic learning rate decay**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — AdaGrad vs Other Optimizers (Background Reference)

| Property | SGD | AdaGrad | RMSProp | Adam |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Per-parameter LR | No | **Yes** | Yes | Yes |
| Uses $r_t$ (sum of sq. grads) | No | **Yes (monotonic)** | Yes (EMA) | Yes (EMA) |
| Decay problem | No | **Yes (LR → 0)** | No | No |
| Best for | General | **Sparse data** | Non-stationary | General DL |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithmic Flow Diagram (Mermaid)

The following block diagram illustrates the *operational architecture* of the AdaGrad update loop — how the accumulator, gradient, and parameter update interact at every time step $t$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["Start: Initialize theta and accumulator r to zero"]
    startB["Set hyperparameters eta and epsilon"]
    paramA["Read current parameters theta_t"]
    gradA["Compute gradient g_t equals gradient of J w.r.t. theta_t"]
    accA["Update accumulator: r_t equals r_t-1 plus g_t squared elementwise"]
    lrateA["Compute adaptive LR: eta_t equals eta divided by sqrt of r_t plus epsilon"]
    updA["Update parameters: theta_t+1 equals theta_t minus eta_t times g_t"]
    convA["Check convergence: loss change less than tolerance"]
    stopA["Stop: Return optimized theta"]
    loopA["Continue to next iteration t plus 1"]

    startA --> startB
    startB --> paramA
    paramA --> gradA
    gradA --> accA
    accA --> lrateA
    lrateA --> updA
    updA --> convA
    convA -->|No, not converged| loopA
    loopA --> gradA
    convA -->|Yes, converged| stopA
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture

The architecture can be broken into **three functional blocks** that communicate sequentially:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    blockA["BLOCK 1: GRADIENT COMPUTATION MODULE\nInput: theta_t, mini-batch data\nOutput: g_t = grad J theta_t"]
    blockB["BLOCK 2: ADAPTIVE SCALING MODULE\nMaintains: r_t elementwise\nOutputs: eta_t equals eta over sqrt r_t plus epsilon"]
    blockC["BLOCK 3: PARAMETER UPDATE MODULE\nInput: theta_t, eta_t, g_t\nOutput: theta_t+1"]

    blockA -->|"g_t vector"| blockB
    blockB -->|"eta_t vector per parameter"| blockC
    blockC -->|"theta_t+1 for next iteration"| blockA
```

## 4.3 Information Flow — Per-Parameter Adaptation View

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sparseFeat["Sparse Feature: Rare Parameter i\nSmall historical gradient: small r_t,i"]
    denseFeat["Dense Feature: Frequent Parameter j\nLarge historical gradient: large r_t,j"]
    sparseLR["LARGE effective LR\nAggressive update for rare feature"]
    denseLR["SMALL effective LR\nConservative update for frequent feature"]

    sparseFeat --> sparseLR
    denseFeat --> denseLR
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Notice the **closed feedback loop** in Block-Level Architecture — the updated parameters $\theta_{t+1}$ are fed back into the Gradient Computation Module for the next step. This makes AdaGrad a **stateful** optimizer (state = $r_t$ must be persisted across iterations).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]**
> **Define AdaGrad. What problem in standard SGD does it primarily solve?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
AdaGrad (Adaptive Gradient Algorithm) is a gradient-based optimization algorithm that assigns a *separate, adaptive learning rate* to each parameter by scaling the global learning rate $\eta$ inversely with the square root of the sum of all past squared gradients for that parameter. It primarily solves the problem of **using a single learning rate for all parameters** in SGD, which is inefficient when features have very different frequencies (sparse vs. dense). The update rule is $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - (\eta / \sqrt{r_t + \epsilon}) \odot g_t$. It is particularly effective for **sparse gradient scenarios** such as NLP and large-scale recommender systems. *(3 marks — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for update rule, 1 mark for application context.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **State and explain the role of the accumulator $r_t$ in the AdaGrad update rule.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The accumulator $r_t$ is defined as $r_{t,i} = r_{t-1,i} + g_{t,i}^2$, i.e., the sum of squares of all past gradients for parameter $\theta_i$ up to time step $t$. It serves two crucial roles: **(i)** It acts as a *measure of historical gradient magnitude* — parameters updated frequently accumulate large $r_t$, while sparse parameters have small $r_t$. **(ii)** It appears in the denominator of the adaptive learning rate $\eta_{t,i} = \eta / \sqrt{r_{t,i} + \epsilon}$, causing the effective learning rate to **decay monotonically over time** without manual scheduling, providing automatic annealing of step sizes — especially valuable for convex optimization. *(3 marks — 1 mark definition, 1 mark for accumulation, 1 mark for annealing role.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Question A. [KTU University Exam – July 2024, Model QP]**

**(a) Derive the AdaGrad update rule starting from standard Stochastic Gradient Descent. Show the role of the accumulator $r_t$ and the smoothing constant $\epsilon$ in the derivation. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

We start with the standard SGD rule:

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta g_t$$

**Step 1:** Recognizing that different parameters need different learning rates, we write the per-parameter form:

$$\theta_{t+1,i} = \theta_{t,i} - \eta_i g_{t,i}$$

**Step 2:** Define the accumulator as the running sum of squared gradients for parameter $i$:

$$r_{t,i} = \sum_{\tau=1}^{t} g_{\tau,i}^2 = r_{t-1,i} + g_{t,i}^2$$

**Step 3:** Set the adaptive learning rate inversely proportional to $\sqrt{r_{t,i}}$:

$$\eta_{t,i} = \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{r_{t,i} + \epsilon}}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting back, we obtain the final AdaGrad update:

$$\theta_{t+1,i} = \theta_{t,i} - \frac{\eta \cdot g_{t,i}}{\sqrt{r_{t,i} + \epsilon}}$$

**Role of $r_t$:** It tracks historical gradient magnitude. Parameters with large $r_t$ (frequently updated) get smaller step sizes; sparse parameters with small $r_t$ get larger step sizes.

**Role of $\epsilon$:** It is a tiny positive constant (typically $10^{-8}$) added inside the square root to **prevent division by zero** when $r_t$ is initially zero or extremely small. It also provides numerical stability. *(7 marks — 1 mark for starting point, 2 marks for accumulator definition, 2 marks for adaptive LR substitution, 2 marks for explaining $r_t$ and $\epsilon$ roles.)*

---

**(b) Discuss the major advantages and limitations of AdaGrad. How does the decaying learning rate affect training in non-convex deep learning problems? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Advantages:**

1. **No manual learning rate tuning:** The per-parameter adaptation eliminates the need to tune individual learning rates.
2. **Superior on sparse data:** Performs exceptionally well for sparse features (NLP, large-scale logistic regression).
3. **Automatic annealing:** The learning rate naturally decays over time, mimicking a learning rate schedule automatically.
4. **Theoretical convergence guarantees:** Provably converges for convex functions.

**Limitations:**

1. **Aggressive learning rate decay:** Since $r_t$ is a *monotonically increasing* sum (no decay), the effective LR shrinks continuously. For **non-convex deep learning**, training may *stop too early* — the model gets stuck because the learning rate becomes infinitesimally small.
2. **No memory of gradient direction:** The accumulator only stores squared magnitudes, not directional information.
3. **Sensitivity to initial gradients:** An unusually large early gradient permanently inflates $r_t$ and starves the parameter of updates for the rest of training.

**Effect in non-convex problems:**
In non-convex landscapes (e.g., deep neural networks), AdaGrad can cause training to **stall prematurely** because the monotonically growing $r_t$ drives the learning rate to near-zero, preventing the optimizer from escaping local minima or saddle points. This motivated the development of **RMSProp** and **Adam**, which use exponential moving averages (decay) instead of cumulative sums. *(7 marks — 3 marks for advantages, 2 marks for limitations, 2 marks for non-convex behavior.)*

---

> **Question B. [Internal Choice Alternative — 14 Marks]**

**(a) Explain how AdaGrad handles sparse gradients effectively. Illustrate with the example of word embeddings in NLP. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**The Sparse Gradient Problem:**
In many real-world problems (NLP, recommender systems, CTR prediction), most input features are zero in any given mini-batch. The corresponding gradient components are therefore zero or near-zero. Standard SGD treats all parameters equally, so rare-feature parameters receive the *same step size* as frequent-feature parameters — leading to **slow convergence** on rare features.

**AdaGrad's Solution:**
Because $r_{t,i}$ accumulates *only the squared gradients* that have actually been non-zero, a rare feature has a *small $r_{t,i}$*, giving it a *large effective learning rate*. Each time the rare feature appears, it receives a **disproportionately large update** — accelerating learning for that feature.

**Example — Word Embeddings in NLP:**
Consider training word2vec on a 100,000-word vocabulary. In any mini-batch of sentences, only ~50 unique words appear, so only ~50 rows of the embedding matrix receive non-zero gradients.

- With **SGD**: All 100,000 embedding rows get the *same* learning rate $\eta = 0.1$ — but only 50 ever get updated meaningfully. Rare words barely move.
- With **AdaGrad**: Common words (frequent in many batches) accumulate large $r_{t,i}$, getting small updates. Rare words (appearing only a few times) have small $r_{t,i}$, getting *larger* updates each time they appear — so they make rapid progress despite their rarity.

This is precisely why AdaGrad was the **default optimizer for word2vec and GloVe** before Adam became popular. *(7 marks — 2 marks problem statement, 2 marks AdaGrad mechanism, 3 marks NLP illustration.)*

---

**(b) Compare AdaGrad with standard SGD. Discuss the key mathematical difference and its impact on convergence. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | Standard SGD | AdaGrad |
|---|---|---|
| Learning rate | Single global $\eta$ | Per-parameter $\eta_{t,i}$ |
| Historical info used | Only current $g_t$ | All past squared gradients $r_t$ |
| Update rule | $\theta_t - \eta g_t$ | $\theta_t - (\eta / \sqrt{r_t + \epsilon}) \odot g_t$ |
| LR over time | Constant | Monotonically decreasing |
| Sparse data performance | Poor | Excellent |
| Non-convex training | Stable but slow | Risk of premature stop |

**Key Mathematical Difference:**
The presence of the **diagonal preconditioner** $G_t^{-1/2} = \text{diag}(1/\sqrt{r_{t,i} + \epsilon})$ in the update. In SGD, the gradient is used as-is; in AdaGrad, it is **element-wise rescaled** by a function of its own past.

**Impact on Convergence:**

- For **convex problems**, AdaGrad provides *regret bounds* of $O(\sqrt{T})$ and faster convergence than SGD when features are sparse or ill-conditioned.
- For **non-convex deep learning**, the cumulative $r_t$ causes the effective LR to shrink to near-zero, often **halting training before reaching a good minimum** — this is AdaGrad's main weakness, addressed by RMSProp, Adam, and Adadelta (which use exponential moving averages with a decay factor).
- SGD, lacking this decay, may oscillate near minima but doesn't suffer from premature stagnation. *(7 marks — 3 marks for comparison table, 2 marks for mathematical difference, 2 marks for convergence impact.)*

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the square root** — Writing $\eta_{t,i} = \eta / (r_{t,i} + \epsilon)$ instead of $\eta / \sqrt{r_{t,i} + \epsilon}$ costs **2 marks immediately**. Always show the $\sqrt{}$ symbol.
> 2. **Omitting $\epsilon$** — Examiners specifically test whether you remember the smoothing constant. Mentioning $\epsilon = 10^{-8}$ adds 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing $r_t$ with momentum/moving average** — AdaGrad uses a *cumulative sum* (no decay), unlike RMSProp/Adam which use *exponential moving average*. This distinction is frequently tested.
> 4. **Writing global LR instead of per-parameter** — Saying "AdaGrad uses a different learning rate" without specifying *per-parameter* is incomplete. Always state **"per-parameter, time-varying learning rate."**
> 5. **Missing the $\odot$ (Hadamard) symbol** in vector form — Use $\odot$ for element-wise operations, not regular multiplication.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Full Name:** **Ada**ptive **Grad**ient Algorithm (Duchi et al., 2011).
- **Core Idea:** Replace the single global learning rate with a *per-parameter* rate that adapts based on past gradient history.
- **Accumulator:** $r_{t,i} = r_{t-1,i} + g_{t,i}^2$ — sum of *squared* past gradients (element-wise, **no decay**).
- **Update Rule:** $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - (\eta / \sqrt{r_t + \epsilon}) \odot g_t$ — element-wise (Hadamard) operation.
- **Smoothing Constant:** $\epsilon \approx 10^{-8}$ prevents division by zero and ensures numerical stability.
- **Monotonic Decay:** Since $r_t$ only grows, the effective LR $\eta_{t,i}$ monotonically decreases — automatic annealing.
- **Best Use Case:** **Sparse data** — NLP (word2vec, GloVe), large-vocabulary logistic regression, recommender systems.
- **Main Weakness:** Aggressive decay $\Rightarrow$ effective LR can become *infinitely small*, **stalling training on non-convex problems** (e.g., deep CNNs, RNNs).
- **Successors:** **RMSProp** (decay factor on $r_t$), **Adam** (momentum + RMSProp), **Adadelta** — all fix AdaGrad's decay problem.
- **Symbol to Remember in Exams:** The $\odot$ (Hadamard product) is the *signature symbol* of AdaGrad's vector form.
- **Key Phrase for Theory Answers:** "AdaGrad adapts the learning rate *per-parameter* by scaling inversely with the *square root* of the *cumulative sum* of *squared gradients*."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RMSProp
topics.content:
