# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — The Design Workflow

Designing an RNN follows a structured engineering workflow. Each step must be deliberately chosen based on the task requirements.

### Step 1: Problem Formulation
Identify the type of sequence-to-sequence mapping required:
- **Sequence classification** (e.g., sentiment analysis) → many-to-one
- **Sequence generation** (e.g., music generation) → one-to-many
- **Sequence translation** (e.g., machine translation) → many-to-many (encoder-decoder)
- **Frame-wise prediction** (e.g., video classification per frame) → many-to-many (synchronized)

### Step 2: Input Representation Design
Each token in the sequence must be transformed into a **dense vector** via an embedding layer:

$$x^{(t)} = E[w^{(t)}] \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{emb}}$$

where $E \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert V \vert \times d_{emb}}$ is the embedding matrix and $w^{(t)}$ is the one-hot or index representation of the $t$-th word.

### Step 3: Recurrent Cell Selection
Choose the recurrent cell type:
- **Vanilla (Elman) RNN** — simple $\tanh$ activation
- **LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory)** — gated cell with forget, input, and output gates
- **GRU (Gated Recurrent Unit)** — simplified gating with reset and update gates
- **Peephole LSTM** — gates see cell state

### Step 4: Unrolling Strategy
Decide the **unrolling length** — how many time steps to backpropagate through. This determines the **effective depth** of the network in the time dimension.

### Step 5: Output Layer Design
Choose the output projection:
- **Every step** → $y^{(t)} = g(Vh^{(t)} + c)$ (for frame-wise prediction)
- **Last step only** → $y = g(Vh^{(T)} + c)$ (for classification)
- **Sampled/generative** → $y^{(t)} \sim \text{Categorical}(\text{softmax}(Vh^{(t)}))$

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Design Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why parameter sharing?** If we used different weights at each time step, the model would need a **separate parameter set per position**, making it impossible to generalize to sequence lengths not seen during training. Sharing $U, W, V$ across all time steps is what makes RNNs **length-generalizable**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why hidden state?** The hidden state is a **lossy summary** of all past inputs. It compresses the entire history $(x^{(1)}, x^{(2)}, \ldots, x^{(t-1)})$ into a fixed-size vector $h^{(t-1)}$. This is the **only mechanism** by which the past influences the future in the network.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why non-linearity $f$?** Without a non-linear activation, stacking recurrent layers would collapse into a single linear transformation (since linear compositions are linear). The non-linearity enables the network to learn **complex temporal manifolds**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Formatting Note:** In the table below, the absolute value bars (used for matrix determinants or vector magnitudes) are written as `\vert` to prevent them from breaking the markdown table parser.

| # | Formula | Description | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $h^{(t)} = f(U x^{(t)} + W h^{(t-1)} + b)$ | Hidden state update | Core recurrence |
| 2 | $y^{(t)} = g(V h^{(t)} + c)$ | Output projection | Frame-wise prediction |
| 3 | $h^{(0)} = \mathbf{0}$ (or learned) | Initial state | Bootstrap of computation |
| 4 | $P_{total} = \vert U \vert + \vert W \vert + \vert V \vert + \vert b \vert + \vert c \vert$ | Parameter count | Model sizing |
| 5 | $\text{Unroll}(T) = T \times L$ | Effective depth | BPTT depth |
| 6 | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{t=1}^{T} \log p(y^{(t)} \mid x^{(1:T)})$ | Cross-entropy loss (per-step) | Training objective |
| 7 | $h^{(t)} = \text{LSTM}(x^{(t)}, h^{(t-1)}, C^{(t-1)})$ | LSTM cell update | Long-range dependencies |
| 8 | $\overrightarrow{h}^{(t)} = f(\overrightarrow{W} x^{(t)} + \overrightarrow{U} \overrightarrow{h}^{(t-1)} + \overrightarrow{b})$ | Forward direction (Bi-RNN) | Bidirectional context |
| 9 | $\overleftarrow{h}^{(t)} = f(\overleftarrow{W} x^{(t)} + \overleftarrow{U} \overleftarrow{h}^{(t-1)} + \overleftarrow{b})$ | Backward direction (Bi-RNN) | Bidirectional context |
| 10 | $h^{(t)} = [\overrightarrow{h}^{(t)}; \overleftarrow{h}^{(t)}]$ | Concatenation (Bi-RNN) | Combined representation |
| 11 | $h_{l}^{(t)} = f(U_l h_{l-1}^{(t)} + W_l h_{l}^{(t-1)} + b_l)$ | Stacked (deep) RNN layer $l$ | Hierarchical features |
| 12 | $\text{Teacher Forcing: } y^{(t)}_{train} = y^{(t)}_{true}$ | Training with ground truth | Faster convergence |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> RNN design principles underpin critical real-world systems:
> - **Speech Recognition** (Google Assistant, Apple Siri) — many-to-one to one-to-many
> - **Machine Translation** (Google Translate pre-Transformer era) — encoder-decoder
> - **Music Generation** (MuseNet, Magenta) — one-to-many autoregressive
> - **Time Series Forecasting** (stock prices, weather) — many-to-one
> - **Video Frame Prediction** — synchronized many-to-many
> - **Named Entity Recognition** — many-to-many frame-wise

The design choices discussed above directly determine whether a deployed system can meet real-time latency requirements, handle variable-length inputs, and generalize to novel sequences.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Unfolding the Computational Graph

The compact recurrence $h^{(t)} = f(U x^{(t)} + W h^{(t-1)} + b)$ is equivalent to an unrolled computational graph of depth $T$. We derive this explicitly.

### Step 1: Write the recurrence at $t = 1$

$$h^{(1)} = f(U x^{(1)} + W h^{(0)} + b)$$

**Conversion Logic:** The first hidden state is computed by feeding $x^{(1)}$ and the initial state $h^{(0)}$ through the recurrent cell. The initial state is typically a zero vector or a learned parameter.

### Step 2: Write the recurrence at $t = 2$

$$h^{(2)} = f(U x^{(2)} + W h^{(1)} + b)$$

**Conversion Logic:** Now $h^{(1)}$ (already computed in Step 1) is used as the previous state. This introduces a **dependency** on the entire past.

### Step 3: Substitute $h^{(1)}$ into $h^{(2)}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
h^{(2)} &= f(U x^{(2)} + W \left[ f(U x^{(1)} + W h^{(0)} + b) \right] + b) \\
&= f(U x^{(2)} + W f(U x^{(1)} + W h^{(0)} + b) + b)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** This explicit substitution shows that $h^{(2)}$ depends on $x^{(1)}, x^{(2)}, h^{(0)}$. Continuing this process makes the **full history** dependency explicit.

### Step 4: Generalize to arbitrary $t$

$$
\begin{aligned}
h^{(t)} &= f(U x^{(t)} + W h^{(t-1)} + b) \\
&= f(U x^{(t)} + W f(U x^{(t-1)} + W h^{(t-2)} + b) + b) \\
&= f\left( U x^{(t)} + W f\left( U x^{(t-1)} + W f\left( \cdots f(U x^{(1)} + W h^{(0)} + b) \cdots \right) + b \right) + b \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** This nested function composition is the mathematical reason RNNs are theoretically capable of capturing arbitrarily long dependencies — but also why they suffer from **vanishing/exploding gradients** in practice.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Parameter Count for a Vanilla RNN

The total number of trainable parameters in a vanilla RNN with input dimension $d_x$, hidden dimension $d_h$, and output dimension $d_y$ is derived as follows.

### Step 1: Identify the weight matrices

- $U \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h \times d_x}$ (input-to-hidden)
- $W \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h \times d_h}$ (hidden-to-hidden)
- $V \in \mathbb{R}^{d_y \times d_h}$ (hidden-to-output)
- $b \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h}$ (hidden bias)
- $c \in \mathbb{R}^{d_y}$ (output bias)

### Step 2: Count parameters in each

$$
\begin{aligned}
|U| &= d_h \cdot d_x \\
|W| &= d_h \cdot d_h = d_h^2 \\
|V| &= d_y \cdot d_h \\
|b| &= d_h \\
|c| &= d_y
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Each weight matrix has dimensions equal to the product of its output and input sizes. Bias vectors have one entry per output dimension.

### Step 3: Sum all parameters

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{total} &= d_h \cdot d_x + d_h^2 + d_y \cdot d_h + d_h + d_y \\
&= d_h(d_x + d_h + d_y + 1) + d_y
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** This is the **constant** parameter count — it does **not** grow with sequence length $T$. This is the key property of parameter sharing that makes RNNs tractable for long sequences.

### Step 4: Numerical Example

For a sentiment classifier with $d_x = 300$ (word embedding), $d_h = 128$, $d_y = 2$ (positive/negative):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{total} &= 128 \cdot 300 + 128^2 + 2 \cdot 128 + 128 + 2 \\
&= 38400 + 16384 + 256 + 128 + 2 \\
&= 55170 \text{ parameters}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Regardless of whether the input review is 10 words or 1000 words, the network has only **55,170** parameters. This is the engineering marvel of RNN design.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Output Strategies for Different Topologies

The design of the output layer depends on the input-output topology.

### Topology A: Many-to-One (Sequence Classification)

Only the **final** hidden state produces an output:

$$y = g(V h^{(T)} + c)$$

**Conversion Logic:** The hidden state $h^{(T)}$ has already integrated information from all $T$ inputs, so it serves as a fixed-size summary of the entire sequence.

### Topology B: One-to-Many (Sequence Generation)

The initial state encodes the input, and outputs are produced at every step:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h^{(t)} &= f(U x^{(t)} + W h^{(t-1)} + b) \\
y^{(t)} &= g(V h^{(t)} + c) \\
x^{(t+1)} &= y^{(t)} \quad \text{(autoregressive)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The output of the previous step becomes the input of the next step. This autoregressive loop is the basis of language models.

### Topology C: Many-to-Many (Encoder-Decoder)

The encoder summarizes the input sequence, and the decoder generates the output:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Encoder:} \quad & h_{enc}^{(t)} = f(U_{enc} x^{(t)} + W_{enc} h_{enc}^{(t-1)} + b_{enc}) \\
& c = h_{enc}^{(T)} \quad \text{(context vector)} \\
\text{Decoder:} \quad & h_{dec}^{(t')} = f(U_{dec} y^{(t'-1)} + W_{dec} h_{dec}^{(t'-1)} + V_{dec} c + b_{dec}) \\
& y^{(t')} = g(V_{out} h_{dec}^{(t')} + c_{out})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The context vector $c$ is the **information bottleneck** between encoder and decoder. Its fixed dimensionality is a major design constraint (addressed by attention mechanisms).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Topology Selection Guide

| Topology | Input | Output | $h^{(0)}$ | Output When | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **One-to-One** | Single $x$ | Single $y$ | Not used | After computation | Standard classification |
| **One-to-Many** | Single $x$ | Sequence $y^{(1:T)}$ | Encodes $x$ | Every step | Image captioning, music generation |
| **Many-to-One** | Sequence $x^{(1:T)}$ | Single $y$ | Zero/learned | After $T$ steps | Sentiment analysis, classification |
| **Many-to-Many (sync)** | Sequence $x^{(1:T)}$ | Sequence $y^{(1:T)}$ | Zero/learned | Every step | POS tagging, frame-wise labeling |
| **Many-to-Many (async)** | Sequence $x^{(1:T_x)}$ | Sequence $y^{(1:T_y)}$ | Encoded by encoder | Decoder steps | Machine translation |

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Stacking Recurrent Layers (Deep RNN)

A "deep" RNN has multiple recurrent layers stacked vertically. The design is as follows.

### Step 1: Define layer-1 hidden state

$$h_1^{(t)} = f(U_1 x^{(t)} + W_1 h_1^{(t-1)} + b_1)$$

### Step 2: Define layer-$l$ hidden state for $l > 1$

$$h_l^{(t)} = f(U_l h_{l-1}^{(t)} + W_l h_l^{(t-1)} + b_l)$$

**Conversion Logic:** Each layer $l$ takes the output sequence of layer $l-1$ as its input. This creates a **hierarchical feature extraction** analogous to deep CNNs: lower layers capture short-term patterns, higher layers capture long-term semantics.

### Step 3: Final output projection

$$y^{(t)} = g(V h_L^{(t)} + c)$$

where $h_L^{(t)}$ is the topmost layer's hidden state.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Recurrent Network Compact vs. Unrolled Form

The following Mermaid diagram shows the relationship between the **compact** (recurrent) representation and the **unrolled** (computational graph) representation that is essential to understanding RNN design.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[x_t: Input at time t] --> B[Hidden Cell f]
    B --> C[h_t: Hidden State]
    C --> D[Output Layer g]
    D --> E[y_t: Output]
    C -.->|h_t-1 feedback| B
    F[x_t-1] --> B
    G[x_t+1] --> B
```

**Caption:** The compact form shows the recurrent loop (h_{t-1} feeds back into the cell). The unrolled form (Diagram 2) explicitly shows this as a chain.

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Unrolled Computational Graph in Time

```mermaid
graph LR
    x1[x1] --> h1[h1 = f]
    h0[h0: initial] --> h1
    h1 --> h2[h2 = f]
    x2[x2] --> h2
    h2 --> h3[h3 = f]
    x3[x3] --> h3
    h3 --> h4[h4T = f]
    x4[xT] --> h4
    h1 --> y1[y1 = g]
    h2 --> y2[y2 = g]
    h3 --> y3[y3 = g]
    h4 --> yT[yT = g]
```

**Caption:** The unrolled graph shows the same weight matrices $(U, W, V)$ being reused at every time step. This visualization is the foundation of **Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)**.

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — RNN Input-Output Topologies

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph T1["One-to-One (Standard NN)"]
        A1[Input x] --> B1[Hidden] --> C1[Output y]
    end
    subgraph T2["One-to-Many (e.g., Image Captioning)"]
        A2[Input x] --> B2[h1]
        B2 --> C2[y1]
        B2 --> D2[h2]
        D2 --> E2[y2]
        D2 --> F2[h3]
        F2 --> G2[y3]
    end
    subgraph T3["Many-to-One (e.g., Sentiment)"]
        A3[x1] --> B3[h1]
        B3 --> C3[h2]
        x2 --> C3
        C3 --> D3[hT]
        x3 --> D3
        D3 --> E3[Output y]
    end
    subgraph T4["Many-to-Many (e.g., Translation)"]
        A4[x1] --> B4[h1_enc]
        B4 --> C4[h2_enc]
        x2 --> C4
        C4 --> D4[hT_enc]
        D4 --> E4[Decoder h1]
        E4 --> F4[Decoder h2]
        F4 --> G4[Output y2]
        E4 --> H4[Output y1]
    end
```

**Caption:** The four fundamental RNN topologies. Each design choice directly follows from the problem's input-output structure.

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Bidirectional RNN Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    x1[x1] --> fw1[Forward Cell]
    x2[x2] --> fw2[Forward Cell]
    x3[x3] --> fw3[Forward Cell]
    x4[x4] --> fw4[Forward Cell]
    x1 --> bw4[Backward Cell]
    x2 --> bw3[Backward Cell]
    x3 --> bw2[Backward Cell]
    x4 --> bw1[Backward Cell]
    fw1 --> c1[h1 = fwd concat bwd]
    fw2 --> c2[h2 = fwd concat bwd]
    fw3 --> c3[h3 = fwd concat bwd]
    fw4 --> c4[h4 = fwd concat bwd]
    bw4 --> c1
    bw3 --> c2
    bw2 --> c3
    bw1 --> c4
    c4 --> out[Output y]
```

**Caption:** A Bidirectional RNN processes the sequence in both directions, concatenating the forward and backward hidden states. This is useful when the entire sequence is available at inference time (e.g., text, but **not** real-time speech).

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Stacked (Deep) RNN Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    x1[x1] --> L1A[h1_layer1]
    x2[x2] --> L1B[h2_layer1]
    x3[x3] --> L1C[h3_layer1]
    L1A --> L2A[h1_layer2]
    L1B --> L2B[h2_layer2]
    L1C --> L2C[h3_layer2]
    L2A --> L3A[h1_layer3]
    L2B --> L3B[h2_layer3]
    L2C --> L3C[h3_layer3]
    L3A --> OUT1[y1]
    L3B --> OUT2[y2]
    L3C --> OUT3[y3]
    L1A -.->|h0| L1B
    L1B -.->|h1| L1C
    L2A -.->|h0| L2B
    L2B -.->|h1| L2C
```

**Caption:** A 3-layer stacked RNN. Each layer processes the full sequence with its own recurrent connection, while passing temporal representations vertically to the next layer.

## 4.6 Diagram 6 — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of RNN Training

```mermaid
graph TB
    A[Input Sequence x1 to xT] --> B[Embedding Layer E]
    B --> C[Recurrent Cell Layer 1]
    C --> D[Recurrent Cell Layer 2]
    D --> E[Output Projection V]
    E --> F[Softmax or Linear Output]
    F --> G[Loss Computation L]
    H[Ground Truth Labels] --> G
    G --> I[Gradient Computation BPTT]
    I --> J[Parameter Update U W V b c]
    J -.->|Updated weights| C
    J -.->|Updated weights| D
    J -.->|Updated weights| E
```

**Caption:** The complete functional flow of RNN design and training. The **dashed feedback loop** represents the iterative parameter updates that occur during training.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A1]**
> **Q1.** Define the term **parameter sharing** in the context of Recurrent Neural Network design. Why is it considered the most important design property of RNNs? **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
Parameter sharing in RNNs refers to the practice of using the **same weight matrices** $(U, W, V)$ at **every time step** of the network, rather than learning separate weights for each position in the sequence. This is considered the most important design property because:
1. It enables the network to **generalize to variable-length sequences** not seen during training.
2. It dramatically **reduces the parameter count** — the total number of parameters does not grow with sequence length $T$.
3. It allows the model to treat the sequence as having **translation invariance** in time, analogous to CNN's translation invariance in space. **(3 Marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A2]**
> **Q2.** List and briefly explain any **three fundamental RNN input-output topologies** with one real-world example for each. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Many-to-One:** The input is a sequence and the output is a single value. *Example:* Sentiment classification (input: review text; output: positive/negative). The final hidden state $h^{(T)}$ is fed to the output layer.
2. **One-to-Many:** A single input is expanded into a sequence. *Example:* Image captioning (input: image; output: caption word sequence). The input seeds $h^{(0)}$ and outputs are generated autoregressively.
3. **Many-to-Many (Synchronized):** Both input and output are sequences of the same length. *Example:* Part-of-speech tagging (input: sentence; output: POS tag per word). An output is produced at every time step. **(3 Marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question B-A]**
> **Q.A(a).** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a Recurrent Neural Network**. Derive the forward propagation equations and show how the same set of weights is reused at every time step via **unrolling**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Architecture Description:**
A Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) consists of three sets of weight matrices:
- $U$: input-to-hidden weights (shape $d_h \times d_x$)
- $W$: hidden-to-hidden recurrent weights (shape $d_h \times d_h$)
- $V$: hidden-to-output weights (shape $d_y \times d_h$)

The hidden state $h^{(t)}$ acts as the **memory** of the network, summarizing all past inputs.

**Step 2 — Forward Propagation Equations:**

$$h^{(t)} = f(U x^{(t)} + W h^{(t-1)} + b)$$

$$y^{(t)} = g(V h^{(t)} + c)$$

where $f$ is typically $\tanh$ and $g$ is softmax for classification.

**Step 3 — Unrolling Demonstration:**
At $t=1$: $h^{(1)} = f(U x^{(1)} + W h^{(0)} + b)$
At $t=2$: $h^{(2)} = f(U x^{(2)} + W h^{(1)} + b) = f(U x^{(2)} + W f(U x^{(1)} + W h^{(0)} + b) + b)$
At $t=3$: $h^{(3)} = f(U x^{(3)} + W h^{(2)} + b)$

**Step 4 — Parameter Reuse:**
Notice that the **same matrices $U, W, b$** appear at every time step. This is the **unrolling trick**: the recurrent cell with a self-loop is mathematically equivalent to a deep feedforward network with $T$ layers, where all layers share the same weights. **(7 Marks)**

---

> **Q.A(b).** Explain the design considerations for choosing between a **Vanilla RNN, LSTM, and GRU** cell. Compare their parameter counts and explain when each is preferred in practice. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Vanilla RNN:**
The simplest recurrent cell uses a single non-linear transformation:
$$h^{(t)} = \tanh(W h^{(t-1)} + U x^{(t)} + b)$$
**Parameter count:** $d_h(d_h + d_x + 1)$. **Problem:** Suffers from vanishing/exploding gradients for long sequences. **Use:** Short sequences, baseline models, teaching purposes.

**Step 2 — LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory):**
Introduces a **cell state** $C^{(t)}$ and three gates (forget $f$, input $i$, output $o$):
$$
\begin{aligned}
f^{(t)} &= \sigma(W_f [h^{(t-1)}, x^{(t)}] + b_f) \\
i^{(t)} &= \sigma(W_i [h^{(t-1)}, x^{(t)}] + b_i) \\
\tilde{C}^{(t)} &= \tanh(W_C [h^{(t-1)}, x^{(t)}] + b_C) \\
C^{(t)} &= f^{(t)} \odot C^{(t-1)} + i^{(t)} \odot \tilde{C}^{(t)} \\
o^{(t)} &= \sigma(W_o [h^{(t-1)}, x^{(t)}] + b_o) \\
h^{(t)} &= o^{(t)} \odot \tanh(C^{(t)})
\end{aligned}
$$
**Parameter count:** $4 \times d_h(d_h + d_x + 1)$ (4× the vanilla RNN). **Use:** Long sequences, complex dependencies (e.g., document-level NLP).

**Step 3 — GRU (Gated Recurrent Unit):**
Simplified gating with **reset** $r$ and **update** $z$ gates:
$$
\begin{aligned}
z^{(t)} &= \sigma(W_z [h^{(t-1)}, x^{(t)}]) \\
r^{(t)} &= \sigma(W_r [h^{(t-1)}, x^{(t)}]) \\
\tilde{h}^{(t)} &= \tanh(W [r^{(t)} \odot h^{(t-1)}, x^{(t)}]) \\
h^{(t)} &= (1 - z^{(t)}) \odot h^{(t-1)} + z^{(t)} \odot \tilde{h}^{(t)}
\end{aligned}
$$
**Parameter count:** $3 \times d_h(d_h + d_x + 1)$ (3× the vanilla RNN). **Use:** When LSTM is overkill but vanilla RNN is insufficient; faster training.

**Step 4 — Comparison Table:**

| Cell | Gates | Parameters | Training Speed | Long-Range Memory |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Vanilla RNN | 0 | $1\times$ | Fastest | Poor |
| GRU | 2 | $3\times$ | Fast | Good |
| LSTM | 3 | $4\times$ | Slower | Best |

**Step 5 — Practical Selection Rule:**
- Sequence length $T < 20$: Vanilla RNN is acceptable.
- $20 \leq T \leq 200$: GRU is the sweet spot.
- $T > 200$ or document-level tasks: LSTM is preferred. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question B-B]**
> **Q.B(a).** Explain the **Encoder-Decoder (Seq2Seq) architecture** for RNN design. Derive the forward pass equations and explain the role of the **context vector**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Motivation:**
Many sequence tasks have **input and output sequences of different lengths** (e.g., machine translation, summarization). A standard RNN cannot map a variable-length input to a variable-length output directly. The Encoder-Decoder architecture solves this.

**Step 2 — Encoder Phase:**
The encoder processes the input sequence $(x^{(1)}, x^{(2)}, \ldots, x^{(T_x)})$ one step at a time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{enc}^{(t)} &= f_{enc}(U_{enc} x^{(t)} + W_{enc} h_{enc}^{(t-1)} + b_{enc}) \quad \text{for } t = 1, 2, \ldots, T_x
\end{aligned}
$$

The **context vector** is the final encoder hidden state:
$$c = h_{enc}^{(T_x)} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h}$$

**Step 3 — Decoder Phase:**
The decoder generates the output sequence one token at a time, conditioned on the context vector and the previously generated token:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{dec}^{(t')} &= f_{dec}(U_{dec} y^{(t'-1)} + W_{dec} h_{dec}^{(t'-1)} + V_{dec} c + b_{dec}) \\
y^{(t')} &= g_{dec}(V_{out} h_{dec}^{(t')} + c_{out})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Role of the Context Vector:**
The context vector $c$ serves as a **fixed-size summary** of the entire input sequence. It is the **only channel of information** from encoder to decoder. This creates an **information bottleneck**, especially problematic for long input sequences — a limitation that later motivated **attention mechanisms** (Bahdanau et al., 2014).

**Step 5 — Teacher Forcing (Training):**
During training, the decoder's input at time $t'$ is the **ground truth** $y^{(t'-1)}_{true}$ rather than its own previous prediction. This accelerates convergence but introduces **exposure bias** at inference time. **(7 Marks)**

---

> **Q.B(b).** Explain **Bidirectional RNNs** and **Deep (Stacked) RNNs**. Show how the forward pass equations are modified in each case and state one practical application for each. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Bidirectional RNN (Bi-RNN):**
A Bi-RNN processes the input sequence in **both forward and backward** directions using two separate recurrent cells, then concatenates (or sums) the hidden states.

**Forward pass equations:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\overrightarrow{h}^{(t)} &= f(\overrightarrow{W} x^{(t)} + \overrightarrow{U} \overrightarrow{h}^{(t-1)} + \overrightarrow{b}) \\
\overleftarrow{h}^{(t)} &= f(\overleftarrow{W} x^{(t)} + \overleftarrow{U} \overleftarrow{h}^{(t+1)} + \overleftarrow{b}) \\
h^{(t)} &= [\overrightarrow{h}^{(t)}; \overleftarrow{h}^{(t)}] \in \mathbb{R}^{2 d_h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Key design property:** At each time step $t$, the model has access to **both past and future context**. This is critical for tasks like:
- **Named Entity Recognition** (a word's tag depends on both preceding and following words)
- **Machine Translation** (encoder side)
- **Protein structure prediction**

**Limitation:** Requires the **entire input sequence to be available** at inference — cannot be used for real-time streaming.

**Step 2 — Deep (Stacked) RNN:**
Multiple recurrent layers are stacked vertically, with the output of layer $l-1$ serving as the input to layer $l$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_1^{(t)} &= f(U_1 x^{(t)} + W_1 h_1^{(t-1)} + b_1) \\
h_2^{(t)} &= f(U_2 h_1^{(t)} + W_2 h_2^{(t-1)} + b_2) \\
&\vdots \\
h_L^{(t)} &= f(U_L h_{L-1}^{(t)} + W_L h_L^{(t-1)} + b_L) \\
y^{(t)} &= g(V h_L^{(t)} + c)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Key design property:** Each layer learns a **hierarchical representation** of the sequence. Lower layers capture **local/syntactic patterns**; higher layers capture **long-range/semantic patterns**.

**Practical applications:** Speech recognition (deep LSTM stacks of 3–5 layers), machine translation (4–8 layer encoder-decoder).

**Step 3 — Parameter Count Comparison:**
- Shallow Bi-RNN: $2 \times d_h(d_h + d_x + d_y + 1)$
- Deep RNN with $L$ layers: $L \times d_h(d_h + d_x + 1) + d_y(d_h + 1)$

**Step 4 — Design Trade-off:**
Stacking increases **representational capacity** but also **training difficulty** (more vanishing gradients). In practice, 2–4 layers is the sweet spot for vanilla RNNs; LSTMs can be stacked 4–8 layers. **(7 Marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing parameter sharing with parameter tying:** Students often write that "weights are the same" without explaining *why* this enables length generalization. Always explicitly state the engineering rationale.
> 2. **Forgetting the bias terms $b$ and $c$:** These are part of the design. Omitting them costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Mixing up input/output notation:** $x^{(t)}$ is input, $h^{(t)}$ is hidden, $y^{(t)}$ is output. Examiners are strict about this.
> 4. **Not drawing the unrolled graph:** For questions asking "with a neat diagram," failing to show the unrolled version (vs. compact form) results in a 2-mark deduction.
> 5. **Bidirectional RNNs at inference time:** Many students incorrectly claim Bi-RNNs can be used for real-time prediction. State explicitly that the full sequence is required.
> 6. **Encoder-Decoder context vector size:** Always state the dimension of $c$ (it equals $d_h$) and the resulting information bottleneck.
> 7. **Parameter count for stacked RNNs:** Use the formula $L \times d_h(d_h + d_x + 1) + d_y(d_h + 1)$, **not** the formula for a single layer multiplied by $L$ (you'll get partial credit only).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — RNN Design**

- ✅ **Parameter sharing** is the *defining* design property of RNNs — same weights $(U, W, V)$ at every time step.
- ✅ The **hidden state** $h^{(t)}$ is the lossy summary of all past inputs; it is the *only* carrier of memory.
- ✅ The **non-linearity** $f$ (usually $\tanh$) is essential — without it, recurrent layers collapse to a single linear map.
- ✅ Four canonical topologies: **one-to-one, one-to-many, many-to-one, many-to-many** (sync and async).
- ✅ **Encoder-Decoder** uses a fixed-size **context vector** $c = h_{enc}^{(T_x)}$ as the information bottleneck between input and output sequences.
- ✅ **Bidirectional RNNs** use two cells (forward and backward) and concatenate hidden states — requires the full sequence at inference.
- ✅ **Stacked (Deep) RNNs** stack $L$ recurrent layers vertically; each layer's output is the next layer's input sequence.
- ✅ **Parameter count** for vanilla RNN: $P = d_h(d_x + d_h + d_y + 1) + d_y$ — **independent of sequence length $T$**.
- ✅ **Teacher forcing** speeds up training by using ground truth $y^{(t-1)}_{true}$ as decoder input.
- ✅ **LSTM** has **4× the parameters** of vanilla RNN; **GRU** has **3×**.
- ✅ RNN design must account for **vanishing/exploding gradients** in long sequences — motivates LSTM/GRU/attention.
- ✅ **Output projection** is either at every step (frame-wise) or only at the final step $t = T$ (classification).
- ✅ The unrolled RNN is mathematically equivalent to a **deep feedforward network with weight sharing** across layers.
- ✅ Initial hidden state $h^{(0)}$ is typically zero or a **learned parameter**; this is a design choice.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Encoder-Decoder Sequence to Sequence Architectures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET753 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Encoder-Decoder Sequence to Sequence Architectures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Encoder-Decoder Sequence-to-Sequence Architectures

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Encoder-Decoder Sequence-to-Sequence (Seq2Seq) Model:** A neural network architecture composed of two recurrent networks — an **Encoder** that compresses an input sequence of variable length $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_T)$ into a fixed-dimensional latent representation (called the **context vector** or **thought vector** $\mathbf{c}$), and a **Decoder** that generates an output sequence of variable length $\mathbf{y} = (y_1, y_2, \dots, y_{T'})$ conditioned on $\mathbf{c}$.

The model is trained to maximize the conditional probability:

$$P(y_1, y_2, \dots, y_{T'} \mid x_1, x_2, \dots, x_T)$$

This class of models was popularized by **Sutskever et al. (2014)** and **Cho et al. (2014)** for statistical machine translation, and forms the conceptual backbone of virtually all modern neural sequence transduction systems (Transformers being a non-recurrent descendant).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Tourist & The Translator

Imagine you are an English tourist in Kerala who does not know Malayalam. You hand a long Malayalam sentence to a **Translator (Encoder)**. The translator reads the entire sentence, summarizes its *meaning* in their mind into a single mental "gist" (the **context vector** $\mathbf{c}$), and then, one word at a time, **speaks out (Decodes)** the English translation back to you.

- The *mind-gist* is the fixed-size context vector.
- The *translator* is the encoder RNN/LSTM.
- The *speaking-out process* is the decoder RNN/LSTM generating output tokens autoregressively.
- If the sentence is too long, the *mind-gist* becomes fuzzy — this is exactly the **information bottleneck** problem of vanilla Seq2Seq models, and is the prime motivation for the **Attention Mechanism**.

### 1.3 Standard Notation & Constants

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value / Shape |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $T$ | Length of input sequence | Variable |
| $T'$ | Length of output sequence | Variable |
| $\mathbf{x}_t$ | One-hot or embedded input token at step $t$ | $\mathbb{R}^{d_x}$ |
| $\mathbf{h}_t$ | Encoder hidden state at step $t$ | $\mathbb{R}^{d_h}$ |
| $\mathbf{c}$ | Context (thought) vector | $\mathbb{R}^{d_h}$ |
| $\mathbf{s}_t$ | Decoder hidden state at step $t$ | $\mathbb{R}^{d_h}$ |
| $\mathbf{y}_t$ | Output token at step $t$ | $\mathbb{R}^{d_y}$ |
| $V$ | Output vocabulary size | $10^4$–$10^5$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The encoder-decoder framework is the *gateway* concept to Attention and Transformers (covered in the next module). Master this — almost every KTU question on sequence models builds on it.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Flow of a Vanilla Seq2Seq Model

The pipeline is split into three logical phases:

### Phase A — **Encoding (Comprehension)**
- The input tokens $\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2, \dots, \mathbf{x}_T$ are fed **one at a time** into the encoder RNN/LSTM/GRU.
- At every step, the hidden state is updated:

$$\mathbf{h}_t = f_{\text{enc}}(\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t)$$

- For LSTM, $f_{\text{enc}}$ encapsulates the **input, forget, output** gates and the **cell state** update.
- The final hidden state $\mathbf{h}_T$ is taken as the context vector $\mathbf{c}$ (or a non-linear transformation of it).

### Phase B — **Decoding (Generation)**
- The decoder is initialized with $\mathbf{s}_0 = g(\mathbf{c})$, where $g$ is typically a tanh layer.
- At each step $t$, it produces a hidden state and a probability distribution over the vocabulary:

$$\mathbf{s}_t = f_{\text{dec}}(\mathbf{s}_{t-1}, \mathbf{y}_{t-1}, \mathbf{c})$$

$$P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{x}) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\mathbf{W}_o \, \mathbf{s}_t\right)$$

### Phase C — **End-of-Sequence**
- A special **\<EOS\>** token is appended to every target sequence during training.
- The decoder stops generating when **\<EOS\>** is emitted, allowing variable-length outputs.

### 2.2 Training vs. Inference — The Teacher Forcing Trick

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Teacher Forcing (Williams & Zipser, 1989):** During training, the decoder is fed the **ground-truth previous token** $y_{t-1}^{\text{true}}$ rather than its own prediction $\hat{y}_{t-1}$. This stabilizes training and drastically accelerates convergence.

At inference time, teacher forcing is *not* available — the decoder must rely on its own previous predictions. This *exposure bias* is one of the main drawbacks of vanilla Seq2Seq.

### 2.3 The Information Bottleneck Problem

A single fixed-size vector $\mathbf{c} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h}$ must encode **all** semantic information of a potentially long input (e.g., a 50-word sentence). Empirical results show:
- Performance degrades sharply for $T > 20$–$30$.
- Gradient flow through $T$ time steps suffers from **vanishing/exploding gradients**, even with LSTM.

This bottleneck is the *theoretical justification* for the Attention Mechanism (Bahdanau et al., 2015).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Variables |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\mathbf{h}_t = f_{\text{enc}}(\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t)$ | Encoder recurrence | $f_{\text{enc}}$ = RNN/LSTM/GRU cell |
| 2 | $\mathbf{c} = \mathbf{h}_T$ | Context vector (vanilla) | Last encoder hidden state |
| 3 | $\mathbf{s}_0 = \tanh\!\left(\mathbf{W}_c \, \mathbf{c}\right)$ | Decoder initialization | $\mathbf{W}_c \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h \times d_h}$ |
| 4 | $\mathbf{s}_t = f_{\text{dec}}(\mathbf{s}_{t-1}, \mathbf{y}_{t-1}, \mathbf{c})$ | Decoder recurrence | Conditional on context |
| 5 | $P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{x}) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\mathbf{W}_o \mathbf{s}_t\right)$ | Output distribution | $\mathbf{W}_o \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert V \vert \times d_h}$ |
| 6 | $P(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{x}) = \prod_{t=1}^{T'} P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{x})$ | Joint likelihood | Chain rule factorization |
| 7 | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{t=1}^{T'} \log P(y_t^{\text{true}} \mid y_{<t}^{\text{true}}, \mathbf{x})$ | Cross-entropy loss | One term per target token |
| 8 | $\text{BLEU} = \text{BP} \cdot \exp\!\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)$ | BLEU evaluation score | $p_n$ = modified n-gram precision |
| 9 | $\mathbf{c}_i = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \alpha_{it} \, \mathbf{h}_t$ | Attention context (preview) | $\alpha_{it}$ = alignment weights |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Seq2Seq is the foundational paradigm behind **Google Translate (pre-Transformer era)**, **GNMT (Google Neural Machine Translation)**, **speech-to-text captioning**, **code generation (e.g., GitHub Copilot's older models)**, and **chatbot reply generation**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Joint Probability Factorization

The decoder generates tokens autoregressively. By the **chain rule of probability**, the joint distribution of the output sequence factorizes as a product of conditionals.

**Step 1 — Start with the joint probability.**

$$P(y_1, y_2, \dots, y_{T'} \mid \mathbf{x})$$

**Step 2 — Apply the chain rule.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(y_1, y_2, \dots, y_{T'} \mid \mathbf{x}) 
&= P(y_1 \mid \mathbf{x}) \cdot P(y_2 \mid y_1, \mathbf{x}) \cdot P(y_3 \mid y_1, y_2, \mathbf{x}) \cdots \\
&= \prod_{t=1}^{T'} P(y_t \mid y_1, y_2, \dots, y_{t-1}, \mathbf{x})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compact notation using $y_{<t}$.**

$$P(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{x}) = \prod_{t=1}^{T'} P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{x})$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the softmax output.**

$$
P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{x}) = \frac{\exp\!\left(\mathbf{W}_o^{(y_t)} \mathbf{s}_t\right)}{\sum_{k=1}^{\vert V \vert} \exp\!\left(\mathbf{W}_o^{(k)} \mathbf{s}_t\right)}
$$

**Interpretation:** The numerator is the score (logit) of the *true* word at step $t$, while the denominator sums scores across the entire vocabulary to normalize into a valid probability.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Training Loss (Cross-Entropy)

**Step 1 — Goal:** Maximize $P(\mathbf{y}^{\text{true}} \mid \mathbf{x})$ for each training example.

**Step 2 — Take the negative logarithm to obtain a minimization objective.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}(\theta) 
&= -\log P(\mathbf{y}^{\text{true}} \mid \mathbf{x}; \theta) \\
&= -\log \prod_{t=1}^{T'} P(y_t^{\text{true}} \mid y_{<t}^{\text{true}}, \mathbf{x}; \theta) \\
&= -\sum_{t=1}^{T'} \log P(y_t^{\text{true}} \mid y_{<t}^{\text{true}}, \mathbf{x}; \theta)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the softmax explicitly.**

$$
\mathcal{L}(\theta) = -\sum_{t=1}^{T'} \left[ \mathbf{W}_o^{(y_t^{\text{true}})} \mathbf{s}_t - \log \sum_{k=1}^{\vert V \vert} \exp\!\left(\mathbf{W}_o^{(k)} \mathbf{s}_t\right) \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Average over the minibatch of $N$ examples.**

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{batch}}(\theta) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}^{(n)}(\theta)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Cross-Entropy and not MSE?** Output tokens are discrete (one-hot-like distributions over a vocabulary of $\vert V \vert$ words). MSE assumes Gaussian targets and is mathematically ill-suited for categorical softmax outputs. Cross-entropy is the canonical loss for classification-style token generation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: LSTM Encoder Cell (Forward Pass)

For completeness, the encoder cell update (LSTM variant) is given by:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{i}_t &= \sigma\!\left(\mathbf{W}_i [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}; \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_i\right) \quad &\text{(Input gate)} \\
\mathbf{f}_t &= \sigma\!\left(\mathbf{W}_f [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}; \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_f\right) \quad &\text{(Forget gate)} \\
\mathbf{o}_t &= \sigma\!\left(\mathbf{W}_o [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}; \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_o\right) \quad &\text{(Output gate)} \\
\tilde{\mathbf{c}}_t &= \tanh\!\left(\mathbf{W}_c [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}; \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_c\right) \quad &\text{(Candidate)} \\
\mathbf{c}_t &= \mathbf{f}_t \odot \mathbf{c}_{t-1} + \mathbf{i}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{c}}_t \quad &\text{(Cell state)} \\
\mathbf{h}_t &= \mathbf{o}_t \odot \tanh(\mathbf{c}_t) \quad &\text{(Hidden state)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Here $[\mathbf{h}_{t-1}; \mathbf{x}_t]$ denotes concatenation and $\odot$ is the element-wise (Hadamard) product.

**Key insight:** The cell state $\mathbf{c}_t$ acts as a *long-term memory highway*, while the hidden state $\mathbf{h}_t$ is the *short-term working memory* passed to the next time step.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Training vs. Inference Comparison

| Aspect | Training Phase | Inference Phase |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Input to decoder at step $t$** | Ground-truth token $y_{t-1}^{\text{true}}$ (Teacher Forcing) | Model's own prediction $\hat{y}_{t-1}$ |
| **Loss used** | Cross-entropy $\mathcal{L}(\theta)$ | None (no labels) |
| **Stop criterion** | Fixed $T'$ (known) | Emission of \<EOS\> or max length |
| **Search strategy** | N/A (parallel across positions) | Greedy / Beam Search / Sampling |
| **Backpropagation** | **BPTT** (Backpropagation Through Time) | No weight updates |
| **Performance metric** | Perplexity, token-level accuracy | BLEU, ROUGE, METEOR |

---

## 3.5 Inference Decoding Strategies

### (a) Greedy Decoding
At each step, pick the token with the highest probability:

$$\hat{y}_t = \arg\max_{y} \, P(y \mid \hat{y}_{<t}, \mathbf{x})$$

**Pros:** Fast, deterministic. **Cons:** Myopic — a locally optimal token may lead to a globally poor sequence.

### (b) Beam Search (Width = $k$)
Maintain the top-$k$ partial hypotheses at every step. Score each by cumulative log-probability:

$$\text{score}(\mathbf{y}_{\le t}) = \sum_{i=1}^{t} \log P(y_i \mid y_{<i}, \mathbf{x})$$

**Pros:** Explores multiple futures, higher BLEU scores. **Cons:** $O(k \cdot \vert V \vert \cdot T')$ complexity, still may suffer from length bias (mitigated by *length normalization* $\div t^{\alpha}$).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Encoder-Decoder Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src["INPUT SEQUENCE X1 XT"]:::input
    encBox["ENCODER RNN OR LSTM OR GRU"]:::encoder
    cVec["CONTEXT VECTOR C EQUALS H_T"]:::context
    initLayer["DECODER INIT S0 EQUALS TANH W_C C"]:::init
    decBox["DECODER RNN OR LSTM OR GRU"]:::decoder
    outSeq["OUTPUT SEQUENCE Y1 Y_DASH T_DASH"]:::output
    eos["EOS STOP TOKEN"]:::endtoken

    src --> encBox
    encBox --> cVec
    cVec --> initLayer
    initLayer --> decBox
    decBox --> outSeq
    outSeq --> eos

    classDef input fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#0D47A1,stroke-width:2px
    classDef encoder fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#E65100,stroke-width:2px
    classDef context fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#880E4F,color:#880E4F,stroke-width:3px
    classDef init fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,color:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px
    classDef decoder fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px
    classDef output fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064,color:#006064,stroke-width:2px
    classDef endtoken fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.2 Unrolled Time-Step View of Seq2Seq

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ENC["ENCODER TIME STEPS"]
        direction LR
        e1["H1 = f(H0, X1)"]:::estep
        e2["H2 = f(H1, X2)"]:::estep
        e3["H3 = f(H2, X3)"]:::estep
        e4["H_T = CONTEXT C"]:::estepFinal
        e1 --> e2 --> e3 --> e4
    end

    subgraph DEC["DECODER TIME STEPS"]
        direction LR
        d0["S0 = INIT FROM C"]:::dstep
        d1["S1 = f(S0, EOS, C)"]:::dstep
        d2["S2 = f(S1, Y1, C)"]:::dstep
        d3["S3 = f(S2, Y2, C)"]:::dstep
        d4["OUTPUT Y3 EOS"]:::dstepFinal
        d0 --> d1 --> d2 --> d3 --> d4
    end

    e4 -.FEEDS CONTEXT.-> d0
    e4 -.FEEDS CONTEXT.-> d1
    e4 -.FEEDS CONTEXT.-> d2
    e4 -.FEEDS CONTEXT.-> d3

    classDef estep fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#000,stroke-width:1px
    classDef estepFinal fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef dstep fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000,stroke-width:1px
    classDef dstepFinal fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.3 Training vs. Inference Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TRAIN["TRAINING WITH TEACHER FORCING"]
        t1["GROUND TRUTH Y1"]:::train
        t2["GROUND TRUTH Y2"]:::train
        t3["GROUND TRUTH Y3"]:::train
        tdec["DECODER"]:::tdec
        tloss["CROSS ENTROPY LOSS"]:::loss
        t1 --> tdec
        t2 --> tdec
        t3 --> tdec
        tdec --> tloss
    end

    subgraph INFER["INFERENCE AUTOREGRESSIVE"]
        i0["EOS"]:::infer
        i1["PREDICTED Y1 HAT"]:::infer
        i2["PREDICTED Y2 HAT"]:::infer
        i3["PREDICTED Y3 HAT"]:::infer
        idec["DECODER"]:::idec
        ibeam["BEAM SEARCH OR GREEDY"]:::beam
        i0 --> idec
        idec --> ibeam
        ibeam --> i1
        i1 --> idec
        idec --> ibeam
        ibeam --> i2
        i2 --> idec
        idec --> ibeam
        ibeam --> i3
    end

    classDef train fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    classDef tdec fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef loss fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef infer fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef idec fill:#DCEDC8,stroke:#33691E,color:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef beam fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.4 The Bottleneck Problem Visualized

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1["VERY LONG INPUT SEQUENCE T = 80"]:::long
    L2["SINGLE CONTEXT VECTOR C IN R D_H"]:::bottle
    L3["DECODER STRUGGLES FOR LONG T"]:::output

    L1 ==>>|"INFORMATION OVERLOAD"| L2
    L2 ==>>|"GRADIENT FADES"| L3

    classDef long fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    classDef bottle fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000,stroke-width:4px
    classDef output fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Takeaway from the diagrams:** The context vector $\mathbf{c}$ is the *single point of contact* between encoder and decoder. Once you understand that, the *motivation for Attention* (one context vector *per decoder step*) becomes obvious.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** Define the *Encoder-Decoder Sequence-to-Sequence* architecture. List two real-world applications.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An **Encoder-Decoder Seq2Seq** model is a neural architecture comprising two recurrent networks:
- **Encoder:** Reads the input sequence $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, \dots, x_T)$ and compresses it into a fixed-dimensional context vector $\mathbf{c} = \mathbf{h}_T$.
- **Decoder:** Generates the output sequence $\mathbf{y} = (y_1, \dots, y_{T'})$ one token at a time, conditioned on $\mathbf{c}$ and previously generated tokens.

**Applications:**
1. **Machine Translation** (e.g., English → Malayalam).
2. **Automatic Text Summarization** (long document → short abstract).
3. *(Optional third)* Speech Recognition, Image Captioning, Conversational Chatbots.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for explaining encoder/decoder roles, 1 mark for two applications.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** What is *Teacher Forcing*? Why is it used only during training and not during inference?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Teacher Forcing** is a training strategy in which the decoder receives the **ground-truth previous token** $y_{t-1}^{\text{true}}$ as input instead of its own prediction $\hat{y}_{t-1}$.

**Why during training:** It stabilizes training, prevents error accumulation, and allows efficient parallel computation of the loss across all time steps (since all inputs are known).

**Why NOT during inference:** At inference, ground-truth tokens do not exist — the model must operate autoregressively, using its own predictions to generate the next token. This mismatch is called the **exposure bias** problem.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for training benefit, 1 mark for inference limitation.)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the joint probability $P(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{x})$ for a Seq2Seq model in terms of the decoder's softmax outputs. Clearly state all intermediate steps. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the *Information Bottleneck* problem of vanilla Seq2Seq models. How does the Attention mechanism conceptually resolve it? **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Q.A(a)

**Goal:** Express $P(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{x})$ as a product of conditional distributions, then substitute the decoder's softmax.

**Step 1 — Joint distribution by chain rule.**

$$
P(y_1, y_2, \dots, y_{T'} \mid \mathbf{x}) = \prod_{t=1}^{T'} P(y_t \mid y_1, \dots, y_{t-1}, \mathbf{x})
$$

**Step 2 — Compact notation:** $y_{<t} = (y_1, \dots, y_{t-1})$.

$$
P(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{x}) = \prod_{t=1}^{T'} P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{x})
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute decoder hidden-state recurrence.**

The decoder computes:
$$\mathbf{s}_t = f_{\text{dec}}(\mathbf{s}_{t-1}, y_{t-1}, \mathbf{c})$$

**Step 4 — Apply softmax over vocabulary $V$.**

$$
P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{x}) = \frac{\exp\!\left(\mathbf{W}_o^{(y_t)} \mathbf{s}_t\right)}{\sum_{v \in V} \exp\!\left(\mathbf{W}_o^{(v)} \mathbf{s}_t\right)}
$$

**Step 5 — Final joint form.**

$$
\boxed{\,P(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{x}) = \prod_{t=1}^{T'} \frac{\exp\!\left(\mathbf{W}_o^{(y_t)} \mathbf{s}_t\right)}{\sum_{v \in V} \exp\!\left(\mathbf{W}_o^{(v)} \mathbf{s}_t\right)}\,}
$$

*(1 mark each for steps 1–4, 2 marks for the final assembled equation.)*

---

### Model Solution — Q.A(b)

**Information Bottleneck Problem:**

In a vanilla Seq2Seq model, the **entire** input sequence is compressed into a **single fixed-dimensional** context vector $\mathbf{c} = \mathbf{h}_T \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h}$. This creates a severe bottleneck because:

1. **Capacity limit:** $d_h$ is fixed (e.g., 256 or 512), but the input may carry arbitrarily more information (e.g., a 100-word sentence).
2. **Long-range dependencies:** Earlier tokens' gradients must traverse $T$ time steps via BPTT, suffering from vanishing gradients.
3. **Empirical degradation:** BLEU scores drop sharply for $T > 30$.

**How Attention Resolves It:**

Instead of a single $\mathbf{c}$, attention computes a **distinct context vector $\mathbf{c}_i$ for every decoder step $i$**:

$$\mathbf{c}_i = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \alpha_{it} \, \mathbf{h}_t$$

where the **alignment weights** $\alpha_{it}$ are learned dynamically:

$$\alpha_{it} = \frac{\exp(e_{it})}{\sum_{k=1}^{T} \exp(e_{ik})}, \quad e_{it} = \text{score}(\mathbf{s}_{i-1}, \mathbf{h}_t)$$

This:
- **Removes the bottleneck** — every encoder state is preserved, not collapsed.
- **Enables soft alignment** — the decoder learns *which* source words to focus on at each output step.
- **Shortens gradient paths** — direct connections between encoder states and decoder steps alleviate vanishing gradients.

*(2 marks for bottleneck explanation, 2 marks for the three sub-points, 2 marks for attention formula, 1 mark for overall benefits.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Describe the architecture of an LSTM-based Encoder-Decoder model for Neural Machine Translation. Include all relevant equations for encoder, context vector, decoder, and output probability. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Greedy Decoding** and **Beam Search** for inference in Seq2Seq models. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Q.B(a)

**Architecture:** A neural machine translation (NMT) system that maps a source sentence $(x_1, \dots, x_T)$ to a target sentence $(y_1, \dots, y_{T'})$ using two LSTMs.

**Step 1 — Encoder (LSTM).**

For each input embedding $\mathbf{e}_t = \mathbf{E} x_t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{i}_t &= \sigma(\mathbf{W}_i [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}; \mathbf{e}_t] + \mathbf{b}_i) \\
\mathbf{f}_t &= \sigma(\mathbf{W}_f [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}; \mathbf{e}_t] + \mathbf{b}_f) \\
\mathbf{o}_t &= \sigma(\mathbf{W}_o [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}; \mathbf{e}_t] + \mathbf{b}_o) \\
\tilde{\mathbf{c}}_t &= \tanh(\mathbf{W}_c [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}; \mathbf{e}_t] + \mathbf{b}_c) \\
\mathbf{c}_t &= \mathbf{f}_t \odot \mathbf{c}_{t-1} + \mathbf{i}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{c}}_t \\
\mathbf{h}_t &= \mathbf{o}_t \odot \tanh(\mathbf{c}_t)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Context Vector.**

$$\mathbf{c} = \mathbf{h}_T$$

**Step 3 — Decoder Initialization.**

$$\mathbf{s}_0 = \tanh(\mathbf{W}_s \, \mathbf{c})$$

**Step 4 — Decoder (LSTM), conditioned on $\mathbf{c}$.**

$$\mathbf{s}_t = \text{LSTM}_{\text{dec}}(\mathbf{s}_{t-1}, [\mathbf{E} y_{t-1}; \mathbf{c}])$$

i.e., the previous target embedding and the context vector are concatenated as input to the decoder cell.

**Step 5 — Output Probability.**

$$P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{x}) = \text{softmax}(\mathbf{W}_o \mathbf{s}_t + \mathbf{b}_o)$$

**Step 6 — Generation ends with the \<EOS\> token.**

*(1 mark for each step — 6 steps for full marks.)*

---

### Model Solution — Q.B(b)

| Aspect | Greedy Decoding | Beam Search (width $k$) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Selection rule** | $\hat{y}_t = \arg\max_y P(y \mid \hat{y}_{<t}, \mathbf{x})$ | Keep top-$k$ partial sequences at each step |
| **Search space** | $O(\vert V \vert T')$ | $O(k \vert V \vert T')$ |
| **Myopic?** | Yes — locally optimal only | Partially mitigated by maintaining $k$ alternatives |
| **Determinism** | Deterministic | Deterministic (for fixed $k$) |
| **Best use case** | Real-time / low-latency apps | Translation, summarization (accuracy-critical) |

**Greedy — Advantage:** Fast, $O(1)$ memory per step. **Disadvantage:** Cannot recover from early mistakes; may produce ungrammatical sequences.

**Beam Search — Advantage:** Higher BLEU scores, explores promising alternatives. **Disadvantage:** Computationally expensive, may suffer *length bias* (over-preference for short sequences), and is still vulnerable to label bias.

**Length Normalization (essential fix for Beam Search):**
$$\text{score}_{\text{norm}}(\mathbf{y}) = \frac{1}{t^{\alpha}} \sum_{i=1}^{t} \log P(y_i \mid y_{<i}, \mathbf{x}), \quad 0 \le \alpha \le 1$$

*(1 mark for each row of comparison, 1 mark for advantages, 1 mark for disadvantages, 1 mark for length normalization, 1 mark for trade-off discussion.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Deduction Traps:**
> 1. **Forgetting the `c` input in the decoder recurrence.** Many students write $\mathbf{s}_t = f(\mathbf{s}_{t-1}, y_{t-1})$ and lose 2 marks. *Always* show $\mathbf{c}$ being fed at every step.
> 2. **Conflating the LSTM cell state $\mathbf{c}_t$ with the context vector $\mathbf{c}$.** Use different symbols (e.g., $\mathbf{C}_t$ for cell state, $\mathbf{c}$ for context) to avoid ambiguity.
> 3. **Writing softmax without the denominator.** The full form $\frac{\exp(z_i)}{\sum_j \exp(z_j)}$ is required — a partial expression loses a mark.
> 4. **Confusing teacher forcing with curriculum learning.** They are different concepts; teacher forcing is input-side, not loss-side.
> 5. **Not stating the cross-entropy loss explicitly when asked about training.** The KTU rubric specifically awards marks for the negative-log-likelihood form.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Seq2Seq = Encoder + Decoder**, connected through a context vector $\mathbf{c} = \mathbf{h}_T$.
- The **decoder is autoregressive** — each step depends on the previous output and $\mathbf{c}$.
- **Training loss = cross-entropy** summed over target tokens.
- **Teacher Forcing** feeds ground-truth tokens during training, not inference.
- **Beam Search** outperforms Greedy decoding for accuracy-critical tasks like NMT.
- **Vanilla Seq2Seq has a bottleneck** because one $\mathbf{c}$ must encode everything → motivates **Attention**.
- The decoder **LSTM cell state** $\mathbf{C}_t$ ≠ **context vector** $\mathbf{c}$ — keep notation distinct.
- **BLEU** is the standard automatic evaluation metric for NMT outputs.
- Seq2Seq is the **conceptual predecessor of the Transformer** (which drops recurrence for self-attention).
- For long sequences, vanilla Seq2Seq fails — this is the *most-tested* KTU concept on this topic.
- **BPTT (Backpropagation Through Time)** is the training algorithm for the recurrent parts.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deep RNNs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET753 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Deep RNNs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Deep Recurrent Neural Network (Deep RNN)** is a multi-layered extension of the classical recurrent neural network in which the recurrent hidden state computation is stacked vertically across **L ≥ 2** distinct hidden layers, where each layer maintains its own recurrent state over the temporal axis, and the output of one layer feeds as input into the next layer for the same time step. Formally, for layer *l* at time step *t*, the hidden state is computed as a parameterized non-linear function of the current input, the previous hidden state of the same layer, and the hidden state of the layer below at the current time step.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Deep RNNs are explicitly distinguished from *stacked RNNs* and *bidirectional RNNs* in the KTU 2024 scheme. The defining criterion is **depth in the recurrent axis (temporal)** as well as **depth in the layer axis (spatial)**, typically realized by unrolling the network through both dimensions.

The key parameters associated with a Deep RNN are summarized below:

- **Hidden Layers (L):** The number of stacked recurrent layers. A *shallow* RNN uses **L = 1**; a *deep* RNN uses **L ≥ 2**.
- **Hidden Units (n):** The dimensionality of each hidden state vector $h_t^{(l)} \in \mathbb{R}^n$.
- **Input Sequence Length (T):** The number of time steps over which the network is unrolled.
- **Parameter Sharing:** Weights $W^{(l)}$ and $U^{(l)}$ are shared across all time steps *t* within the same layer *l*, but **not** shared across layers.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine reading a complex novel paragraph by paragraph. A **shallow RNN** is like a single reader who tries to remember every detail of the story in just one mental notebook. As the story grows, the notebook becomes cluttered and the reader forgets earlier events.

A **Deep RNN**, on the other hand, is like a team of readers arranged in a hierarchy. The **first reader** focuses on raw words and basic grammar. They pass a high-level summary to the **second reader**, who interprets themes and character emotions. The **third reader** synthesizes plot twists and long-term arcs. Each reader maintains their *own* short-term memory (recurrent state) while also receiving refined context from the layer below. The information is progressively abstracted — just as deep CNNs build hierarchical features (edges → shapes → objects), Deep RNNs build hierarchical temporal features (phonemes → syllables → words → semantics).

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Takeaway:** *Shallow RNN = 1 layer of memory. Deep RNN = multiple layers of memory, where each layer handles a different level of temporal abstraction.*

## 1.3 Why Depth Matters in Recurrent Networks

Theoretically, a single-layer RNN with sufficient hidden units is a universal approximator for any sequence-to-sequence mapping (as proven by Schäfer & Zimmermann, 2006). However, in practice, this requires an **exponentially large** number of hidden units for long sequences. Stacking recurrent layers achieves the same expressive power with a **polynomially smaller** parameter budget, similar to the depth-efficiency trade-off in deep feedforward networks.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Heuristic:** Empirically, going from **L = 1** to **L = 4** recurrent layers often yields a substantial gain in perplexity (language modeling) or BLEU (translation). Beyond **L = 4**, gains diminish rapidly and vanishing-gradient pathologies worsen.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture Decomposition

A Deep RNN unrolls along **two axes simultaneously**: the **temporal axis** (left-to-right time flow) and the **depth axis** (bottom-to-top layer stacking). For an *L*-layer network processing an input sequence $\{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_T\}$ of length *T*:

- **Layer 1 (Bottom):** Receives the raw input $x_t$ at every time step. Maintains hidden state $h_t^{(1)}$.
- **Layer l (Middle, 1 < l < L):** Receives the hidden state $h_t^{(l-1)}$ from the layer below at time *t*, and its own previous state $h_{t-1}^{(l)}$. Computes new state $h_t^{(l)}$.
- **Layer L (Top):** Receives $h_t^{(L-1)}$ from below. Its hidden state $h_t^{(L)}$ is typically fed to an output layer (e.g., softmax classifier or next-token predictor).

The **output** at time step *t* may depend only on the top layer's hidden state $h_t^{(L)}$, or on a combination of all layers' hidden states depending on the task.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Forward Propagation Logic

The forward pass of a Deep RNN is computed layer-by-layer, time-step-by-time-step:

- **Step 1 — Input Embedding:** The raw input token $x_t$ is mapped to a dense vector via an embedding matrix $E$, yielding $e_t = E \cdot x_t$.
- **Step 2 — Layer 1 Hidden Update:** Compute the bottom-layer hidden state using the current input embedding and the previous bottom-layer hidden state.
- **Step 3 — Vertical Propagation:** The hidden state $h_t^{(l)}$ from layer *l* becomes the input to layer *l + 1* at the same time step *t*.
- **Step 4 — Recurrent Update Per Layer:** For each subsequent layer, the hidden state is a function of its own previous state and the current output of the layer immediately below.
- **Step 5 — Output Projection:** At the top layer, the final hidden state is passed through an output weight matrix and a softmax (or task-specific head) to produce predictions $\hat{y}_t$.
- **Step 6 — Loss Computation:** A per-time-step loss $\mathcal{L}_t$ is computed and summed (or averaged) across all time steps to yield the total sequence loss.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol** | **Meaning** | **Mathematical Form** | **Shape / Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $h_t^{(l)}$ | Hidden state of layer $l$ at time $t$ | Activation of affine combination | $\mathbb{R}^{n_l}$ |
| $U^{(l)}$ | Recurrent weight matrix in layer $l$ | Shared across all time steps of layer $l$ | $n_l \times n_l$ |
| $W^{(l)}$ | Input-to-hidden weight matrix in layer $l$ | Maps input from layer $l-1$ | $n_l \times n_{l-1}$ |
| $b^{(l)}$ | Bias vector of layer $l$ | Additive bias | $\mathbb{R}^{n_l}$ |
| $V$ | Output projection matrix | Maps top-layer state to output | $\vert V \vert \in \mathbb{R}^{K \times n_L}$ |
| $o_t$ | Output vector at time $t$ | $V h_t^{(L)} + c$ | $\mathbb{R}^{K}$ |
| $\hat{y}_t$ | Softmax probability over $K$ classes | $\text{softmax}(o_t)$ | $\mathbb{R}^{K}$, sums to 1 |
| $\mathcal{L}$ | Total sequence loss | $\sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathcal{L}_t(\hat{y}_t, y_t)$ | Scalar |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Note:** The matrix $U^{(l)}$ is square and **shared** across time within layer $l$ but is **different** for each layer. The cross-layer weights $W^{(l)}$ are **not** recurrent — they are feedforward projections from layer $l-1$ to layer $l$ at the same time step.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Deep RNNs underpin several industrial-grade applications:

- **Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR):** Deep stacked LSTMs (e.g., the 5-layer LSTM acoustic model in Google's early end-to-end ASR pipeline) translate acoustic frames into phonetic and linguistic representations.
- **Machine Translation:** Encoder–decoder architectures with 4-layer stacked LSTMs/GRUs form the backbone of pre-Transformer NMT systems.
- **Music Generation:** Deep RNNs model multi-scale temporal structure in audio (note → measure → phrase).
- **Financial Time-Series Forecasting:** Stacked recurrent layers capture both short-term volatility and long-term trend components.
- **Biomedical Signal Processing:** EEG and ECG analysis leverages multi-layer RNNs to model multi-frequency temporal dependencies.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When asked *"Why is depth beneficial in RNNs?"*, the model answer must include: (1) **hierarchical temporal feature learning**, (2) **parameter efficiency vs. width**, and (3) **easier optimization of long-range dependencies** when paired with LSTM/GRU cells.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Forward Pass Derivation

Consider an *L*-layer Deep RNN with $\tanh$ activations. The full forward pass is given by the following set of coupled recurrences:

For the **first (bottom) layer** at time step $t$:

$$
a_t^{(1)} = W^{(1)} x_t + U^{(1)} h_{t-1}^{(1)} + b^{(1)}
$$

$$
h_t^{(1)} = \tanh\!\left(a_t^{(1)}\right)
$$

For **intermediate layers** $l = 2, 3, \ldots, L-1$ at time step $t$:

$$
a_t^{(l)} = W^{(l)} h_t^{(l-1)} + U^{(l)} h_{t-1}^{(l)} + b^{(l)}
$$

$$
h_t^{(l)} = \tanh\!\left(a_t^{(l)}\right)
$$

For the **top layer** $l = L$, the hidden state is projected to the output:

$$
o_t = V h_t^{(L)} + c
$$

$$
\hat{y}_t = \text{softmax}(o_t) = \frac{\exp(o_{t,k})}{\sum_{k'=1}^{K} \exp(o_{t,k'})}
$$

**Conversion Logic:**
- $W^{(1)} x_t$ transforms the raw input embedding into the same dimensionality as the first hidden state.
- $U^{(1)} h_{t-1}^{(1)}$ injects the recurrent memory from the previous time step of the **same** layer.
- $W^{(l)} h_t^{(l-1)}$ is the vertical (depth-axis) signal coming from the layer immediately below at the **current** time step.
- $U^{(l)} h_{t-1}^{(l)}$ is the horizontal (time-axis) signal coming from the previous time step of the same layer.
- The $\tanh$ squashes the pre-activation to the range $(-1, +1)$.

## 3.2 Loss Function (Cross-Entropy Over Time)

For a sequence of length $T$ with ground-truth labels $y_t$:

$$
\mathcal{L}_t(\hat{y}_t, y_t) = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} y_{t,k} \log \hat{y}_{t,k}
$$

$$
\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathcal{L}_t(\hat{y}_t, y_t)
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The per-time-step loss is the standard categorical cross-entropy. The total loss is the sum (or mean, in practice) across all time steps. This is what we minimize with respect to the union of all weight matrices $\theta = \{W^{(1:L)}, U^{(1:L)}, b^{(1:L)}, V, c\}$.

## 3.3 Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) in a Deep RNN

The gradient of the loss with respect to the top-layer output projection matrix $V$ is computed first, as it has no recurrence. With cross-entropy + softmax, the gradient simplifies elegantly to the **prediction error** $\delta_t^o$:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial o_t} = \hat{y}_t - y_t \;\equiv\; \delta_t^o
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial V} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \delta_t^o \, \left(h_t^{(L)}\right)^{\top}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial c} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \delta_t^o
$$

For the **hidden layers**, gradients flow through **three pathways** in a Deep RNN — horizontally (through $U^{(l)}$), vertically (through $W^{(l+1)}$), and from future time steps (through $U^{(l+1)}$). The total gradient signal arriving at layer $l$ at time $t$ is:

$$
\delta_t^{(l)} \triangleq \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial a_t^{(l)}} = \left(1 - \left(h_t^{(l)}\right)^2\right) \odot \left[ \left(W^{(l+1)}\right)^{\top} \delta_t^{(l+1)} + \left(U^{(l)}\right)^{\top} \delta_{t+1}^{(l)} \right]
$$

where $\odot$ denotes element-wise (Hadamard) multiplication, and for the **top layer** $l = L$:

$$
\delta_t^{(L)} = \left(1 - \left(h_t^{(L)}\right)^2\right) \odot \left[ V^{\top} \delta_t^o + \left(U^{(L)}\right)^{\top} \delta_{t+1}^{(L)} \right]
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The derivative of $\tanh$ is $(1 - \tanh^2(z))$. The term $(W^{(l+1)})^{\top} \delta_t^{(l+1)}$ propagates the error **vertically downward** from the layer above at the same time step. The term $(U^{(l)})^{\top} \delta_{t+1}^{(l)}$ propagates the error **horizontally backward in time** from the next time step of the same layer.

The gradients with respect to the layer-wise parameters are:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(l)}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \delta_t^{(l)} \left(h_t^{(l-1)}\right)^{\top}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial U^{(l)}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \delta_t^{(l)} \left(h_{t-1}^{(l)}\right)^{\top}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(l)}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \delta_t^{(l)}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The parameter gradients are the *outer product* of the local error signal with the input that was fed to that parameter. The summation over $t$ aggregates the contribution from every time step (since each $W^{(l)}$ and $U^{(l)}$ is *used* at every time step, due to parameter sharing).

## 3.4 Vanishing / Exploding Gradient Diagnostic

The magnitude of the gradient flowing through time in layer $l$ is governed by the product of Jacobian matrices:

$$
\prod_{k=0}^{t-1} \frac{\partial h_{t}^{(l)}}{\partial h_{t-k}^{(l)}} = \prod_{k=0}^{t-1} \text{diag}\!\left(1 - \left(h_{t-k}^{(l)}\right)^2\right) \left(U^{(l)}\right)^{\top}
$$

If the spectral radius (largest absolute eigenvalue) of $U^{(l)}$ is:

- **$\rho(U^{(l)}) < 1$:** The product shrinks **exponentially** as $t$ grows → **vanishing gradient**.
- **$\rho(U^{(l)}) > 1$:** The product explodes **exponentially** → **exploding gradient**.

**Conversion Logic:** This is why a Deep RNN is *even more* vulnerable to gradient pathologies than a shallow RNN: the depth axis multiplies the recurrence, compounding the problem. This is the primary motivation for LSTM, GRU, orthogonal initialization, and gradient clipping.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Recurrent Architectures

| **Property** | **Shallow RNN** | **Deep (Stacked) RNN** | **Bidirectional RNN** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of recurrent layers $L$ | 1 | $\geq 2$ | 1 (with forward + backward pass) |
| Temporal direction | Unidirectional | Unidirectional | Both forward and backward |
| Parameter count (approx.) | $\mathcal{O}(n^2 + nd)$ | $\mathcal{O}(\sum_l n_l^2 + n_l n_{l-1})$ | $\approx 2\times$ shallow RNN |
| Captures long-range past context | Yes (with difficulty) | Yes (with hierarchical abstraction) | Yes (uses future context too) |
| Suitable for streaming inference | Yes | Yes | No (requires full sequence) |
| Typical use case | Toy sequence tasks | ASR, NMT, language modeling | POS tagging, NER, speech phoneme classification |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Unrolled Deep RNN Architecture (3 Layers × 4 Time Steps)

The diagram below shows a Deep RNN with **3 hidden layers** ($L = 3$) unrolled across **4 time steps** ($T = 4$). Each row represents a layer, and each column represents a time step. Vertical arrows denote feedforward cross-layer connections; horizontal arrows denote recurrent connections within the same layer.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    x1[Input t1] --> l1a
    x2[Input t2] --> l1b
    x3[Input t3] --> l1c
    x4[Input t4] --> l1d

    l1a[Layer 1 h1] --> l1b[Layer 1 h2]
    l1b --> l1c[Layer 1 h3]
    l1c --> l1d[Layer 1 h4]

    l1a --> l2a[Layer 2 h1]
    l1b --> l2b[Layer 2 h2]
    l1c --> l2c[Layer 2 h3]
    l1d --> l2d[Layer 2 h4]

    l2a --> l2b
    l2b --> l2c
    l2c --> l2d

    l2a --> l3a[Layer 3 h1]
    l2b --> l3b[Layer 3 h2]
    l2c --> l3c[Layer 3 h3]
    l2d --> l3d[Layer 3 h4]

    l3a --> l3b
    l3b --> l3c
    l3c --> l3d

    l3a --> y1[Output t1]
    l3b --> y2[Output t2]
    l3c --> y3[Output t3]
    l3d --> y4[Output t4]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **bottom row** (Layer 1) consumes the raw input $x_t$ at each time step and maintains its own recurrent state.
- The **middle row** (Layer 2) consumes the Layer 1 hidden states and maintains a separate, more abstract recurrent state.
- The **top row** (Layer 3) is the deepest abstraction layer, whose hidden states are projected to the outputs $\hat{y}_t$.
- All four rows form a **3-layer Deep RNN unrolled for 4 time steps**, total of $3 \times 4 = 12$ hidden state nodes.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following block diagram illustrates the *functional* information flow inside a single time step of a Deep RNN, including parameter routing and gradient pathways during BPTT.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph STEP[Single Time Step t in Deep RNN]
        direction TB
        X[Input x_t or h_t from layer l minus 1] --> EMB[Linear Projection W to the power l]
        H_PREV[Hidden State h of t minus 1 from layer l] --> REC[Recurrent Projection U to the power l]
        EMB --> ADD[Addition plus bias b to the power l]
        REC --> ADD
        ADD --> ACT[tanh Activation]
        ACT --> H_NOW[New Hidden State h to the power l of t]
        H_NOW --> NEXT_LAYER[Output to layer l plus 1 OR output head]
    end

    subgraph GRAD[BPTT Gradient Pathways]
        direction TB
        G_OUT[Gradient from above layer and future time step] --> G_VERT[Vertical Error Path W to the power l plus 1 transpose]
        G_OUT --> G_HORIZ[Horizontal Error Path U to the power l transpose]
        G_VERT --> G_PARAM[Gradient with respect to W to the power l and U to the power l]
        G_HORIZ --> G_PARAM
    end
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The top sub-graph shows the **forward pass**: input + previous hidden state → affine combination → $\tanh$ → new hidden state.
- The bottom sub-graph shows the **backward pass**: errors arrive from the layer above (vertical) and from the next time step (horizontal), and are combined to compute parameter gradients.

## 4.3 Compact vs. Rolled Representation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph COMPACT[Compact Rolled View]
        direction TB
        X_IN[Input Sequence] --> H1C[Hidden Layer 1 with Recurrent Loop]
        H1C --> H2C[Hidden Layer 2 with Recurrent Loop]
        H2C --> H3C[Hidden Layer 3 with Recurrent Loop]
        H3C --> Y_OUT[Output Sequence]
        H1C -. self loop .-> H1C
        H2C -. self loop .-> H2C
        H3C -. self loop .-> H3C
    end
```

**Reading the Diagram:** This compact view emphasizes that each layer maintains its **own** recurrent self-loop. Although the unrolled view (Section 4.1) is what we implement in code (since backpropagation requires the full unrolled computation graph), the compact view is the canonical *architectural* representation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]
**Differentiate between a shallow RNN and a Deep RNN. List any two advantages of using a deep architecture in sequence modeling.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Shallow RNN** | **Deep RNN** |
|---|---|---|
| Number of hidden recurrent layers | One ($L = 1$) | Two or more ($L \geq 2$) |
| Hierarchical temporal abstraction | Absent — single level of memory | Present — each layer captures different temporal scales |

**Two advantages of depth (any two for 2 marks):**

1. **Hierarchical Feature Learning:** Lower layers capture local, short-term patterns (e.g., character-level features); higher layers capture long-term, abstract semantics (e.g., sentence-level meaning).
2. **Parameter Efficiency:** A deep network with moderate width per layer achieves the same representational capacity as a much wider shallow network, with fewer total parameters and better generalization.

*(Third valid advantage: Easier optimization of long-range dependencies when paired with LSTM/GRU cells, since depth breaks the recurrence chain into shorter effective segments.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]
**What is meant by "parameter sharing" in the context of a Deep RNN? Why is it computationally important?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):** Parameter sharing in a Deep RNN means that the weight matrices $W^{(l)}$, $U^{(l)}$, and $b^{(l)}$ of each layer $l$ are **reused at every time step** of that layer's recurrent computation. The same $U^{(l)}$ multiplies $h_{t-1}^{(l)}$ for $t = 1, 2, \ldots, T$.

**Importance (1.5 Marks):**

- **Variable-Length Sequence Handling:** A single trained network can process sequences of any length $T$ because the same parameters are applied at every step. Without sharing, we would need a different set of weights for every time step, which is infeasible.
- **Statistical Efficiency & Generalization:** Sharing dramatically reduces the number of free parameters, allowing the model to generalize across positions in the sequence. The model is forced to discover *position-invariant* temporal patterns.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Sample Model Paper, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a Deep Recurrent Neural Network with a neat diagram. Clearly state the equations for the forward pass across all $L$ layers and $T$ time steps. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the gradient of the total loss $\mathcal{L}$ with respect to the output projection matrix $V$ and the recurrent weight matrix $U^{(l)}$ of an intermediate layer $l$. Mention the three pathways through which gradient flows back to an intermediate hidden state. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a) — 7 Marks

**Architecture (3 Marks):** A Deep RNN is built by stacking $L$ recurrent hidden layers vertically. The first layer receives the input $x_t$ at each time step $t = 1, \ldots, T$. Each layer $l$ maintains its own hidden state $h_t^{(l)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_l}$ and propagates it both forward in time (within the same layer) and upward in depth (to the next layer at the same time step). The top layer's hidden state $h_t^{(L)}$ is projected to the output $\hat{y}_t$ via the weight matrix $V$.

*Refer to Section 4.1 for the diagram (3 marks for diagram + 1 mark for explanation).*

**Forward Pass Equations (4 Marks):**

For the **bottom layer** $l = 1$:

$$
a_t^{(1)} = W^{(1)} x_t + U^{(1)} h_{t-1}^{(1)} + b^{(1)}; \quad h_t^{(1)} = \tanh\!\left(a_t^{(1)}\right)
$$

For **intermediate and top layers** $l = 2, \ldots, L$:

$$
a_t^{(l)} = W^{(l)} h_t^{(l-1)} + U^{(l)} h_{t-1}^{(l)} + b^{(l)}; \quad h_t^{(l)} = \tanh\!\left(a_t^{(l)}\right)
$$

**Output projection:**

$$
o_t = V h_t^{(L)} + c; \quad \hat{y}_t = \text{softmax}(o_t)
$$

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b) — 7 Marks

**Gradient w.r.t. $V$ (2 Marks):** From the cross-entropy + softmax combination, the gradient of $\mathcal{L}$ w.r.t. the pre-softmax output $o_t$ is $\delta_t^o = \hat{y}_t - y_t$. Therefore:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial V} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \left(\hat{y}_t - y_t\right) \left(h_t^{(L)}\right)^{\top} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \delta_t^o \left(h_t^{(L)}\right)^{\top}
$$

**Gradient w.r.t. $U^{(l)}$ (3 Marks):** The chain rule applied through the $\tanh$ activation and the recurrent connection gives:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial U^{(l)}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \delta_t^{(l)} \left(h_{t-1}^{(l)}\right)^{\top}
$$

where $\delta_t^{(l)}$ is the local error signal at layer $l$, time $t$, defined by:

$$
\delta_t^{(l)} = \left(1 - \left(h_t^{(l)}\right)^2\right) \odot \left[ \left(W^{(l+1)}\right)^{\top} \delta_t^{(l+1)} + \left(U^{(l)}\right)^{\top} \delta_{t+1}^{(l)} \right]
$$

**Three Gradient Pathways (2 Marks):** The error $\delta_t^{(l)}$ for an intermediate layer $l$ receives contributions from:

1. **The layer above (vertical pathway):** $\left(W^{(l+1)}\right)^{\top} \delta_t^{(l+1)}$ — error flowing down from layer $l+1$ at the same time step.
2. **The next time step (horizontal pathway):** $\left(U^{(l)}\right)^{\top} \delta_{t+1}^{(l)}$ — error flowing backward in time within the same layer.
3. **The top-layer output pathway (top-down pathway):** For $l = L$, an additional contribution $V^{\top} \delta_t^o$ from the output projection.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall #1:**
