> Many students incorrectly write $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial U^{(l)}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \delta_t^{(l)} \left(h_t^{(l)}\right)^{\top}$, using the *current* hidden state instead of the *previous* one. Since $U^{(l)}$ multiplies $h_{t-1}^{(l)}$ in the forward pass, the gradient must use $h_{t-1}^{(l)}$, not $h_t^{(l)}$. **This is a 1-mark deduction for sure.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Sample Model Paper, Module 4, Alternative Set]**

**(a)** With a suitable block diagram, explain the information flow in a Deep RNN during the **forward pass** for a single time step. Clearly distinguish the roles of $W^{(l)}$, $U^{(l)}$, and $b^{(l)}$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **vanishing and exploding gradient problem** in Deep RNNs. Show mathematically why depth in the recurrent axis worsens this problem, and list **three standard remedies** with one-line justifications. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram (3 Marks):** *Refer to the Functional Architecture Flow in Section 4.2.* The diagram should show: (i) the input $h_t^{(l-1)}$ entering a linear projection $W^{(l)}$, (ii) the previous hidden state $h_{t-1}^{(l)}$ entering a recurrent projection $U^{(l)}$, (iii) both being summed with bias $b^{(l)}$, (iv) the result passed through $\tanh$ to yield $h_t^{(l)}$, and (v) $h_t^{(l)}$ being sent both to layer $l+1$ (vertically) and to time step $t+1$ of the same layer (horizontally via storage).

**Role of Each Parameter (4 Marks):**

- **$W^{(l)}$ — Cross-Layer (Vertical) Projection:** Maps the hidden state of the layer below, $h_t^{(l-1)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_{l-1}}$, into the input space of layer $l$, $\mathbb{R}^{n_l}$. It encodes the *feedforward* information pathway between layers at the **same** time step. It is **not** recurrent.
- **$U^{(l)}$ — Intra-Layer (Horizontal/Recurrent) Projection:** Maps the previous hidden state of the same layer, $h_{t-1}^{(l)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_l}$, into the new hidden state. It encodes the *temporal* memory pathway. It is **shared** across all time steps.
- **$b^{(l)}$ — Bias Vector:** Provides an additive shift to the pre-activation, allowing the activation function to operate in a non-centered regime. Shape: $\mathbb{R}^{n_l}$.

The combined affine combination is $a_t^{(l)} = W^{(l)} h_t^{(l-1)} + U^{(l)} h_{t-1}^{(l)} + b^{(l)}$, followed by $h_t^{(l)} = \tanh(a_t^{(l)})$.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b) — 7 Marks

**Mathematical Reason (3 Marks):** During BPTT, the gradient flowing backward in time through layer $l$ involves a product of Jacobians over $T$ time steps. The magnitude is governed by:

$$
\left\| \prod_{k=0}^{T-1} \text{diag}\!\left(1 - \left(h_{T-k}^{(l)}\right)^2\right) \left(U^{(l)}\right)^{\top} \right\| \;\approx\; \rho\!\left(U^{(l)}\right)^{T}
$$

where $\rho(U^{(l)})$ is the spectral radius. If $\rho < 1$, the product decays **exponentially** with $T$ (vanishing gradient). If $\rho > 1$, it grows **exponentially** (exploding gradient). **Stacking $L$ layers multiplies the effective chain** because the vertical pathway $W^{(l+1)}$ compounds on top of the horizontal pathway, making the gradient even more unstable for deep vs. shallow RNNs.

**Three Standard Remedies (4 Marks — 1 mark each for remedy + 1-line justification, scored holistically):**

1. **Use LSTM or GRU cells:** Their **gating mechanisms** (input, forget, output gates in LSTM; update and reset gates in GRU) create *additive* gradient pathways (the cell-state highway) that mitigate vanishing gradients.
2. **Gradient Clipping:** During backprop, **rescale** the gradient vector if its norm exceeds a threshold $\tau$: $g \leftarrow g \cdot \tau / \Vert g \Vert$ if $\Vert g \Vert > \tau$. This directly prevents explosion.
3. **Orthogonal Initialization of $U^{(l)}$:** Initialize $U^{(l)}$ to a random orthogonal matrix, ensuring $\rho(U^{(l)}) = 1$ at initialization. This keeps the gradient product neither vanishing nor exploding at the start of training.

*(An additional valid remedy worth mentioning: **Truncated BPTT**, which limits the number of time steps over which gradients are back-propagated, reducing the depth of the unrolled graph and limiting gradient decay.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall #2:**
> When asked about remedies, students often write "use ReLU activation" as a solution. **This is incorrect for vanilla Deep RNNs** because the recurrent dynamics require bounded activations ($\tanh$, sigmoid) to maintain a stable hidden state distribution. ReLU can be used in the *output* or *feedforward* portions, but not as a direct replacement for $\tanh$ in the recurrent transition. Examiners will deduct 1 mark for this confusion.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Deep RNNs**

- **Definition:** A Deep RNN has $L \geq 2$ recurrent hidden layers stacked vertically; the unrolled graph has *two* dimensions (depth and time).
- **Parameter Count:** Each layer $l$ has its *own* $W^{(l)}$ (vertical), $U^{(l)}$ (horizontal), and $b^{(l)}$. Output projection $V$ is shared across time at the top layer.
- **Forward Pass:** Bottom layer receives $x_t$; intermediate layers receive $h_t^{(l-1)}$; each layer combines input with its own $h_{t-1}^{(l)}$ via $\tanh$ activation.
- **Output Projection:** $\hat{y}_t = \text{softmax}(V h_t^{(L)} + c)$; loss is summed cross-entropy over all $T$ time steps.
- **BPTT Gradient Flow:** Three pathways converge at an intermediate hidden state: (1) **vertical** from the layer above, (2) **horizontal** from the next time step, (3) **top-down** from the output (for $l = L$).
- **Critical Gradient Equation:** $\delta_t^{(l)} = (1 - (h_t^{(l)})^2) \odot [W^{(l+1)\top} \delta_t^{(l+1)} + U^{(l)\top} \delta_{t+1}^{(l)}]$.
- **Vanishing / Exploding Gradient:** Caused by $\rho(U^{(l)}) \neq 1$; depth in RNNs **worsens** this pathology.
- **Remedies:** LSTM/GRU cells, gradient clipping, orthogonal initialization of $U^{(l)}$, truncated BPTT.
- **Comparison to Remember:** Deep RNN ≠ Bidirectional RNN. Deep RNN stacks layers; Bidirectional RNN concatenates forward and backward passes.
- **Practical Heuristic:** $L = 2$ to $4$ recurrent layers is the sweet spot; beyond that, gains diminish and optimization becomes harder.
- **Activation Choice:** Use $\tanh$ for hidden recurrences; use **gated cells** (LSTM/GRU) when $T > 50$ or when long-range dependencies matter.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modern RNN - LSTM and GRU
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET753 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Modern RNN - LSTM and GRU

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Problem with Vanilla RNNs

Before diving into Modern RNNs, we must understand **why** they were invented. A standard Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) suffers from the **vanishing and exploding gradient problem** during Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT). As the sequence length grows, gradients shrink (or blow up) exponentially, making the network unable to learn **long-range temporal dependencies**.

To solve this, two landmark architectures were introduced:
- **LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory)** — proposed by *Hochreiter & Schmidhuber (1997)*
- **GRU (Gated Recurrent Unit)** — proposed by *Cho et al. (2014)*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Modern RNN):**
> Modern RNNs are recurrent architectures that employ **gating mechanisms** (additive or multiplicative gates controlled by sigmoid activations) to regulate the flow of information, thereby preserving long-term dependencies and mitigating the vanishing gradient problem inherent in vanilla RNNs.

## 1.2 The Cell-State Memory Analogy 🧠

Imagine your brain reading a long novel. You cannot remember every word verbatim, but you keep a **running summary** of important plot points, while actively **forgetting** irrelevant details (e.g., the color of a cup a character drank from three chapters ago).

- **The "running summary"** is the **cell state** $C_t$ in an LSTM.
- **The "forgetting" mechanism** is the **forget gate**.
- **The "deciding what to write next"** mechanism is the **input gate**.
- **The "deciding what to read out loud"** mechanism is the **output gate**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Both LSTM and GRU use **gates** — small neural networks that output values between **0** (block everything) and **1** (let everything pass) — to control information flow. This selective memory is what makes them "modern."

## 1.3 High-Level Comparison

| Feature | LSTM | GRU |
|---|---|---|
| **Gates** | 3 (forget, input, output) | 2 (update, reset) |
| **Memory States** | 2 (cell state + hidden state) | 1 (hidden state only) |
| **Parameters** | **4 × (input + hidden)** weights | **3 × (input + hidden)** weights |
| **Training Speed** | Slower (more parameters) | Faster |
| **Performance on Long Sequences** | Excellent | Comparable, sometimes better |
| **Year Proposed** | **1997** | **2014** |

> [!TIP]
> GRU is often considered a **simplified, more computationally efficient variant** of LSTM. In KTU exam answers, always state: *"GRU merges the cell state and hidden state of LSTM into a single hidden state vector."*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 LSTM Architecture — Component Breakdown

The LSTM cell is the foundational unit that replaces the simple hidden state update of a vanilla RNN. It maintains two vectors that travel through time:
- **Cell State** $C_t$ — the long-term memory highway
- **Hidden State** $h_t$ — the working/short-term memory

### 2.1.1 The Three Gates of LSTM

**1. Forget Gate ($f_t$)** — *What should we throw away from memory?*
- Input: previous hidden state $h_{t-1}$ and current input $x_t$
- Output: a vector of values in $[0, 1]$ applied element-wise to $C_{t-1}$

**2. Input Gate ($i_t$)** — *What new information should we store?*
- Two parts: a sigmoid **gate** $i_t$ (decides *which values* to update) and a **tanh** candidate vector $\tilde{C}_t$ (creates *new candidate values*).

**3. Output Gate ($o_t$)** — *What should we output from the cell?*
- Decides which parts of the cell state $C_t$ to expose as the next hidden state $h_t$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why tanh for cell state?** Tanh squashes values to $[-1, 1]$, providing zero-centered outputs that help gradient flow. Sigmoid is used for gates because its output range $[0, 1]$ acts as a natural "knob" for letting information through.

## 2.2 GRU Architecture — Component Breakdown

GRU simplifies LSTM by merging the cell state and hidden state, and combining the forget + input gates into a single **update gate**.

### 2.2.1 The Two Gates of GRU

**1. Update Gate ($z_t$)** — *How much of the past should be kept?*
- Behaves like LSTM's forget + input gates combined.
- When $z_t \approx 0$, the past is preserved; when $z_t \approx 1$, the new candidate replaces the past.

**2. Reset Gate ($r_t$)** — *How much of the past should be forgotten for computing the new candidate?*
- When $r_t \approx 0$, the previous hidden state is ignored, effectively resetting memory.
- When $r_t \approx 1$, the previous state is fully used.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation:** $W_f, W_i, W_C, W_o, b_f, b_i, b_C, b_o$ are learnable weight matrices and bias vectors. $\sigma$ denotes the **sigmoid** function, $\tanh$ denotes the **hyperbolic tangent**, and $*$ denotes **element-wise (Hadamard) product**.

| # | Component | Equation | Output Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | LSTM Forget Gate | $f_t = \sigma(W_f [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)$ | $[0, 1]$ |
| 2 | LSTM Input Gate | $i_t = \sigma(W_i [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i)$ | $[0, 1]$ |
| 3 | LSTM Candidate Cell | $\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(W_C [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C)$ | $[-1, 1]$ |
| 4 | LSTM Cell State Update | $C_t = f_t * C_{t-1} + i_t * \tilde{C}_t$ | $[-1, 1]$ (approx) |
| 5 | LSTM Output Gate | $o_t = \sigma(W_o [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o)$ | $[0, 1]$ |
| 6 | LSTM Hidden State | $h_t = o_t * \tanh(C_t)$ | $[-1, 1]$ |
| 7 | GRU Update Gate | $z_t = \sigma(W_z [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_z)$ | $[0, 1]$ |
| 8 | GRU Reset Gate | $r_t = \sigma(W_r [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_r)$ | $[0, 1]$ |
| 9 | GRU Candidate Hidden | $\tilde{h}_t = \tanh(W [r_t * h_{t-1}, x_t] + b)$ | $[-1, 1]$ |
| 10 | GRU Hidden State | $h_t = (1 - z_t) * h_{t-1} + z_t * \tilde{h}_t$ | $[-1, 1]$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Domain | Application | Why LSTM/GRU? |
|---|---|---|
| **Natural Language Processing** | Machine translation, text generation, sentiment analysis | Captures long-range word dependencies |
| **Speech Recognition** | Converting audio waveforms to text | Models temporal audio patterns |
| **Time-Series Forecasting** | Stock prices, weather, energy demand | Retains trend + seasonal memory |
| **Medical Signals** | ECG/EEG classification | Detects anomalies over long time windows |
| **Video Captioning** | Generating captions from video frames | Aligns visual + temporal features |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why gates solve vanishing gradients:** The cell state $C_t$ is updated by an **additive** term $i_t * \tilde{C}_t$, not a multiplicative one. This creates a **constant error carousel** — gradients can flow across many time steps with minimal decay because the path $C_{t-1} \rightarrow C_t$ is additive, not nested inside a tanh repeatedly.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: LSTM Forward Pass — Complete Step-by-Step

**Setup:** Given a sequence of inputs $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_T$, with initial hidden state $h_0 = 0$ and initial cell state $C_0 = 0$. The LSTM processes one time step at a time.

### Step 1 — Concatenate Previous Hidden State with Current Input

$$
h_{t-1} \text{ (dim: } n_h\text{)} \quad \text{and} \quad x_t \text{ (dim: } n_x\text{)}
$$

are concatenated into a single vector of dimension $n_h + n_x$:

$$
z_t = \begin{bmatrix} h_{t-1} \\ x_t \end{bmatrix}
$$

*Why?* This combined vector contains **all the information** available at time $t$ — both the previous memory and the new observation. Weight matrices will project this combined signal into gate activations.

### Step 2 — Compute the Three Gates in Parallel

All three gates receive the same input $z_t$ but use different weight matrices:

$$
f_t = \sigma(W_f \cdot z_t + b_f)
$$

$$
i_t = \sigma(W_i \cdot z_t + b_i)
$$

$$
o_t = \sigma(W_o \cdot z_t + b_o)
$$

The sigmoid $\sigma(x) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-x}}$ ensures outputs lie in $(0, 1)$, so they can act as **soft switches**.

### Step 3 — Generate the Candidate Cell State $\tilde{C}_t$

$$
\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(W_C \cdot z_t + b_C)
$$

*Why tanh?* It produces zero-centered values in $[-1, 1]$, which represent **candidate new memory** (positive = "remember this strongly", negative = "remember the opposite").

### Step 4 — Update the Cell State (Core Innovation)

$$
C_t = f_t * C_{t-1} + i_t * \tilde{C}_t
$$

**Explanation of the two terms:**
- $f_t * C_{t-1}$: The forget gate **selectively erases** parts of the old memory. If $f_t \approx 0$ for some dimension, that dimension of $C_{t-1}$ is forgotten.
- $i_t * \tilde{C}_t$: The input gate **selectively writes** new candidate values into memory.

*Why this solves vanishing gradients:* Unlike vanilla RNN, the gradient of $C_t$ with respect to $C_{t-1}$ is just $f_t$ (a diagonal matrix of sigmoid outputs), **not** a chain of multiplied Jacobians. The gradient can thus propagate across hundreds of time steps without exponential decay.

### Step 5 — Compute the New Hidden State

$$
h_t = o_t * \tanh(C_t)
$$

The output gate $o_t$ decides **which parts of the long-term memory** to expose to the next time step and the output layer. The $\tanh(C_t)$ squashes the cell state back into $[-1, 1]$.

### Step 6 — Output (Task-Specific)

For classification, a final dense layer with softmax is applied:

$$
y_t = \text{softmax}(V h_t + c)
$$

where $V$ and $c$ are output projection parameters.

## 3.2 Derivation: GRU Forward Pass — Complete Step-by-Step

### Step 1 — Compute Update and Reset Gates

$$
z_t = \sigma(W_z \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_z)
$$

$$
r_t = \sigma(W_r \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_r)
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Candidate Hidden State with Reset Applied

$$
\tilde{h}_t = \tanh(W \cdot [r_t * h_{t-1}, x_t] + b)
$$

*Explanation:* The reset gate $r_t$ is applied **element-wise** to $h_{t-1}$ **before** it is concatenated with $x_t$. If $r_t \approx 0$, the previous hidden state is effectively zeroed out, forcing the candidate to depend **only** on the current input.

### Step 3 — Linear Interpolation Between Past and Present

$$
h_t = (1 - z_t) * h_{t-1} + z_t * \tilde{h}_t
$$

*Why this is elegant:* This is a **convex combination** because $(1 - z_t)$ and $z_t$ are both in $[0, 1]$ and sum to 1 element-wise. The update gate $z_t$ acts as a slider: 
- $z_t = 0 \Rightarrow h_t = h_{t-1}$ (copy past verbatim)
- $z_t = 1 \Rightarrow h_t = \tilde{h}_t$ (use new candidate only)

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Parameter Count Comparison

Assume input dimension $n_x$ and hidden dimension $n_h$.

| Architecture | Weight Matrices | Parameter Count (excluding biases) |
|---|---|---|
| Vanilla RNN | $W_{xh}, W_{hh}$ | $n_h \times (n_x + n_h)$ |
| **LSTM** | $W_f, W_i, W_C, W_o$ | $4 \times n_h \times (n_x + n_h)$ |
| **GRU** | $W_z, W_r, W$ | $3 \times n_h \times (n_x + n_h)$ |

**Example:** If $n_x = 128$ and $n_h = 256$:
- Vanilla RNN: $256 \times 384 = 98{,}304$ params
- LSTM: $4 \times 98{,}304 = 393{,}216$ params
- GRU: $3 \times 98{,}304 = 294{,}912$ params

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** GRU uses **25% fewer parameters** than LSTM for the same hidden size, making it preferable when compute/memory is constrained (e.g., edge devices, mobile NLP).

## 3.4 Why LSTM/GRU Solve Vanishing Gradients — Mathematical Intuition

In a vanilla RNN, the hidden state update is:
$$
h_t = \tanh(W_{hh} h_{t-1} + W_{xh} x_t)
$$

The gradient of loss $L$ with respect to $h_k$ (for $k < t$) involves a product of Jacobians:

$$
\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_k} = \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial h_i}{\partial h_{i-1}} = \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \text{diag}(1 - h_i^2) \cdot W_{hh}
$$

After $T - k$ time steps, the largest singular value of $W_{hh}$, denoted $\rho = \sigma_{\max}(W_{hh})$, is raised to the power $(T - k)$:
$$
\left\| \frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_k} \right\| \leq \rho^{(T - k)}
$$

If $\rho < 1$ → **vanishing gradient**; if $\rho > 1$ → **exploding gradient**.

In LSTM, the analogous gradient flows through the cell state:

$$
\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-1}} = \text{diag}(f_t) + \text{(smaller correction terms)}
$$

Since $f_t$ is a vector of sigmoid outputs in $(0, 1)$, the diagonal matrix $\text{diag}(f_t)$ can have eigenvalues close to 1, allowing gradients to **persist across long sequences** without decay.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — LSTM Cell Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    xt["Input x_t"] --> concatA
    htm1["h_{t-1}"] --> concatA
    concatA["Concatenation"] --> gateF["W_f * z + b_f"]
    concatA --> gateI["W_i * z + b_i"]
    concatA --> candC["W_C * z + b_C"]
    concatA --> gateO["W_o * z + b_o"]
    gateF -->|"sigmoid f_t"| multF["Element-wise Multiply"]
    Ctm1["C_{t-1}"] --> multF
    multF --> sumA["+"]
    gateI -->|"sigmoid i_t"| multI["Element-wise Multiply"]
    candC -->|"tanh C~_t"| multI
    multI --> sumA
    sumA --> Ct["C_t (New Cell State)"]
    Ct --> tanhC["tanh C_t"]
    gateO -->|"sigmoid o_t"| multO["Element-wise Multiply"]
    tanhC --> multO
    multO --> ht["h_t (New Hidden State)"]
    Ct --> htm1
    ht --> htm1N["h_t feeds next step"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — GRU Cell Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    xt["Input x_t"] --> concG
    htm1["h_{t-1}"] --> concG
    concG["Concatenation"] --> gateZ["W_z * z + b_z"]
    concG --> gateR["W_r * z + b_r"]
    gateZ -->|"sigmoid z_t"| inter["Linear Interpolation"]
    gateR -->|"sigmoid r_t"| multR["Element-wise Multiply"]
    htm1 --> multR
    multR --> concC["Concatenation with x_t"]
    xt --> concC
    concC --> candH["W * z + b"]
    candH -->|"tanh h~_t"| inter
    htm1 --> inter
    inter -->|"h_t = z*h~ + 1-z*h_{t-1}"| ht["h_t (New Hidden State)"]
    ht --> htm1N["h_t feeds next step"]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — LSTM Information Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph t_minus_1 ["TIME STEP t-1"]
        A1["C_{t-1} (Long-term Memory)"] --> A2["h_{t-1} (Short-term Working Memory)"]
    end
    subgraph t ["TIME STEP t - LSTM CELL"]
        B1["x_t (New Input)"] --> B2["GATING LAYER"]
        A2 --> B2
        B2 -->|"f_t, i_t, o_t"| B3["MEMORY UPDATE LAYER"]
        A1 --> B3
        B3 --> B4["C_t (Updated Long-term Memory)"]
        B3 --> B5["h_t (Updated Working Memory)"]
    end
    subgraph t_plus_1 ["TIME STEP t+1"]
        B4 --> C1["C_t forwarded"]
        B5 --> C2["h_t forwarded"]
    end
    subgraph output ["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        B5 --> D1["Dense + Softmax"]
        D1 --> D2["y_t (Prediction)"]
    end
```

## 4.4 Comparison Flowchart — LSTM vs GRU Decision Guide

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Choose a Gated Recurrent Architecture"] --> q1{"Is long-term memory critical?"}
    q1 -->|"Yes, sequences > 1000 steps"| lstm["Use LSTM"]
    q1 -->|"Moderate, < 500 steps"| q2{"Is model size / speed critical?"}
    q2 -->|"Yes (edge/mobile)"| gru["Use GRU"]
    q2 -->|"No, prefer flexibility"| lstm2["Use LSTM"]
    start --> alt["Alternative: Stacked / Bi-directional variants"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Solid arrows represent **tensor data flow** (vectors/matrices), while the gate outputs $f_t, i_t, o_t, z_t, r_t$ act as **multiplicative controllers** on these flows. The cell state $C_t$ in LSTM is the only signal that flows through time with **purely additive updates** — this is the structural reason behind gradient stability.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
*List the three gates used in an LSTM cell. State the activation function used for each gate and explain its role in one sentence.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Gate | Activation | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Forget gate ($f_t$)** | Sigmoid | Decides what information to **discard** from the previous cell state $C_{t-1}$. |
| **Input gate ($i_t$)** | Sigmoid | Decides which **new candidate values** $\tilde{C}_t$ should be written into the cell state. |
| **Output gate ($o_t$)** | Sigmoid | Controls what parts of the cell state $C_t$ are **exposed** as the hidden state $h_t$. |

> *Award 1 mark for correct listing, 1 mark for activations, 1 mark for the one-line role of each.*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*Compare LSTM and GRU in terms of (i) number of gates, (ii) number of state vectors maintained, and (iii) parameter efficiency.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

(i) **Number of gates:** LSTM has **3 gates** (forget, input, output); GRU has **2 gates** (update, reset). *(1 mark)*

(ii) **State vectors:** LSTM maintains **2 vectors** (cell state $C_t$ and hidden state $h_t$); GRU maintains **only 1 vector** (hidden state $h_t$, which absorbs the role of the cell state). *(1 mark)*

(iii) **Parameter efficiency:** GRU has approximately **25% fewer parameters** than LSTM for the same hidden size, because it uses $3 \times n_h(n_x + n_h)$ weights versus LSTM's $4 \times n_h(n_x + n_h)$. Hence GRU is preferred when computational efficiency is critical. *(1 mark)*

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout ⚠️**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students often write the LSTM equations but **forget to specify the activation function** (sigmoid for gates, tanh for candidate/output). This costs **1–2 marks** per equation block.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing the **candidate cell state** $\tilde{C}_t$ with the **cell state** $C_t$. Remember: $\tilde{C}_t$ is the *proposed* new memory, while $C_t$ is the *final* memory after gating.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Failing to explain *why* LSTM solves the vanishing gradient problem. The key reason is the **additive update** of the cell state $C_t = f_t * C_{t-1} + i_t * \tilde{C}_t$ — the gradient flows through an identity-like path, not a chain of nested tanh derivatives.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Drawing the LSTM diagram without labeling the **gates** and the **two states**. KTU examiners expect a fully labeled block diagram with all 6 tensor flows.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Draw and explain the architecture of an **LSTM cell** with all its gates. Discuss the role of each gate in mitigating the vanishing gradient problem. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the **forward pass equations** of an LSTM cell step by step, clearly stating the activation functions used. Explain how the cell state update enables long-term memory retention. *(7 marks)*

#### 🔹 Model Solution for Q.A (a) — Architecture & Gates (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Block Diagram (3 marks):**

Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes. The diagram must show:
- Input $x_t$ and previous hidden state $h_{t-1}$ entering a concatenation block.
- Three parallel gate computations: $f_t$, $i_t$, $o_t$ (all sigmoid).
- A candidate cell state branch with $\tanh$ activation producing $\tilde{C}_t$.
- Cell state update $C_t = f_t * C_{t-1} + i_t * \tilde{C}_t$.
- Final hidden state $h_t = o_t * \tanh(C_t)$.

**Step 2 — Role of Each Gate (3 marks):**

- **Forget gate $f_t$:** Acts as a "memory eraser." By outputting values close to 0, it allows the cell to **selectively discard** irrelevant past information. This is the key to long-term memory — old, unimportant context can be removed to make room for new, important information.
- **Input gate $i_t$:** Acts as a "memory writer." Combined with the candidate $\tilde{C}_t$, it decides **what new information** to add to the cell state. This is what allows the network to **update** its long-term memory with relevant new facts.
- **Output gate $o_t$:** Acts as a "memory reader." It controls which dimensions of the cell state are **exposed** to the rest of the network via the hidden state $h_t$.

**Step 3 — Mitigation of Vanishing Gradients (1 mark):**

The cell state update is **additive**, not purely multiplicative. During BPTT, the gradient flowing back through the cell state is modulated by the forget gate $f_t$ only, without repeated multiplication by Jacobian matrices. This creates a "highway" for gradient flow, enabling learning of dependencies over hundreds of time steps.

#### 🔹 Model Solution for Q.A (b) — Forward Pass Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Concatenation (1 mark):**

$$
z_t = \begin{bmatrix} h_{t-1} \\ x_t \end{bmatrix}, \quad z_t \in \mathbb{R}^{(n_h + n_x)}
$$

**Step 2 — Gate Computations (2 marks):**

$$
f_t = \sigma(W_f z_t + b_f), \quad i_t = \sigma(W_i z_t + b_i), \quad o_t = \sigma(W_o z_t + b_o)
$$

All gates use **sigmoid** activation $\sigma$ so outputs lie in $(0, 1)$, acting as soft binary switches.

**Step 3 — Candidate Cell State (1 mark):**

$$
\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(W_C z_t + b_C)
$$

The **tanh** activation keeps candidate values in $[-1, 1]$, providing zero-centered memory.

**Step 4 — Cell State Update (2 marks):**

$$
C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t
$$

This is the **crucial additive update**. The gradient $\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-1}} = \text{diag}(f_t)$ is **bounded and stable**, preventing exponential decay.

**Step 5 — Hidden State (1 mark):**

$$
h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)
$$

The output gate $o_t$ modulates what portion of the squashed cell state is released as the working memory for the next time step and the output layer.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a **Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU)** with neat diagrams. List all forward pass equations. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare LSTM and GRU architectures in detail. Discuss a real-world NLP application where gated RNNs outperform vanilla RNNs, and justify the choice of architecture. *(7 marks)*

#### 🔹 Model Solution for Q.B (a) — GRU Architecture (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Architecture Overview (2 marks):**

Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes. The GRU has:
- A single hidden state $h_t$ that flows through time (replacing LSTM's $C_t$ + $h_t$).
- Two gates: **update gate** $z_t$ and **reset gate** $r_t$.
- A candidate hidden state $\tilde{h}_t$ computed using the reset-gated previous state.

**Step 2 — Forward Pass Equations (3 marks):**

$$
z_t = \sigma(W_z [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_z) \quad \text{(Update gate)}
$$

$$
r_t = \sigma(W_r [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_r) \quad \text{(Reset gate)}
$$

$$
\tilde{h}_t = \tanh(W [r_t * h_{t-1}, x_t] + b) \quad \text{(Candidate hidden state)}
$$

$$
h_t = (1 - z_t) * h_{t-1} + z_t * \tilde{h}_t \quad \text{(Final hidden state)}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation (2 marks):**

- **Update gate $z_t$:** Controls the interpolation between past and present. When $z_t \to 0$, the past is preserved; when $z_t \to 1$, the network overwrites the past with the new candidate.
- **Reset gate $r_t$:** When $r_t \to 0$, the previous state is forgotten for the purpose of computing the new candidate, allowing the network to "drop context" when needed (e.g., beginning of a new sentence).
- The convex combination $h_t = (1 - z_t) h_{t-1} + z_t \tilde{h}_t$ ensures that the hidden state evolves **smoothly** without abrupt jumps.

#### 🔹 Model Solution for Q.B (b) — Comparison & Application (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Detailed Comparison (3 marks):**

| Aspect | LSTM | GRU |
|---|---|---|
| Gates | Forget, Input, Output (3) | Update, Reset (2) |
| State | Cell $C_t$ + Hidden $h_t$ | Single $h_t$ |
| Update rule | Additive on $C_t$ | Convex combination of $h$ |
| Parameters | $4 n_h (n_x + n_h)$ | $3 n_h (n_x + n_h)$ |
| Training cost | Higher | Lower (~25% fewer params) |
| Interpretability | More complex | Simpler |

**Step 2 — Real-World Application (2 marks):**

**Sentiment Analysis on Long Movie Reviews** (or **machine translation**).
- A review like *"The movie started slow, but the climax and the plot twist at the end made it absolutely worth watching"* requires the model to remember the **initial negative sentiment** and **override** it with the **final positive sentiment**.
- Vanilla RNN forgets the early words due to vanishing gradients, defaulting to the last few words.
- LSTM/GRU retain the long-range cue "started slow" through the cell state / hidden state.

**Step 3 — Justification (2 marks):**

For this application, **LSTM is preferred** because:
- The two-state design (cell + hidden) allows **explicit separation** between long-term sentiment context and short-term syntactic features.
- The forget gate can be trained to **erase** the early "slow" sentiment once the later "worth watching" signal is received, simulating a sentiment reversal.
- GRU would also work well, but the explicit forget mechanism of LSTM gives finer control on **when** to forget — important for handling negations and sentiment shifts.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Memorize These 10 Points:**

- ✅ **LSTM has 3 gates** (forget, input, output) and **2 states** (cell $C_t$, hidden $h_t$).
- ✅ **GRU has 2 gates** (update, reset) and **1 state** (hidden $h_t$ only).
- ✅ **Sigmoid** is used for all gates; **tanh** is used for candidate cell/hidden state generation and the cell-state-to-hidden-state projection.
- ✅ The **LSTM cell state update** is $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ — note the **additive** nature.
- ✅ The **GRU hidden state update** is $h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$ — a **convex combination**.
- ✅ **Vanishing gradient solution:** The cell state path is essentially linear (additive), so gradients can flow across many time steps without exponential decay.
- ✅ **GRU = ~75% of LSTM parameters** — choose GRU when speed/memory is critical, LSTM when explicit long-term control is needed.
- ✅ All weight matrices have dimensions: $W \in \mathbb{R}^{n_h \times (n_h + n_x)}$ and $b \in \mathbb{R}^{n_h}$.
- ✅ In KTU answers, always **draw a labeled block diagram** — marks are awarded for the diagram, not just equations.
- ✅ In KTU answers, always **state the activation function** when writing a gate equation — silent omissions cost 1 mark each.

> 🎯 **Pro Tip for KTU:** When asked to "compare," always use a **table** + a **1-line summary** stating which is preferred under what conditions. Examiners reward structured answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications - Natural Language Processing (NLP)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET753 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Applications - Natural Language Processing (NLP)**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Natural Language Processing (NLP)** is a sub-field of Artificial Intelligence and Deep Learning concerned with the interaction between computers and human (natural) languages. It involves designing algorithms, neural network architectures, and statistical models that enable machines to **understand, interpret, generate, and respond to** text and speech in a manner that is both syntactically valid and semantically meaningful.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, NLP is treated as a *domain-application* of Deep Learning where sequential models (RNN, LSTM, GRU) and attention-based architectures (Transformers, BERT, GPT) are deployed to solve language-centric tasks such as Machine Translation, Sentiment Analysis, Named Entity Recognition (NER), Question Answering (QA), and Text Generation.

### Core Sub-Tasks of NLP

| Sub-Task | KTU Description | Example |
|----------|-----------------|---------|
| **Tokenization** | Splitting raw text into atomic units (words/sub-words). | `"Deep Learning"` → `["Deep", "Learning"]` |
| **Embedding** | Mapping discrete tokens to dense, continuous real-valued vectors. | Word → 300-D vector in $\mathbb{R}^{300}$ |
| **Sequence Modeling** | Capturing temporal/contextual dependencies in tokens. | Predicting the next word in a sentence |
| **Attention** | Dynamically weighting the relevance of input tokens. | Translation alignment (English → Malayalam) |
| **Generation** | Autoregressive or non-autoregressive token synthesis. | ChatGPT generating an essay |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Polyglot Librarian"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a librarian who has memorized every book in a vast library but only understands the *gist* of each book. When you ask the librarian a question, the librarian doesn't literally re-read every page — instead, the librarian has built a **mental map** (an embedding) where books on similar topics sit near each other on imaginary shelves. The librarian can then rapidly walk through this map (recurrent/attention pathways) to retrieve contextually relevant passages and synthesize an answer in your language.
>
> - The **library** = the training corpus.
> - The **mental map** = the **embedding space** $\mathbb{R}^{d}$.
> - The **walking pathway** = the **recurrent/attention mechanism**.
> - The **answer** = the **generated output sequence**.

This is exactly what a Deep Learning NLP model does: it embeds tokens into a learned geometric space, navigates that space via learned attention/reccurrence weights, and emits output tokens one at a time.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

- **Vocabulary Size $V$** — total number of unique tokens (typically $30\text{K} \leq V \leq 250\text{K}$ for BPE tokenizers).
- **Embedding Dimension $d$** — dimensionality of token vectors (commonly **$d = 300$** for Word2Vec/GloVe, **$d = 768$** for BERT-base, **$d = 12288$** for GPT-3).
- **Context Window $T$** — number of preceding tokens considered (typically **$T \in \{32, 128, 512, 2048\}$**).
- **Perplexity (PPL)** — exponential of the cross-entropy loss; lower is better.
- **BLEU Score** — bounded in $[0, 1]$ (sometimes scaled to $[0, 100]$); higher is better.
- **F1-Score** — harmonic mean of precision and recall, bounded in $[0, 1]$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** In the KTU ESE, you are expected to know at least *one* end-to-end NLP pipeline (tokenization → embedding → encoder → decoder → output) and *one* application (e.g., Machine Translation or Sentiment Analysis) thoroughly. Rote-memorization of architectures scores poorly; understanding the *flow of information* scores highly.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Canonical NLP Deep Learning Pipeline

A modern NLP system built on Deep Learning follows a structured six-stage pipeline:

1. **Text Acquisition & Pre-processing** — raw text is gathered, normalized (lowercased, punctuation stripped, Unicode-normalized).
2. **Tokenization** — text is segmented into sub-word units using algorithms such as **Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE)**, **WordPiece**, or **SentencePiece**.
3. **Embedding** — each token is mapped to a dense vector $\mathbf{e}_i \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$.
4. **Contextual Encoding** — sequential/attention models transform embeddings into context-aware representations $\mathbf{h}_i$.
5. **Task-Specific Head** — a classification/regression/generation head is appended (e.g., softmax over $V$ tokens).
6. **Decoding / Inference** — outputs are converted back to natural language (greedy, beam search, or top-$k$ sampling).

## 2.2 Theoretical Foundations — "Why" and "How"

### 2.2.1 Why Embeddings?
A one-hot vector of vocabulary size $V$ lives in $\mathbb{R}^{V}$ and is **orthogonal** to every other token. This makes it impossible to express semantic similarity. An embedding matrix $E \in \mathbb{R}^{V \times d}$ projects one-hot vectors into a **dense, low-dimensional, semantically meaningful** space where similar words cluster.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distributional Hypothesis (Harris, 1954):** "Words that occur in similar contexts tend to have similar meanings." This is the foundational *why* behind all embedding models.

### 2.2.2 Why Recurrence / Attention?
Natural language is inherently **sequential** (word order matters) and exhibits **long-range dependencies** (a pronoun can refer to a noun hundreds of words earlier). Vanilla feed-forward networks cannot model variable-length sequences. Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) and attention mechanisms address this by maintaining a **hidden state** that summarizes past context.

### 2.2.3 Why Transformers Dominated RNNs?
Transformers use **self-attention** to process all tokens in parallel, avoiding the sequential bottleneck of RNNs. This enables:
- Faster training on GPUs/TPUs.
- Better modeling of long-range dependencies.
- Scalability to billions of parameters (GPT, LLaMA, PaLM).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **must-memorize** mathematical core for the KTU ESE on NLP applications. Render any absolute values using `\vert` — never the vertical pipe character — to preserve markdown table syntax.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | **One-Hot Encoding** | $\mathbf{x}_i = [0,\dots,0,1,0,\dots,0]^{\top} \in \mathbb{R}^{V}$ | Sparse indicator of token $i$. |
| 2 | **Embedding Lookup** | $\mathbf{e}_i = E \cdot \mathbf{x}_i$ | $E \in \mathbb{R}^{V \times d}$ is embedding matrix. |
| 3 | **Word2Vec Softmax (CBOW/Skip-gram)** | $P(w_O \mid w_I) = \dfrac{\exp(\mathbf{v}_{w_O}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I})}{\sum_{w=1}^{V} \exp(\mathbf{v}_{w}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I})}$ | Probability of output word given input/context. |
| 4 | **Negative Sampling Loss** | $\mathcal{L} = -\log \sigma(\mathbf{v}_{w_O}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I}) - \sum_{k=1}^{K} \log \sigma(-\mathbf{v}_{\tilde{w}_k}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I})$ | Approximates full softmax; $K$ negative samples. |
| 5 | **RNN Hidden Update** | $\mathbf{h}_t = \tanh(W_{hh}\mathbf{h}_{t-1} + W_{xh}\mathbf{x}_t + \mathbf{b}_h)$ | Sequential state propagation. |
| 6 | **LSTM Forget Gate** | $\mathbf{f}_t = \sigma(W_f \cdot [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_f)$ | Values in $(0,1)$; controls memory erasure. |
| 7 | **Scaled Dot-Product Attention** | $\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$ | $Q, K, V$ are query, key, value matrices. |
| 8 | **Multi-Head Attention** | $\text{MHA}(Q,K,V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1,\dots,\text{head}_h) W^O$ | $\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(QW_i^Q, KW_i^K, VW_i^V)$ |
| 9 | **Positional Encoding** | $PE_{(pos, 2i)} = \sin(pos / 10000^{2i/d})$, $PE_{(pos, 2i+1)} = \cos(\cdot)$ | Injects order into token embeddings. |
| 10 | **Cross-Entropy Loss** | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{t=1}^{T} y_t \log \hat{y}_t$ | Standard sequence prediction objective. |
| 11 | **Perplexity** | $\text{PPL} = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T} \log P(w_t \mid w_{<t})\right)$ | Exponentiated average negative log-likelihood. |
| 12 | **BLEU Score (n-gram precision)** | $\text{BLEU} = BP \cdot \exp\!\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)$ | $BP$ = brevity penalty; $w_n = 1/N$. |
| 13 | **Cosine Similarity (Embeddings)** | $\text{sim}(\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}) = \dfrac{\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v}}{\vert \mathbf{u} \vert \vert \mathbf{v} \vert}$ | Measures semantic closeness. |
| 14 | **Transformer FFN Block** | $\text{FFN}(x) = \max(0, xW_1 + b_1) W_2 + b_2$ | Position-wise two-layer MLP with ReLU. |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Search Engines** (Google, Bing): BERT-based re-ranking of search results.
- **Virtual Assistants** (Alexa, Siri, Google Assistant): ASR → NLU → Dialogue → TTS pipelines built on Transformers.
- **Healthcare**: Clinical NLP for electronic health record (EHR) mining, ICD-code auto-assignment.
- **Legal/Finance**: Contract clause extraction, sentiment scoring of earnings calls.
- **Code Intelligence**: GitHub Copilot uses GPT-style decoder-only Transformers trained on code+text.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section contains **exhaustive, step-by-step derivations** of three KTU-relevant formulas: (i) the Word2Vec Skip-gram objective with negative sampling, (ii) the Scaled Dot-Product Attention output, and (iii) the BLEU score for a 2-gram example.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Word2Vec Skip-gram with Negative Sampling

**Goal:** Maximize the log-probability of observing context words given a center word, while efficiently approximating the costly full softmax.

### Step 1 — Define the Skip-gram Objective

Given a center word $w_I$ and a window of context words $w_{O,1}, w_{O,2}, \dots, w_{O,C}$, the full softmax objective is:

$$
J(\theta) = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{-c \le j \le c,\, j \neq 0} \log P(w_{t+j} \mid w_t;\, \theta)
$$

### Step 2 — Express the Conditional Probability

Using the softmax over the entire vocabulary $V$:

$$
P(w_O \mid w_I) = \frac{\exp\!\left(\mathbf{v}_{w_O}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I}\right)}{\sum_{w=1}^{V} \exp\!\left(\mathbf{v}_{w}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I}\right)}
$$

Here $\mathbf{v}_w \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ is the vector representation of word $w$. The denominator sums over **all $V$** words, which is computationally prohibitive for large $V$ (e.g., $V = 10^5$).

### Step 3 — Reformulate as a Binary Classification Problem

Negative sampling (Mikolov et al., 2013) replaces the softmax with $K$ negative samples $\tilde{w}_k$ drawn from a noise distribution $P_n(w)$. The new objective per (center, context) pair becomes:

$$
J_{\text{NS}}(\theta) = \log \sigma\!\left(\mathbf{v}_{w_O}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I}\right) + \sum_{k=1}^{K} \mathbb{E}_{\tilde{w}_k \sim P_n}\!\left[\log \sigma\!\left(-\mathbf{v}_{\tilde{w}_k}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I}\right)\right]
$$

where $\sigma(x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-x}}$ is the sigmoid function.

### Step 4 — Take the Negative Log-Loss (Minimization Form)

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{NS}} = -\log \sigma\!\left(\mathbf{v}_{w_O}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I}\right) - \sum_{k=1}^{K} \log \sigma\!\left(-\mathbf{v}_{\tilde{w}_k}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I}\right)
$$

### Step 5 — Gradient Update (Stochastic Gradient Descent)

The partial derivative with respect to the output (positive) word vector $\mathbf{v}_{w_O}$ is:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{\text{NS}}}{\partial \mathbf{v}_{w_O}} = \left(\sigma\!\left(\mathbf{v}_{w_O}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I}\right) - 1\right) \mathbf{v}_{w_I}
$$

The partial derivative with respect to the input (center) word vector $\mathbf{v}_{w_I}$ is:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{\text{NS}}}{\partial \mathbf{v}_{w_I}} = \left(\sigma\!\left(\mathbf{v}_{w_O}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I}\right) - 1\right) \mathbf{v}_{w_O} + \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sigma\!\left(\mathbf{v}_{\tilde{w}_k}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I}\right) \mathbf{v}_{\tilde{w}_k}
$$

### Step 6 — Update Rule

$$
\mathbf{v}_{w_I}^{\text{new}} = \mathbf{v}_{w_I}^{\text{old}} - \eta \cdot \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{\text{NS}}}{\partial \mathbf{v}_{w_I}}
$$

where $\eta$ is the learning rate (typically $\eta = 0.025$ decaying linearly).

**Intuition:** If the model assigns high probability to the true context word and low probability to the negatives, the gradients vanish and the embeddings stabilize. This causes semantically similar words (e.g., *"king"* and *"queen"*) to acquire similar vector directions.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Scaled Dot-Product Attention Output

**Goal:** Show step-by-step how the attention mechanism produces a weighted sum of value vectors.

### Step 1 — Define Inputs

We have $n$ query vectors, each of dimension $d_k$, packed into a matrix $Q \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k}$. Similarly, $K \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times d_k}$ and $V \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times d_v}$.

### Step 2 — Compute Compatibility Scores

Take the dot product of every query with every key to get the score matrix $S \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$:

$$
S = Q K^{\top}
$$

Each entry $S_{ij}$ measures how much query $i$ "attends to" key $j$.

### Step 3 — Scale to Stabilize Gradients

For large $d_k$, the dot products grow in magnitude, pushing the softmax into regions of extremely small gradients. We divide by $\sqrt{d_k}$:

$$
S_{\text{scaled}} = \frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert Scores to Probabilities (Row-wise Softmax)

$$
A = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)
$$

Each row $A_i$ is a probability distribution over the $m$ keys (sums to 1).

### Step 5 — Weighted Sum of Values

$$
\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = A \cdot V
$$

For token $i$:

$$
\text{output}_i = \sum_{j=1}^{m} A_{ij} \cdot V_j
$$

### Step 6 — Full Closed-Form

$$
\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V
$$

**Intuition:** Each output token is a *context-aware* blend of all input value vectors, where the blending weights come from query–key similarity. This is the mathematical engine of BERT, GPT, and the entire Transformer family.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: BLEU Score Calculation (BiLingual Evaluation Understudy)

**Setup:** A machine translation system produces the candidate sentence:

> *Candidate:* `["the", "cat", "sat", "on", "the", "mat"]`

The reference (human) sentence is:

> *Reference:* `["the", "cat", "is", "on", "the", "mat"]`

We compute **BLEU-2** (bigram BLEU).

### Step 1 — Extract Bigrams

- Candidate bigrams: `(the,cat)`, `(cat,sat)`, `(sat,on)`, `(on,the)`, `(the,mat)` → **5 bigrams**.
- Reference bigrams: `(the,cat)`, `(cat,is)`, `(is,on)`, `(on,the)`, `(the,mat)` → **5 bigrams**.

### Step 2 — Compute Clipped Precision

For each candidate bigram, count its maximum occurrences in any reference, then clip.

| Candidate Bigram | Count in Cand. | Max in Ref. | Clipped Count |
|------------------|----------------|-------------|----------------|
| (the, cat) | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| (cat, sat) | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| (sat, on) | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| (on, the) | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| (the, mat) | 1 | 1 | 1 |

Sum of clipped counts = $1 + 0 + 0 + 1 + 1 = 3$.

### Step 3 — Precision

$$
p_2 = \frac{\text{Clipped Count}}{\text{Total Candidate Bigrams}} = \frac{3}{5} = 0.6
$$

### Step 4 — Brevity Penalty

Candidate length $c = 6$, closest reference length $r = 6$.

$$
BP = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } c > r \\ e^{1 - r/c} & \text{if } c \le r \end{cases}
$$

Since $c = r$, $BP = 1$.

### Step 5 — Final BLEU-2 Score

Assuming unigram precision $p_1 = \frac{5}{6} \approx 0.833$ and $N = 2$ with $w_1 = w_2 = 0.5$:

$$
\text{BLEU} = BP \cdot \exp\!\left(0.5 \log p_1 + 0.5 \log p_2\right) = 1 \cdot \sqrt{0.833 \times 0.6} \approx \sqrt{0.500} \approx 0.707
$$

**Interpretation:** A BLEU score of $\approx 0.71$ indicates reasonably good translation quality (BLEU $> 0.5$ is generally considered a strong translation).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Architecture | Parallelism | Long-Range Deps. | Training Cost | Best Use-Case |
|--------------|-------------|------------------|---------------|---------------|
| **Vanilla RNN** | None | Poor (vanishing gradient) | Low | Toy sequences |
| **LSTM** | None | Moderate | High | Speech, classical NER |
| **GRU** | None | Moderate | Medium | Lightweight seq2seq |
| **Seq2Seq + Attention** | Encoder only | Good | High | Machine Translation (pre-2017) |
| **Transformer (Encoder)** | Full | Excellent | Very High | BERT-style understanding |
| **Transformer (Decoder)** | Full (causal mask) | Excellent | Very High | GPT-style generation |
| **Transformer (Enc-Dec)** | Full | Excellent | Very High | T5, BART, NMT |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define **word embedding**. Why is a learned dense representation preferred over a one-hot encoding for downstream NLP tasks?
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A *word embedding* is a dense, low-dimensional, learned vector representation $\mathbf{e}_w \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ (typically $d \in [50, 1024]$) of a word $w$, such that semantically similar words occupy nearby points in the embedding space.
>
> **Why preferred over one-hot:**
> 1. **Dimensionality:** One-hot vectors live in $\mathbb{R}^{V}$ with $V$ often $\geq 10^5$; embeddings live in $\mathbb{R}^{d}$ with $d \ll V$, drastically reducing parameter count and computation.
> 2. **Semantic similarity:** One-hot vectors are mutually orthogonal (cosine similarity $= 0$ for all distinct pairs), so semantic relationships cannot be encoded. Embeddings encode the *distributional hypothesis* — words with similar contexts have similar vectors (cosine similarity $\neq 0$).
> 3. **Generalization:** Embeddings allow the model to share statistical strength across similar words (e.g., *"king"* and *"queen"*), whereas one-hot treats every word as an isolated category.

> **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Differentiate between **RNN** and **Transformer** architectures in terms of **sequential processing** and **long-range dependency modeling**.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Aspect | RNN | Transformer |
> |--------|-----|-------------|
> | **Processing** | Strictly sequential; token $t$ depends on hidden state from token $t-1$. | Fully parallel; all tokens processed simultaneously via self-attention. |
> | **Long-Range Dependencies** | Suffers from vanishing/exploding gradients; information decays over distance. | Direct attention between any two positions — distance is $O(1)$ in path length. |
> | **Training Speed** | Slow due to sequential bottleneck. | Fast on GPUs/TPUs due to parallelism. |
> | **Positional Info** | Implicit (via recurrence). | Explicit (via positional encodings). |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Note:** KTU's End Semester Examination (ESE) Module 4 typically offers an internal choice. Below are two fully independent questions, each broken into 7 + 7 sub-parts with **complete model solutions**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Machine Translation with Seq2Seq + Attention

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Modified]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the **Encoder–Decoder architecture** for machine translation. How does the **attention mechanism** overcome the bottleneck of fixed-length context vectors in vanilla Seq2Seq? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Given the source sentence *"I love deep learning"* (tokenized as `["I", "love", "deep", "learning"]`), the decoder is producing the target word at step $t=3$. Suppose the attention scores (pre-softmax) between the current decoder state and the four encoder states are $\mathbf{s} = [2.0,\ 3.0,\ 4.0,\ 1.0]$.
> Compute:
> (i) The **softmax attention weights** $\alpha_{t,i}$.
> (ii) The **context vector** $\mathbf{c}_t$ given encoder outputs $\mathbf{h}_1=[1,0]^{\top}$, $\mathbf{h}_2=[0,1]^{\top}$, $\mathbf{h}_3=[1,1]^{\top}$, $\mathbf{h}_4=[0,0]^{\top}$.
> (iii) Comment on which source word receives the **maximum attention** and why this is linguistically reasonable. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The **Encoder–Decoder (Seq2Seq)** architecture, introduced by Sutskever et al. (2014) and refined by Bahdanau et al. (2015), consists of two recurrent networks:

- **Encoder RNN** reads the source sequence $\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2, \dots, \mathbf{x}_T$ and compresses the entire sentence into a fixed-length context vector $\mathbf{c} = \mathbf{h}_T$ (the final hidden state).
- **Decoder RNN** generates the target sequence $\mathbf{y}_1, \mathbf{y}_2, \dots, \mathbf{y}_{T'}$ one token at a time, conditioned on $\mathbf{c}$.

**The Bottleneck Problem:** All information about the source must pass through a single fixed-size vector $\mathbf{c}$. For long sentences, this is severely lossy. Empirical results show translation quality degrades sharply for sentences longer than $\sim 20$ tokens.

**Attention as the Cure:** Bahdanau et al.'s **additive (soft) attention** replaces the single context vector with a *dynamic*, *position-specific* context vector $\mathbf{c}_t$ computed at every decoder step $t$:

$$
\mathbf{c}_t = \sum_{i=1}^{T} \alpha_{t,i} \cdot \mathbf{h}_i^{\text{enc}}
$$

where the attention weights are:

$$
\alpha_{t,i} = \frac{\exp(e_{t,i})}{\sum_{j=1}^{T} \exp(e_{t,j})}, \quad e_{t,i} = \mathbf{v}_a^{\top} \tanh(W_a \mathbf{s}_{t-1} + U_a \mathbf{h}_i^{\text{enc}})
$$

This allows the decoder to "look back" at *all* encoder states with *learned, content-dependent* weights. The model effectively learns a soft alignment between source and target words.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Softmax Attention Weights:**

Compute $\exp(s_i)$ for each score:
- $\exp(2.0) \approx 7.389$
- $\exp(3.0) \approx 20.086$
- $\exp(4.0) \approx 54.598$
- $\exp(1.0) \approx 2.718$

Sum $= 7.389 + 20.086 + 54.598 + 2.718 = 84.791$.

Therefore:

$$
\alpha_{t,1} = \frac{7.389}{84.791} \approx 0.087
$$

$$
\alpha_{t,2} = \frac{20.086}{84.791} \approx 0.237
$$

$$
\alpha_{t,3} = \frac{54.598}{84.791} \approx 0.644
$$

$$
\alpha_{t,4} = \frac{2.718}{84.791} \approx 0.032
$$

**(ii) Context Vector $\mathbf{c}_t$:**

$$
\mathbf{c}_t = \sum_{i=1}^{4} \alpha_{t,i} \cdot \mathbf{h}_i = 0.087\begin{bmatrix}1\\0\end{bmatrix} + 0.237\begin{bmatrix}0\\1\end{bmatrix} + 0.644\begin{bmatrix}1\\1\end{bmatrix} + 0.032\begin{bmatrix}0\\0\end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\mathbf{c}_t = \begin{bmatrix} 0.087 + 0 + 0.644 + 0 \\ 0 + 0.237 + 0.644 + 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.731 \\ 0.881 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**(iii) Linguistic Comment:**

The source token *"deep"* (position 3) receives the **highest attention weight** ($\alpha_{t,3} \approx 0.644$). This is linguistically reasonable because at decoder step $t=3$, the model is likely producing a word semantically related to *"deep"* (e.g., the Malayalam target word for *learning/study* often aligns with the root concept *deep* in the source phrase). The attention mechanism has effectively learned a *soft word alignment* between English and the target language — a hallmark of NMT attention models.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Sentiment Analysis with Deep Learning

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Modified]**
>
> **(a)** Design a **complete deep learning pipeline** for binary sentiment classification (positive/negative) of movie reviews. Specify the role of each component: tokenizer, embedding layer (with pretrained vs. scratch trade-off), encoder (LSTM vs. BERT), and classification head. Justify each design choice. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A review *"the movie was not bad at all"* is encoded by an LSTM. Suppose the final hidden state is $\mathbf{h}_T = [0.8,\ -0.6,\ 0.3,\ 0.1]^{\top}$ and the classification weight matrix is $W = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & -0.4 & 0.3 & 0.2 \\ -0.5 & 0.4 & -0.3 & -0.2 \end{bmatrix}$ with bias $\mathbf{b} = [0.1,\ -0.1]^{\top}$.
> Compute:
> (i) The **logits** $\mathbf{z} = W \mathbf{h}_T + \mathbf{b}$.
> (ii) The **softmax probabilities** for both classes.
> (iii) The **predicted class** and the **cross-entropy loss** given the true label $y = [0, 1]$ (i.e., the true class is *negative*). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

A robust deep-learning pipeline for binary sentiment classification comprises five stages:

| Stage | Component | Design Choice & Justification |
|-------|-----------|--------------------------------|
| 1. **Text Pre-processing** | Lower-casing, HTML-stripping, optional lemmatization. | Reduces vocabulary size, improves generalization on noisy IMDB/Amazon reviews. |
| 2. **Tokenization** | WordPiece (BERT) or BPE. | Handles out-of-vocabulary words via sub-word units; e.g., *"unbelievable"* → `["un", "##believ", "##able"]`. |
| 3. **Embedding** | Pretrained (GloVe, Word2Vec, BERT) **vs.** trained from scratch. | Pretrained embeddings drastically reduce training data requirements; they encode prior linguistic knowledge. For low-data regimes, freeze the embedding layer; for high-data, fine-tune. |
| 4. **Encoder** | **LSTM** for sequential modeling, or **BERT** for contextual understanding. | LSTM is computationally cheaper and interpretable; BERT yields state-of-the-art accuracy via bidirectional self-attention. Trade-off: latency vs. accuracy. |
| 5. **Classification Head** | Linear layer → Softmax over 2 classes. | Final hidden state $\mathbf{h}_T$ (LSTM) or `[CLS]` token representation (BERT) is fed to a 2-unit softmax. |

**Justification Summary:** The pipeline is *modular* — each stage can be swapped independently. The use of pretrained embeddings is a form of **transfer learning** that is essential when labeled data is scarce (a common scenario in KTU project work).

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Logits $\mathbf{z} = W \mathbf{h}_T + \mathbf{b}$:**

$$
W \mathbf{h}_T = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & -0.4 & 0.3 & 0.2 \\ -0.5 & 0.4 & -0.3 & -0.2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.8 \\ -0.6 \\ 0.3 \\ 0.1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Row 1: $0.5(0.8) + (-0.4)(-0.6) + 0.3(0.3) + 0.2(0.1) = 0.4 + 0.24 + 0.09 + 0.02 = 0.75$

Row 2: $-0.5(0.8) + 0.4(-0.6) + (-0.3)(0.3) + (-0.2)(0.1) = -0.4 - 0.24 - 0.09 - 0.02 = -0.75$

Adding bias:

$$
\mathbf{z} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.75 + 0.1 \\ -0.75 - 0.1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.85 \\ -0.85 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**(ii) Softmax Probabilities:**

$$
P(\text{class}=0 \mid \mathbf{h}_T) = \frac{e^{0.85}}{e^{0.85} + e^{-0.85}} = \frac{2.340}{2.340 + 0.427} = \frac{2.340}{2.767} \approx 0.846
$$

$$
P(\text{class}=1 \mid \mathbf{h}_T) = \frac{e^{-0.85}}{2.767} \approx 0.154
$$

**(iii) Predicted Class and Cross-Entropy Loss:**

Since $P(\text{class}=0) = 0.846 > P(\text{class}=1) = 0.154$, the **predicted class is 0 (positive)**.

The true label is $\mathbf{y} = [0, 1]$ (negative). The cross-entropy loss is:

$$
\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{i=1}^{2} y_i \log \hat{y}_i = -(0 \cdot \log 0.846 + 1 \cdot \log 0.154) = -\log(0.154) \approx 1.871
$$

**Interpretation:** The model **misclassifies** this review (predicts positive when the true label is negative). The high loss ($\approx 1.87$) reflects the model's high confidence in the wrong answer — a phenomenon called *confident misclassification*, often caused by the sarcastic/idiomatic phrase *"not bad at all"* which is a known failure mode of vanilla LSTMs. Fine-tuning BERT would likely handle this case correctly.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling factor** in the Scaled Dot-Product Attention formula. Many students write $QK^{\top}$ without dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$. This is a **2-mark deduction** in the KTU key.
> 2. **Confusing word2vec CBOW with Skip-gram.** CBOW predicts the center word from context; Skip-gram predicts context from the center. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 3. **Writing RNNs as if they process tokens in parallel** — they are strictly sequential. Mentioning "parallelism" for an RNN is an automatic zero on architecture-comparison questions.
> 4. **Omitting residual connections and layer normalization** in Transformer diagrams. These are *mandatory* components in a correct encoder-block drawing. KTU examiners explicitly look for them.
> 5. **Mishandling the negative log-likelihood in cross-entropy** — students often forget the negative sign. Always write $\mathcal{L} = -\sum y \log \hat{y}$, not $\sum y \log \hat{y}$.
> 6. **Failing to specify tokenization strategy** — a common omission in pipeline-design questions. Always mention BPE / WordPiece / SentencePiece explicitly.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: NLP Applications**

### Foundational Concepts
- [x] **NLP definition:** Computational processing of natural language for understanding, generation, and translation.
- [x] **Tokenization strategies:** Word-level, sub-word (BPE, WordPiece, SentencePiece), character-level.
- [x] **One-hot vs. embedding:** One-hot is sparse ($V$-dim) and orthogonal; embeddings are dense ($d$-dim) and semantically meaningful.

### Architectures — Know Each One
- [x] **RNN:** Sequential, vanishing-gradient problem, hidden state $\mathbf{h}_t = \tanh(W_{hh}\mathbf{h}_{t-1} + W_{xh}\mathbf{x}_t)$.
- [x] **LSTM:** Adds forget $\mathbf{f}_t$, input $\mathbf{i}_t$, output $\mathbf{o}_t$ gates and cell state $\mathbf{C}_t$.
- [x] **GRU:** Simplified LSTM with reset $\mathbf{r}_t$ and update $\mathbf{z}_t$ gates (only 2 gates).
- [x] **Seq2Seq:** Encoder RNN → context vector $\mathbf{c}$ → Decoder RNN.
- [x] **Attention:** Dynamic context $\mathbf{c}_t = \sum_i \alpha_{t,i} \mathbf{h}_i$; scores via additive or dot-product.
- [x] **Transformer:** Multi-head self-attention + position-wise FFN; *no recurrence*; parallelizable.
- [x] **BERT:** Encoder-only, masked language modeling + next-sentence prediction, bidirectional.
- [x] **GPT:** Decoder-only, causal (autoregressive) language modeling, unidirectional.

### Must-Memorize Formulas
- [x] **Softmax attention:** $\text{softmax}(QK^{\top}/\sqrt{d_k})V$
- [x] **Cross-entropy:** $\mathcal{L} = -\sum y_t \log \hat{y}_t$
- [x] **BLEU:** $BP \cdot \exp(\sum w_n \log p_n)$
- [x] **Perplexity:** $\exp(-\frac{1}{T}\sum \log P(w_t \mid w_{<t}))$
- [x] **Positional encoding:** Sine/cosine at varying frequencies.

### Applications — Be Able to Describe
- [x] **Sentiment Analysis:** Text → Encoder → Softmax over {positive, negative}.
- [x] **Machine Translation:** Seq2Seq + Attention (Bahdanau) or full Transformer (Vaswani).
- [x] **Named Entity Recognition:** BiLSTM-CRF or BERT fine-tuning with token-level softmax.
- [x] **Question Answering:** SQuAD-style — span prediction via start/end logits.
- [x] **Text Summarization:** Extractive (TextRank) vs. Abstractive (Pointer-Generator, BART, T5).

### Evaluation Metrics
- [x] **Classification:** Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1.
- [x] **Translation:** BLEU, ROUGE, METEOR.
- [x] **Generation:** Perplexity, BERTScore, human evaluation.
- [x] **NER:** Token-level F1.

### KTU-Specific Exam Tips
- [x] Always **draw the architecture** before writing math — diagrams earn easy 2–3 marks.
- [x] **State assumptions** (e.g., "assuming $d = 300$" or "with $K = 5$ negative samples").
- [x] **Distinguish between encoder-only, decoder-only, and encoder-decoder** Transformers — this is a favorite KTU question.
- [x] Mention **transfer learning** and **fine-tuning** wherever BERT/pretrained embeddings are used.
- [x] Use **LaTeX-style notation** for all math; the KTU answer script has grid lines that don't tolerate messy equations.
